name: Home
title: Home
description: Landing page that provides an overview of the boutique hotel and routes users to room search, featured rooms, offers, restaurant, meetings & events, gift cards, and newsletter signup.

core_features: Load and display high-level hotel overview information (intro text, location summary, key counts) using getHomeOverview.

Provide a room search form that is pre-populated with suggested dates, guest counts, and sort options using getRoomSearchFilterOptions, and that starts a room search on the rooms.html page via URL parameters.
Display a small set of featured rooms using getHomeFeaturedRooms, with each room card linking directly to its detail page via room_detail.html and the roomId URL parameter.
Display a curated strip of featured offers and spa packages using getHomeFeaturedOffers, including a control to navigate to the offers.html page with featureFilters and sortBy URL parameters.
Expose quick navigation teasers to the Restaurant, Meetings & Events, and Gift Cards sections, routing users to restaurant.html, meetings_events.html, and gift_cards.html.
Show a newsletter teaser section using getNewsletterTeaserContent with a clear call-to-action that navigates to newsletter.html for full subscription management.
user_workflows:
Start a room search from Home:
1. User lands on index.html (no URL parameters expected).
2. On page initialization, the room search component calls getRoomSearchFilterOptions.
3. The search form fields for check-in, check-out, adults, children, and sortBy are pre-filled using defaultCheckInDate, defaultCheckOutDate, defaultAdults, defaultChildren, and a default selection from sortOptions.
4. The user may adjust any of these inputs.
5. The user submits the search form.
6. The page navigates to rooms.html, adding checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, and sortBy as URL query parameters derived from the current form values.

Explore featured rooms from Home:
1. On page initialization, the featured rooms component calls getHomeFeaturedRooms with a configured limit.
2. The component renders a list of featured room cards using the returned room objects and related pricing/highlight data.
3. The user selects a featured room by clicking its card.
4. The page navigates to room_detail.html with a roomId URL parameter set to the clicked room's id.

Explore featured offers from Home:
1. On page initialization, the featured offers component calls getHomeFeaturedOffers with a types array (e.g., based on Offer.type values) and a limit.
2. The component renders offer cards using the returned offer objects and their highlight metadata.
3. The user clicks an individual offer card if provided; the component can either navigate to offers.html with a featureFilters parameter focused on that offer or simply scroll/focus to offers section depending on implementation (navigation details are kept consistent with the global routing strategy).
4. Alternatively, the user clicks a "view all"-style control in this section.
5. The featured offers component builds a featureFilters value (homeFeaturedOffersFilter) based on the same types or tags used for getHomeFeaturedOffers.
6. The page navigates to offers.html with featureFilters set to homeFeaturedOffersFilter and sortBy set to guest_rating_desc via URL parameters.

Navigate to restaurant, meetings & events, and gift cards:
1. User sees navigation teaser elements for Restaurant, Meetings & Events, and Gift Cards.
2. For Restaurant and Meetings & Events teasers, the component may use getHomeOverview highlightCounts fields (e.g., restaurantName, meetingRoomsCount) for contextual text.
3. The user clicks the Restaurant teaser; the page navigates to restaurant.html with no additional URL parameters.
4. The user clicks the Meetings & Events teaser; the page navigates to meetings_events.html with no additional URL parameters.
5. The user clicks the Gift Cards teaser; the page navigates to gift_cards.html with no additional URL parameters.

Proceed to newsletter signup from Home:
1. On page initialization, the newsletter teaser component calls getNewsletterTeaserContent.
2. The component displays the teaser headline, subcopy, and image from the response.
3. The user clicks the main call-to-action button in the teaser.
4. The page navigates to newsletter.html with no additional URL parameters, where the full NewsletterSubscription flow is handled.
interactions:
Room search form interactions:
- User changes check-in or check-out date fields; the component updates its internal form state and may validate that check-out is after check-in.
- User increments or decrements adult and child guest counters; the component ensures non-negative integers and may enforce logical minimum/maximum values based on UX rules.
- User selects a sorting option from a select control populated using sortOptions from getRoomSearchFilterOptions; the selected option is stored as homeSearchForm.sortBy.
- On submit, the component prevents default form submission behavior, constructs a rooms.html URL with query parameters mapped from the internal form state, and triggers a navigation.

Featured rooms interactions:
- On initialization, the component calls getHomeFeaturedRooms with a limit parameter and stores the result as a list of featuredRoom items.
- For each returned item, the component binds room.name, room.viewType, room.bedType, room.maxOccupancyAdults/Children, images, and pricing fields to displayable elements.
- When the user clicks anywhere on a featured room card (or a defined interactive area), the component reads featuredRoom.room.id and navigates to room_detail.html with a roomId URL parameter matching this value.

Featured offers interactions:
- On initialization, the component calls getHomeFeaturedOffers with a types array and a limit; the types array is defined by the page configuration or system settings using Offer.type values and stored in component state.
- The component renders each offer card using offer.name, offer.shortTitle, offer.description, nightlyRate, currency, and imageUrls, plus ribbonLabel and highlightText.
- Optionally, the user can click an offer card; when such an interaction requires navigation to offers.html, the component may build a featureFilters value specifically for that offer or for the broader types set and navigate accordingly.
- The user can click a general "view all" control for this section; the component then uses its stored types array (or other filter settings) to compute homeFeaturedOffersFilter and navigates to offers.html with featureFilters and sortBy=guest_rating_desc URL parameters.

Overview and teaser interactions:
- On initialization, the overview component calls getHomeOverview and binds introText, locationSummary, heroImageUrl, and highlightCounts.* fields to display and to support contextual labels for teaser sections.
- The Restaurant teaser may be clickable; when clicked, the component triggers navigation to restaurant.html.
- The Meetings & Events teaser is clickable; when clicked, the component navigates to meetings_events.html.
- The Gift Cards teaser is clickable; when clicked, the component navigates to gift_cards.html.

Newsletter teaser interactions:
- On initialization, the newsletter teaser component calls getNewsletterTeaserContent and stores the returned headline, subcopy, and imageUrl.
- The user can click the primary call-to-action button within this component; this triggers navigation to newsletter.html, where the user completes subscription steps.
- If the teaser includes an image or text link, clicking those may also trigger the same navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page (index.html) does not consume any incoming URL parameters; its initial state is fully determined by the responses from getHomeOverview, getHomeFeaturedRooms, getHomeFeaturedOffers, getNewsletterTeaserContent, and getRoomSearchFilterOptions. The room search form state (homeSearchForm.*) is initialized using getRoomSearchFilterOptions.defaultCheckInDate, defaultCheckOutDate, defaultAdults, defaultChildren, and a default selection from sortOptions (for example, the first entry or one flagged as default by configuration outside this spec). Changes made by the user to dates, guests, and sortBy are stored only in component-level state on this page and are not reflected in the Home page URL. When the user submits the room search form, the component constructs a navigation to rooms.html with URL query parameters: checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, and sortBy, each mapped directly from the current homeSearchForm values. These parameters allow rooms.html to reconstruct the user's search state there. The featured offers section maintains an internal representation of the filter criteria used in the call to getHomeFeaturedOffers (for example, an array of Offer.type values or tags). This representation is encoded into a homeFeaturedOffersFilter value (string or serialized structure) when the user chooses to view all offers. Navigation to offers.html includes featureFilters set to this encoded value and sortBy fixed to guest_rating_desc via URL parameters, enabling the offers page to pre-filter its listings consistently with what was shown on Home. Navigation to room_detail.html (from featured rooms), restaurant.html, meetings_events.html, gift_cards.html, and newsletter.html does not add extra URL parameters beyond those defined in the navigation information (room_detail.html uses roomId; others are plain navigations). No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage mechanisms; cross-page state is conveyed solely through URL parameters as described.

components.functionality: Loads high-level hotel overview data and exposes intro text, location summary, a primary image, and highlight counts for other sections (rooms, restaurant, spa, meetings). Provides contextual information and may supply values to teaser components.

Provides a compact room search form that initializes with suggested dates, guest counts, and sort options, lets the user adjust these values, and on submit navigates to rooms.html with all search parameters encoded as URL query parameters.

Retrieves a limited set of featured rooms and renders them as clickable cards showing key room attributes and a starting price. Clicking a card navigates to room_detail.html with the roomId URL parameter set to the selected room's id.

Retrieves a limited set of featured offers (such as room, spa, or dining packages) based on configured types, displays them as cards with highlight information, and provides a control to navigate to offers.html. On navigation it passes featureFilters (derived from the same filter logic used in the API call) and sortBy=guest_rating_desc as URL parameters.
Displays a concise teaser for the restaurant using contextual data (such as restaurantName from getHomeOverview.highlightCounts) and provides a click target that routes the user to restaurant.html.

Shows key information about meetings and event capabilities (for example, based on meetingRoomsCount from getHomeOverview.highlightCounts) and provides a click target that routes the user to meetings_events.html.

Presents a simple entry point to the gift cards section and, when clicked, navigates the user to gift_cards.html. Any labels or iconography are provided by the global localization and asset system, not hardcoded in this component.

Loads newsletter teaser content and displays a brief explanation and visual cue encouraging users to subscribe. The primary action navigates to newsletter.html, where the full newsletter signup form and preference management are handled.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing hero, content continues in a single, linear top-to-bottom sequence within a centered content column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, echoing the hero’s centered booking and logo cluster. Each major block (overview, search, featured rooms, featured offers, navigation teasers, newsletter) is treated as a distinct section band that reads in order as the user scrolls. Within each section, headings and key CTAs are generally left-aligned inside the center column, while secondary information and supporting visuals (cards, inputs) sit beneath or to the right, preserving the mockup’s blend of centered composition and subtle horizontal structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing hero, content continues in a single, linear top-to-bottom sequence within a centered content column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, echoing the hero’s centered booking and logo cluster. Each major block (overview, search, featured rooms, featured offers, navigation teasers, newsletter) is treated as a distinct section band that reads in order as the user scrolls. Within each section, headings and key CTAs are generally left-aligned inside the center column, while secondary information and supporting visuals (cards, inputs) sit beneath or to the right, preserving the mockup’s blend of centered composition and subtle horizontal structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero retains the top 60–70% of the initial viewport; all new components begin just below the fold. Each subsequent section uses generous vertical spacing: 40–64px of margin between sections and 24–32px of internal padding, mirroring the mockup’s tiered rhythm between tagline, logo, status bar, and booking widget. Featured strips (rooms/offers) occupy taller bands (roughly 35–45% of a viewport height each on desktop), while text-heavy or single-purpose sections (overview, newsletter) are slightly shallower (25–35% of viewport height). The full browser width is used for background color blocks and edge-to-edge alignment, but core content is constrained to the central column to maintain the boutique, gallery-like feeling.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderately spacious: each block has clear breathing room above and below, and internal elements (cards, form fields, headings) are separated by consistent 16–32px gaps. Sections that contain more items (like featured room cards) still feel ordered and calm due to consistent card sizing, aligned baselines, and sufficient horizontal gutters (16–24px). The layout avoids both extreme minimalism and visual clutter by limiting each section to a single primary task or story: understand the hotel, refine your search, browse rooms, browse offers, explore other services, then subscribe.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The vertical scroll is the primary motion, but within each band the eye follows a soft Z-pattern similar to the hero: heading or section label begins at the upper-left of the content column, a subtle CTA or secondary text anchors the upper-right, then the gaze moves diagonally down into the center where the main interactive elements (forms, cards) sit. This pattern is repeated down the page so users always know where to look first and where to act. Gold dividers, accent rules, and contrast between background bands (rich green, warm off-white, muted beige) serve as visual rails that guide the eye from one section to the next without abrupt breaks.

layout.overall_description: Directly beneath the immersive hero, the page transitions into a vertically stacked sequence of refined, card-like sections that sit within a centered content column over subtle, full-width background bands. First, a warmly lit overview panel introduces the hotel with left-aligned copy and a gold accent divider, giving users context before they commit to specifics. Just below, a compact but prominent room-search widget echoes the hero’s booking bar, stretching horizontally across the center column with clear date and guest fields framed by a gold outline. Scrolling further reveals two generous feature strips: one for rooms and one for offers, each using horizontally aligned cards that sit like curated tiles in a gallery, framed by plenty of breathing room and anchored by a heading on the left and a “View all” link on the right. Beneath these, an ‘Explore’ band presents three equal-width navigation teasers for the restaurant, meetings & events, and gift cards, arranged in a balanced row that quietly suggests exploration beyond the rooms themselves. At the very bottom of this stack, a calm newsletter section spans the central column, with a left-anchored title and a right-side email field and button, forming a final invitation to stay connected. The overall effect is a smooth, downward flow through a series of curated vignettes, each clearly delineated yet visually related to the hero through consistent spacing, gold accents, and the warm, boutique-hotel palette.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home overview panel sits immediately below the hero section, starting roughly one full viewport height from the top so it reveals as the user begins to scroll. It is contained within the central content column, spanning about 8 of 12 grid columns (approximately 66% of the page width) and centered horizontally with equal left and right margins. There is a generous top margin of about 64px from the bottom edge of the hero and a bottom margin of about 48px before the next section. At the top of this panel, a thin gold horizontal rule or small badge-style accent is centered or left-aligned to echo the hero’s gold dividers. Directly beneath it, a left-aligned section heading (e.g., “A Parisian-inspired townhouse hotel”) sits about 16–24px below the accent, with body text flowing in a single column that occupies roughly the left two-thirds of the panel’s width. To maintain a boutique, editorial feel, any supporting detail (a short bullet list, a subtle icon, or a secondary line like ‘Rooms · Restaurant · Events’) can sit beneath the body text with 16px spacing, aligned to the same left column. The rightmost third of the column stays relatively clean or may hold a small atmospheric image or emblem, ensuring the section feels airy and anchored rather than crowded. Overall height of the panel is about 25–35% of the viewport, depending on text length, and it reads as a calm, narrative introduction bridging the dramatic hero and the functional search below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section, flowing naturally in the document after the hero; it does not stick or overlap other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room search widget is positioned directly beneath the overview panel, with a vertical gap of around 40px creating a distinct but related step in the flow from inspiration to action. It is horizontally centered within the same content column and spans slightly wider than the overview text area—about 70% of the page width or 9 of 12 grid columns—so it feels like a substantial interactive bar similar to the hero’s booking widget. The widget appears as a low-profile, horizontally oriented strip with rounded corners and a delicate gold outline, visually echoing the hero’s booking frame. Internally, it follows a left-to-right sequence: on the far left, a subtle label such as “Plan your stay” or “Check availability” sits above or beside the first input, followed by side-by-side fields for check-in and check-out dates, then a guest selector, and finally a primary BOOK button anchored at the far right. Each field has consistent internal padding (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and is separated by 16px gutters, keeping the layout open. The widget’s height is modest—roughly 1/6 of the viewport height—leaving ample visible background and ensuring it doesn’t overwhelm nearby content. It appears firmly rooted on the page (not floating), with sufficient padding above and below (24px each) so that it feels like a refined toolbar hovering over a warm, neutral background band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the content column; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick. On narrower viewports it can wrap fields into two rows while remaining visually centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured rooms strip sits below the room search widget with a clear separation of roughly 56px, marking the transition from focused search to curated discovery. This section stretches edge-to-edge as a subtle off-white or very light warm background band, while the core content is again constrained to the centered column (about 70% of page width). At the top of the strip, a left-aligned section heading (e.g., “Featured Rooms & Suites”) sits approximately 24px below the top padding, with a gold accent line or small emblem either above or integrated into the heading to anchor the eye. On the right side of this top row, aligned to the same baseline as the heading but flush with the right edge of the content column, a “View all rooms” text link or understated button provides a counterweight, establishing a clear Z-pattern. Below this header row, at about 24px distance, a horizontal row of 3 featured room cards is centered within the column. Each card occupies roughly 30% of the content column width, with 16–24px of horizontal space between them, and follows a consistent vertical structure: an image taking up the top two-thirds of the card, a room name and short description beneath, then perhaps a starting rate and a subtle “Details” link. Each card has internal padding of 16–24px and a fine gold or dark green keyline to match the boutique aesthetic. The strip’s total height is about 35–45% of the viewport, enough to give the cards breathing room above and below (24–32px padding) so they feel like carefully curated tiles rather than cramped thumbnails.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow. On smaller screens, the cards can switch to a horizontal scroll or wrap into two rows, but on desktop they remain a single three-card row centered in the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured offers strip follows immediately after the rooms strip, separated by around 48px of vertical space to signal a new but related content block. To give it slightly stronger emphasis and to differentiate it from the rooms section, this band uses a richer background—either the brand’s deep green or a warm beige—while still hosting the content within the same centered column (60–70% page width). At the top of the section, a left-aligned heading (e.g., “Seasonal Offers & Packages”) sits 24px below the band’s top, with a gold rule or divider just above or below it, echoing the hero’s gold bar and tying the strip to the brand’s premium cues. On the right, aligned with the heading baseline, a “View all offers” link or ghost-style button provides balance and defines the Z-pattern starting point. Below this header row, at a 24px gap, a row of 2–3 offer cards spans the content column. Each offer card is slightly more text-forward than the room cards: a bold offer title, a concise description, key value (e.g., “Stay 3 nights, pay for 2”), and a subtle CTA. Cards sit on white or slightly lighter panels with generous padding (24px) and rounded corners, outlined with a delicate gold stroke to stand out from the darker background. Horizontal gutters of 16–24px ensure spacing between cards. The section height is comparable to or slightly shorter than the rooms strip—approximately 30–40% of the viewport—maintaining visual weight while still feeling airy and editorial.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll. On narrower viewports, cards can stack vertically or become a horizontal carousel, but on desktop they remain in a single row within the centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The restaurant teaser is part of a trio of navigation teasers (restaurant, meetings & events, gift cards) grouped into a single ‘Explore’ band positioned beneath the featured offers strip, separated by about 56px of vertical space. This band spans full width with a neutral, warm background that contrasts gently with the section above, while its content is laid out within the central column. At the top-left of the band, a small all-caps label such as “Explore” followed by a slightly larger heading (e.g., “Beyond your room”) introduces the group, with 16px spacing between label and heading. About 24px below this heading, three equal-width cards are laid out in a horizontal row. The restaurant teaser card occupies the left third of this row, taking up around 30% of the content column width, with 16–24px gutters between it and the adjacent cards. The card itself is vertically structured: a wide image or photograph of the restaurant interior covers the top half to two-thirds of the card; below it, there is a title like “Restaurant & Bar,” a one- or two-line description, and a subtle arrow or “Discover” link aligned to the bottom right. Internal padding of 16–24px and a faint border or shadow keep the card consistent with the boutique aesthetic. This card is horizontally aligned with the meetings & events and gift cards teasers so that all three form a balanced carousel-like row that feels like a curated menu of experiences.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static as part of the three-card row within the ‘Explore’ section; behaves like a standard card on desktop, potentially stacking or sliding horizontally on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meetings & events teaser sits in the center of the same three-card row as the restaurant and gift cards teasers, sharing the ‘Explore’ band below the featured offers. It occupies the middle third of the content column within that row, matching the restaurant card’s width (around 30% of the content column) and height to maintain a strong visual rhythm. There is 16–24px of horizontal space on both sides, creating equal gutters between all three cards. Visually, this card mirrors the restaurant teaser’s structure: a top image of a meeting room or event space takes up roughly the top half to two-thirds of the card height; beneath it, a compact title like “Meetings & Events,” a short descriptive line (e.g., “Intimate salons for gatherings and celebrations”), and a call-to-action link are stacked with 8–16px spacing. All padding and border treatments align with the other cards (gold or dark green keyline, rounded corners, 16–24px internal padding), ensuring the three cards read as a single set. Its central placement ensures the eye naturally passes over it after moving from the left restaurant card, reinforcing the Z-pattern across the row (left teaser → center teaser → right teaser).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the three-card row; it scrolls with the page and behaves like a standard card, with responsive stacking or horizontal scroll on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift cards teaser occupies the rightmost position in the three-card row within the ‘Explore’ band beneath the featured offers strip. It fills the right third of the content column, matching the width and height of the restaurant and meetings & events cards so the row feels balanced and cohesive. A 16–24px gutter separates it from the central meetings & events card, and its right edge aligns with the right boundary of the content column. The card’s internal layout is consistent with its siblings: an evocative image (e.g., a wrapped gift card, ribbon, or detail shot of a key and envelope) sits at the top, taking up about half to two-thirds of the card’s height. Below, a title like “Gift Cards,” a brief line of copy about gifting stays or dinners, and a right-aligned link or small CTA button are arranged with 8–16px vertical spacing. The card uses the same internal padding (16–24px) and fine keyline or shadow treatment to keep the boutique, premium feel. Because of its rightmost placement, it completes the horizontal flow and subtly draws the eye toward the column’s outer edge, echoing the top navigation’s logo-left / CTA-right structure in a smaller, content-focused context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the three-card row; it scrolls normally with the page and adapts to vertical stacking or horizontal scrolling alongside its companion cards on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter teaser sits as the final major section on the home page, positioned below the ‘Explore’ band with approximately 64px of vertical separation, giving it a sense of calm closure. This section returns to a simpler, minimal band—either a light neutral or white background—with content centered in the same 60–70% width column as prior sections. The layout is horizontally structured to echo the hero’s balanced composition: on the left half of the content column, a left-aligned section heading (e.g., “Stay in the know”) and a short line of supporting text (such as “Receive stories, offers, and city tips from our townhouse.”) are stacked with 8–16px spacing. On the right half, aligned to the same vertical center, a compact signup form is placed: a single email input field spanning roughly two-thirds of the right half’s width, followed by a primary CTA button (“Subscribe”) positioned immediately to its right or just beneath it, depending on available space. The form sits inside a subtle outlined or underlined style with internal padding of 12–16px, keeping it lightly framed without overwhelming the section. Generous top and bottom padding (32–48px each) ensure the newsletter feels open and inviting, with enough negative space below it to gently fade into the page footer. This section acts as a quiet, refined final call to action that aligns visually with the site’s premium, boutique aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level content near the bottom of the page; it scrolls normally and does not stick, with the form adapting to vertical stacking of input and button on narrow screens.

name: Rooms & Suites
title: Rooms & Suites
description: Search, filter, sort, and compare rooms and suites for selected stay dates, with direct access to detailed room information and the booking flow.

core_features: Initialize the search form and filter controls using URL parameters or default values returned by getRoomSearchFilterOptions, keeping all visible controls in sync with the current URL state.

Search available rooms with searchAvailableRooms using the current check-in/check-out dates, number of adults and children, active filters, and selected sort option, then display the returned room results.
Allow users to adjust filters (view type, amenities, maximum nightly price) and sort order, and immediately refresh room results via searchAvailableRooms based on the updated criteria.

Display each room as an interactive card showing key room details and cheapest available rate plan information, with navigation into room_detail.html while preserving the current search context via URL parameters.

Enable room comparison by letting users add or remove rooms from a comparison set via addRoomToComparison and removeRoomFromComparison, with a comparison summary and limit feedback based on the interface responses.
Provide a comparison toolbar that reflects the current comparison state and navigates to compare_rooms.html with the current search context when the user chooses to compare selected rooms.
Allow users to clear all comparison selections at once via clearRoomComparison, updating both the comparison toolbar and room cards.
Persist all search, filter, and sort settings in URL parameters so that the page can be deep-linked, shared, and correctly restored when revisited or when using browser navigation.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Landing on the Rooms & Suites page: (1) User navigates to rooms.html either from the site navigation or through a deep link, possibly with URL parameters such as checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, viewType, amenities, maxPrice, and sortBy already present. (2) The page controller parses the URL parameters. (3) The page calls getRoomSearchFilterOptions to obtain available viewTypes, amenityOptions, priceRange, sortOptions, and default values for dates and guests. (4) For each search field and filter, the page chooses the URL value if present, otherwise uses the corresponding default value from getRoomSearchFilterOptions. (5) The chosen values are applied to the search form and filter components, and any missing but now-determined values can be written back into the URL. (6) The controller constructs a filters object based on the active filter controls and calls searchAvailableRooms with the resolved checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, filters, and sortBy. (7) The returned room results populate the room-results list and room-card components.

Workflow 2 – Adjusting dates and guest counts: (1) User changes check-in/check-out dates or the number of adults/children in the search form and submits the form. (2) The search form component updates its internal state and emits the new criteria to the page controller. (3) The controller updates the corresponding URL parameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) without adding any other static defaults. (4) The controller rebuilds the filters object from the filter components and URL, then calls searchAvailableRooms with the updated criteria and current sortBy. (5) The results list refreshes with the new room data returned by searchAvailableRooms.

Workflow 3 – Applying filters and sorting: (1) User selects or changes one or more filter options (e.g., a view type, several amenities, a maximum price) in the filter sidebar. (2) The filter component updates its internal representation of the filters and informs the page controller. (3) The controller updates the relevant URL parameters (viewType, amenities as a comma-separated list of codes, maxPrice) to reflect the new filter state. (4) The controller constructs a filters object from the active filters and calls searchAvailableRooms with the existing dates/guest counts and current sortBy. (5) User changes the selected sort option in the sort control, which updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers another call to searchAvailableRooms, refreshing the results using the same search criteria and filters but a different sort order.

Workflow 4 – Viewing room details from the listing: (1) User reviews room cards in the results list. (2) User clicks a room card to view more information. (3) The click event reads the selected room ID from the card data and the current search form state (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children). (4) The page navigates to room_detail.html with URL parameters roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children, allowing the detail page to show availability and pricing in the same context. (5) If the user returns from room_detail.html to rooms.html using browser navigation or a link that retains those parameters, the Rooms & Suites page re-initializes with the same context and refreshes results accordingly.

Workflow 5 – Managing room comparison: (1) In the room results list, each room card includes a control to add or remove that room from comparison. (2) When the user activates the compare control to add a room, the page calls addRoomToComparison with the roomId. (3) The response includes a comparison object and a maxRoomsReached flag; the comparison-selection bar and room cards update to reflect the new comparison.roomIds, and an informational message can be shown using the response message if the maximum number of comparable rooms has been reached. (4) If the user deactivates the compare control on a room already in the comparison, the page calls removeRoomFromComparison with the roomId and updates the comparison state based on the returned comparison object. (5) At any time, the user can click a clear-comparison action, which calls clearRoomComparison and resets comparison state across all components. (6) When at least two rooms are in the comparison set (as indicated by the current comparison.roomIds length), the Compare Rooms button becomes enabled; clicking it navigates to compare_rooms.html with the current search context via checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children URL parameters.

Workflow 6 – Deep linking and navigation: (1) A user copies the current rooms.html URL, which includes all relevant parameters (dates, guests, filters, sortBy). (2) Another user opens this URL in a browser. (3) The Rooms & Suites page parses the URL parameters, uses getRoomSearchFilterOptions for option lists and any missing defaults, and replays the same search via searchAvailableRooms. (4) The filter sidebar, search form, and sort control reflect the state encoded in the URL, providing a consistent, shareable experience without relying on local storage.
interactions: Interaction – Page initialization: On page load or when the URL changes, the controller component reads all defined URL parameters, calls getRoomSearchFilterOptions once to obtain selectable options and default values, merges URL parameters with defaults for any missing search fields, applies them to the search-form and filter-sidebar components, optionally writes missing but resolved values into the URL, and triggers the initial call to searchAvailableRooms to populate results.

Interaction – Search form submission: When the user submits the search form after changing check-in/check-out dates or guests, onSubmitSearch is fired. The handler updates the URL parameters for checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children, reconstructs the filters object using the current filter component state, and calls searchAvailableRooms with the updated search criteria and the current sortBy value.

Interaction – Filter changes: When the user adjusts any filter (view type selection, amenity toggles, max price control), the filter-sidebar fires onFilterChange. The handler converts the current filter selections into URL parameters (e.g., encoding multiple amenities as a comma-separated list) and updates or removes those parameters accordingly. It then builds the filters object and calls searchAvailableRooms with unchanged dates and guests but updated filters, causing the results list to refresh.

Interaction – Sort option change: When the user selects a different sort option from the sort control, onSortChange is triggered. The handler sets the sortBy URL parameter to the newly selected sort value (taken directly from getRoomSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions), then calls searchAvailableRooms with the existing date, guest, and filter values and the new sortBy, updating the ordering of the results list.

Interaction – Room card click: When the user clicks on a room card's main action area, onClickRoomCard is fired. The handler reads the roomId from the card's bound Room entity and reads the current checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children from the search-form state (which mirrors URL parameters). It then navigates to room_detail.html with these values encoded as URL parameters, without altering any other search settings.

Interaction – Toggle room in comparison: When the user toggles the comparison control on a room card, onToggleCompare is fired. If the room is not currently in the comparison set, the handler calls addRoomToComparison with the roomId; otherwise, it calls removeRoomFromComparison with the roomId. In both cases, the handler uses the returned comparison object to update internal comparison state, the comparison toolbar, and the visual compare state on each card; if maxRoomsReached is true, the handler may show the returned message to explain why additional rooms cannot be added.

Interaction – Clear all compared rooms: When the user clicks the clear-comparison action in the comparison toolbar, onClearComparison is fired. The handler calls clearRoomComparison with no parameters. If comparisonCleared in the response is true, the handler resets the comparison state to empty, disables the Compare Rooms button, and unchecks the compare control on all room cards.

Interaction – Navigate to compare page: When the user clicks the Compare Rooms button and the current comparison state indicates that at least two rooms are selected, onClickCompareRooms is fired. The handler reads the current search form values (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) and navigates to compare_rooms.html with these parameters in the URL. The comparison room set itself is not encoded in the URL but is maintained through the RoomComparison entity managed via the comparison interfaces.

Interaction – Browser navigation (back/forward): When the user uses the browser's back or forward buttons to navigate between different rooms.html states, the URL parameters change. The controller responds to URL change events by re-parsing the current parameters, re-synchronizing the search-form, filter-sidebar, and sort-control components, and calling searchAvailableRooms with the parameters encoded in the URL, thereby restoring the previous or next state without relying on localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic: The source of truth for the Rooms & Suites page state is the URL query string. On initialization, the page parses the URL parameters checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, viewType, amenities, maxPrice, and sortBy. It then calls getRoomSearchFilterOptions to obtain lists of viewTypes, amenityOptions, priceRange, and sortOptions, plus default values for check-in date, check-out date, adults, and children. For each of these core search fields (dates and guests), if a corresponding URL parameter exists, it is used; if not, the default from getRoomSearchFilterOptions is applied and may be written into the URL so that subsequent navigations remain consistent. For filters, viewType and maxPrice map directly to URL parameters. Amenities selections are encoded as a comma-separated string of amenity codes in the amenities parameter, constructed from and parsed into arrays as needed by the filter-sidebar and controller. The sortBy parameter is always taken from the user-selected sort option; if no value is present in the URL, a default may be inferred from getRoomSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions (such as the first option) and optionally written back to the URL. Every time the user changes search fields, filters, or sort order, the controller updates the relevant URL parameters (adding, updating, or removing them) and then invokes searchAvailableRooms with the resolved checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, a filters object built from the current viewType, amenities, maxPrice, and any other filter-related URL values, plus the sortBy parameter. The page does not use localStorage for persistence; instead, state is preserved across sessions and navigation via the URL. Comparison state is not stored in the URL but is managed through the RoomComparison entity returned by addRoomToComparison, removeRoomFromComparison, and clearRoomComparison. The comparison-selection bar and room-card components rely on the current in-memory representation of the latest comparison object and refresh it exclusively through these interfaces.

components.functionality: Central orchestrator for the Rooms & Suites page that initializes state from the URL, invokes getRoomSearchFilterOptions and searchAvailableRooms, coordinates updates between the search form, filters, sort control, results list, and comparison state, and keeps the URL parameters synchronized with user actions.
data_binding: URLParameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, viewType, amenities, maxPrice, sortBy)
getRoomSearchFilterOptions result (viewTypes, amenityOptions, priceRange, sortOptions, defaultCheckInDate, defaultCheckOutDate, defaultAdults, defaultChildren)
searchAvailableRooms result (array of { room: Room, cheapestRatePlan: RatePlan, totalPrice, averageNightlyPrice, currency, nightlyPriceBreakdown, viewLabel, bedLabel, amenityLabels })

components.functionality: Displays and manages the primary search inputs for check-in date, check-out date, number of adults, and number of children. Initializes its fields from URL parameters or from default values provided by getRoomSearchFilterOptions, and submits updated values to the controller to refresh search results and update the URL.

Provides filter controls for narrowing the room list, including view type selection, amenity multi-select, and maximum nightly price. It initializes from the current URL parameters and from the options returned by getRoomSearchFilterOptions, converts user selections into a filters object for searchAvailableRooms, and synchronizes the viewType, amenities, and maxPrice URL parameters with the current filter state.
data_binding: Derived filters object passed into searchAvailableRooms.filters

components.functionality: Allows the user to select how room results are ordered, using sort options provided by getRoomSearchFilterOptions. Maps the selected sort option to the sortBy URL parameter and triggers the controller to re-run searchAvailableRooms with the updated sort criteria.

Displays the list of available rooms returned by searchAvailableRooms. For each result item, creates a room-card instance showing core details, and handles empty or loading states based purely on the dynamic data provided by the controller.
data_binding: searchAvailableRooms result (array of { room: Room, cheapestRatePlan: RatePlan, totalPrice, averageNightlyPrice, currency, nightlyPriceBreakdown, viewLabel, bedLabel, amenityLabels })
RoomComparison.roomIds (for marking which cards are currently selected for comparison)

components.functionality: Represents a single room and its cheapest rate for the current search criteria. Shows room attributes and pricing derived from the Room and RatePlan data, indicates whether the room is in the current comparison set, allows the user to toggle comparison status via the comparison interfaces, and navigates to the room detail page while preserving the current search context.

Displays the current comparison selection state, including the number of rooms selected based on the RoomComparison entity, and provides actions to clear all comparisons or proceed to compare_rooms.html. It relies on addRoomToComparison, removeRoomFromComparison, and clearRoomComparison responses to remain consistent with the backend comparison state.
data_binding: Derived count of selected rooms based on RoomComparison.roomIds.length

components.functionality: Encapsulates communication with the comparison interfaces, updating the shared RoomComparison state in response to user actions on room cards and the comparison-selection bar by calling addRoomToComparison, removeRoomFromComparison, and clearRoomComparison, and broadcasting updated comparison data to dependent components.
data_binding: RoomComparison (latest object returned from comparison interfaces)

components.functionality: Responsible for reading the current URL query parameters on initialization and on navigation events, normalizing them into a structured state object, and applying updates when the search form, filter sidebar, or sort control change values. It ensures that all changes to search, filter, and sort settings are reflected in the URL, and emits URL change events back to the controller.
data_binding: URLParameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, viewType, amenities, maxPrice, sortBy)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom hero-driven structure, but adapts it for browsing rooms: a full-width hero at the top with background imagery, overlaid global header (from the main site), then a centered content column. Within the hero, the Rooms & Suites title and a compact search form sit stacked vertically in the visual center, echoing the original tagline + booking widget stack. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a two-column content area: a left filter sidebar and right room results column, both constrained to a 12-column grid within a centered container. Above the results list, a horizontal control strip aligns the filter label area on the left and the sort control on the right, similar to the status bar + booking widget relationship. A comparison-selection bar appears near the bottom of the viewport as a horizontal strip once rooms are selected, mirroring the low-position, full-width booking widget presence from the hero.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom hero-driven structure, but adapts it for browsing rooms: a full-width hero at the top with background imagery, overlaid global header (from the main site), then a centered content column. Within the hero, the Rooms & Suites title and a compact search form sit stacked vertically in the visual center, echoing the original tagline + booking widget stack. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a two-column content area: a left filter sidebar and right room results column, both constrained to a 12-column grid within a centered container. Above the results list, a horizontal control strip aligns the filter label area on the left and the sort control on the right, similar to the status bar + booking widget relationship. A comparison-selection bar appears near the bottom of the viewport as a horizontal strip once rooms are selected, mirroring the low-position, full-width booking widget presence from the hero.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the hero occupies roughly the top 35–40% of the viewport, similar to the original but slightly shortened to make room for results content below the fold. The centered title + search form cluster uses about 50–60% of the hero’s height, leaving generous image space above and below. The main content area (filters + results) fills most of the remaining viewport and scrolls. Horizontally, all primary content (hero text, search form, filters, results, comparison bar content) stays within a central column about 60–70% of the viewport width, aligned to a 12-column grid. Within this, the filter sidebar uses about 3–4 columns (25–30% width) on desktop, and the room results column uses about 8–9 columns (70–75% width). The comparison bar spans the same content width as the main column but is visually treated like a full-width band across the central container at the bottom of the viewport.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout follows the mockup’s moderately spacious density. Each functional block—hero title/search, filter sidebar, sort bar, room cards, comparison strip—has its own vertical breathing room, with 24–40px gaps between sections and 16–24px internal padding. Room cards are rich but not cramped: images, key details, rate, and actions are clearly tiered with consistent vertical spacing. The filter sidebar is grouped into clear sections with labeled headers and comfortable spacing between controls. Overall, the page feels rich and layered (multiple controls and information visible at once) but controlled, never crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mockup’s Z-pattern. The eye first catches the top-left logo in the global header, then scans to the right across the main navigation and booking CTA. It then drops diagonally into the hero center where the Rooms & Suites title and search form sit, acting as the primary focal point. From there, the eye moves downward to the top of the content area where filters on the left and sort on the right form the next Z line. Attention then slides across to the first room card in the results list, then continues down the list. As users select rooms, the eye is drawn to the bottom-anchored comparison-selection bar, which mirrors the bottom-booking-widget focus from the original hero and provides a clear next step without overpowering the main list.

layout.overall_description: The Rooms & Suites page opens with a full-width hero image echoing the hotel’s warm, boutique-hotel aesthetic: a dark, richly lit room or corridor image stretching edge-to-edge. The global dark green header with logo and navigation overlays the top of this hero, maintaining the same premium, slightly vintage look as the home page. Centered within the hero, a narrow content column holds a refined Rooms & Suites title, flanked or underlined by subtle gold accents, with a compact stay-search form stacked directly beneath it. This form mirrors the original booking widget: a horizontal band with date inputs, guest selector, and a primary BOOK button framed by a fine gold outline, visually hovering above the hero background. As you scroll past the hero, the page transitions into a structured two-column layout on a light background: a left filter sidebar with gold-tinted section dividers and a right results column filled with tall, card-based room entries. Above the results, a horizontal control row aligns the overall filter context on the left with a right-aligned sort dropdown, maintaining a familiar left-to-right reading of options then refinement. Room cards repeat vertically with generous spacing, each anchoring attention with a large room photo on the left and a neatly organized details-and-price block on the right. When rooms are selected for comparison, a dark green, gold-accented comparison bar slides in at the bottom of the viewport, spanning the same central width as the main content and holding selected room chips and a Compare button; it feels like a subtle, luxurious control strip, always accessible but not obstructive. Throughout, the central column, comfortable spacing, and gold accents maintain the refined Parisian atmosphere of the original design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual component acts as the structural wrapper for the entire Rooms & Suites page content. Conceptually, it spans the full viewport width and height, sitting beneath the global header and hero but above the browser background. All other Rooms & Suites components (hero search form, filters, sort control, results list, comparison bar, and the non-visual state managers) are nested inside this controller in a single, top-to-bottom flow. In layout terms, it defines the central content column (approximately 60–70% of viewport width, centered) and the main vertical rhythm (section spacing of roughly 24–40px between blocks).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural container; behaves as a normal document root for this page’s main content, occupying full page width and stacking its child components vertically in order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rooms-search-form sits in the lower half of the hero section, aligned to the center of the page within the main content column. It appears directly beneath the Rooms & Suites title and any decorative gold divider line, with approximately 24–32px of vertical space separating title and form. Horizontally, the form spans about 70–80% of the content column (roughly 45–55% of viewport width on large screens), leaving equal breathing room to the left and right. The form takes the shape of a horizontal pill or rounded rectangle with a thin gold outline and a soft, light interior background, echoing the original booking widget. Inside, the controls are arranged from left to right: check-in date, check-out date, guests selector, and a rightmost BOOK or Update Results button. Each field is given approximately equal width, with the primary action button slightly wider and filled in dark green to stand out. The form visually overlaps the hero background but sits entirely above any detailed bed imagery, preserving clear legibility and a premium, floating card effect. On scroll, it remains in place as part of the hero content and does not remain sticky; users can re-run searches by scrolling back to the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the hero section; positioned in normal flow within a vertically centered hero column, not sticky, but visually framed to feel anchored to the hero’s lower-center region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rooms-filter-sidebar begins just below the hero section, aligned to the left within the central content column. It occupies roughly 3–4 of the 12 grid columns (about 25–30% of the central content width), extending vertically down the page alongside the room results list. The top of the sidebar aligns vertically with the top of the main content area’s control row (where the sort control also sits), with roughly 32px of vertical gap between the bottom of the hero and the top of this row. Inside the sidebar, filters are grouped into stacked segments (e.g., Dates summary, Room type, Occupancy, Price range, Amenities) separated by subtle gold dividers or enough vertical spacing (16–24px) to read as distinct sections. Each section has a small, refined heading (possibly in serif or small caps) in dark green, with inputs (toggles, checkboxes, sliders) arranged in a clean vertical list. On wide screens, the sidebar stays visually anchored to the left edge of the central column; white or light background, with maybe a faint border or shadow to echo the card feeling. It is tall and scrolls with the page content, but its inner content may become sticky near the top so that the filter controls remain visible as the user scrolls through the results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Left column in a two-column layout; positioned in the normal flow but with a sticky inner container that pins the filters near the top of the viewport (e.g., 80–96px offset from the very top to clear the header) while the user scrolls through longer results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rooms-sort-control is placed at the top of the results area, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter sidebar to create a unifying control row. It sits in the right column (room-results-list column) but visually reads as part of a full-width control band spanning from the left edge of the filters to the right edge of the results. The sort control itself is right-aligned within this band, occupying the top-right corner of the results column, with 16–24px of padding around it. To its left (within the same row), there may be a concise label like ‘X rooms found for your dates’ or a chips area indicating active filters, reinforcing the Z-pattern: filters on the left, sort control on the far right. The sort UI could be a compact dropdown or segmented control framed in a thin gold outline or with subtle gold text. Vertical spacing above and below this control row is modest (around 16–24px), so it reads clearly as the header area of the results list without becoming heavy or blocky.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned at the top of the room-results-list column in normal flow, aligned horizontally with the filter sidebar. It scrolls away with the results (not sticky), maintaining a simple, predictable layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-results-list occupies the right portion of the main content area, to the immediate right of the filter sidebar, sharing a consistent baseline at the top with the sort control row. It spans approximately 8–9 of the 12 grid columns within the central container (about 70–75% of the content width), leaving a comfortable 24–32px gutter between itself and the filter sidebar. Within this column, room-card components are stacked vertically, each occupying nearly the full width of the column, with a generous vertical gap (24–32px) between cards to preserve the rich but uncluttered feeling. The list stretches down the page and scrolls, while the hero above and comparison bar below (when visible) act as conceptual caps. The background behind the list is a light, warm neutral tone, providing strong contrast with the card surfaces and tying into the boutique aesthetic. Pagination or infinite-scroll cues appear at the bottom of this column, centered within its width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right column in a two-column layout; positioned in normal document flow, vertically scrollable as the main content area. It respects the fixed or sticky behaviors of the global header, filter sidebar inner content, and comparison-selection-bar, but itself is non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each room-card is a horizontally oriented, premium-feeling card within the room-results-list column. On desktop, it spans nearly the full width of the results column (leaving small 16–24px insets from the column edges) and uses a two-thirds / one-third split: the left roughly 60–65% is dominated by a large room photo, while the right 35–40% contains textual details and pricing. The card has a light background with subtle shadow or a thin gold or off-gold outline, echoing the booking widget and badge motifs. Internally, the card has 16–24px padding on all sides. The left image is cropped so that warm, inviting details (bed, textiles, lighting) are visible but not overly busy; it reaches from the top padding line down to close to the bottom, with slightly rounded corners. On the right, content is stack-structured: room name at the top (larger serif or elegant display type), a short descriptive line beneath, key badges (e.g., ‘Sea View’, ‘Breakfast Included’) laid out horizontally with small gold dividers or chips, then a compact list of amenities or occupancy icons. The lower-right of the card is reserved for rate and actions: price per night in larger, bold type, and a primary action button (‘View Room’ or ‘Book’) in dark green, right-aligned, with possibly a secondary link-style action (‘Add to compare’) just above or below. Vertical spacing inside the card maintains the 8–16px rhythm, preserving clarity. On hover, the card might slightly lift with a very subtle glow or gold accent line to communicate interactivity while maintaining the refined look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Child of the room-results-list, stacked vertically in normal flow. On responsive breakpoints, it can pivot from a side-by-side image/details layout to a vertical image-on-top, details-below stack while maintaining the same padding and spacing logic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-selection-bar is a horizontal strip that appears once the user selects one or more room-cards for comparison. It spans the same central content width as the main container (about 60–70% of the viewport) and is centered horizontally, but visually reads like a full-width control band anchored near the bottom of the viewport. Its background is the signature dark green, with a slim gold top border and gold-accented text and icons, reinforcing its importance and echoing the hero’s status bar/booking widget aesthetic. Within the bar, content is laid out in a left-to-right flow: on the left, a label like ‘Compare (2) rooms’ and a horizontal row of compact room chips or thumbnails; on the right, a prominent ‘Compare Rooms’ button in a light, contrasting fill (e.g., ivory or warm neutral) with dark green text, plus a smaller ‘Clear’ link. The bar has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding on both sides, keeping it visually dense but not cramped. When it appears, it slightly overlaps the bottom of the viewing area while leaving enough room above so that the last room card is still visible; it does not hide essential content but gently compresses the scroll area, remaining in view as the user scrolls through results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport within the page content area (e.g., fixed relative to the viewport with left/right constrained to the central content column). It enters with a subtle upward slide or fade when at least one room is selected, and exits when selections are cleared, without disrupting the scroll position of the results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual component coordinates state between selected room-cards and the comparison-selection-bar, as well as any subsequent comparison view. Conceptually, it spans the whole page but is invisible, sitting behind the room-results-list and comparison-selection-bar. It ensures that the comparison bar only appears when relevant (one or more rooms selected) and that the selected state is reflected consistently across cards and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state manager; logically layered above the rooms-page-controller but below visual components. It has no spatial footprint and does not affect the visual layout directly, only triggering visibility changes and state updates.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is another non-visual component that synchronizes the page’s filters, sort options, search parameters, and possibly comparison selection with the URL. It has no on-screen presence but conceptually wraps around or sits alongside the main interactive components (rooms-search-form, rooms-filter-sidebar, rooms-sort-control, room-results-list, comparison-selection-bar), ensuring that the layout can be refreshed or shared while preserving the same visual state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, no spatial behavior; operates behind the scenes, listening to and updating the URL without affecting positioning or dimensions of any visible components.

name: Room Details & Rates
title: Room Details & Rates
description: Displays full details for a selected room, shows available rate plans for chosen dates and guests, and starts the booking flow for the selected rate.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for the selected room using getRoomDetails (room name, description, view, bed type, amenities, images, and general policies).
Read stay parameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) from the URL and use them to fetch available rate plans with getRoomRates.
Show a list of rate plans for the room (from getRoomRates), including inclusions, refundable conditions, nightly and total prices, and nightly price breakdowns.
Allow the user to adjust stay details (dates and guests) on the page, update the URL parameters accordingly, and re-fetch rate plans via getRoomRates.
Let the user select a single rate plan to book and clearly indicate which plan is currently selected.
Start a booking for the chosen room and rate plan by calling startRoomBooking and, on success, navigate to booking_details.html with the returned bookingId in the URL.
Present general room policies from getRoomDetails alongside rate-specific conditions (such as cancellation policy from each rate plan) for informed selection.
Provide a Back to results navigation that returns to rooms.html with the current stay parameters and any existing sortBy parameter preserved in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View room details from a rooms search result and book a rate
1. User clicks on a room card on rooms.html, which opens room_detail.html with roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, and optionally sortBy in the URL.
2. On page load, the page controller reads roomId and stay parameters from the URL and calls getRoomDetails(roomId) to fetch room information.
3. If checkInDate and checkOutDate are present, the page calls getRoomRates(roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) and displays all returned rate plans in the rate-plan list.
4. User reviews room information, amenities, images, and policies while the rate-plan list shows total prices and inclusions for each plan.
5. User selects one rate plan in the list; the selection state is stored in page state and reflected in the UI.
6. User clicks the primary booking button (Book Now / Continue); the page calls startRoomBooking(roomId, selectedRatePlanId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children).
7. On successful response, the page reads startRoomBookingResponse.booking.id and redirects the browser to booking_details.html?bookingId={id}.
8. If the API returns an error, the booking action bar displays the error message from the API and keeps the user on the same page.

Workflow 2: Land on a room from a non-dated entry point (e.g., homepage) and then select dates
1. User clicks a room link on index.html or offers.html, which opens room_detail.html with roomId in the URL but without stay parameters.
2. On page load, the page controller reads roomId and calls getRoomDetails(roomId). Since checkInDate and checkOutDate are missing, getRoomRates is not called initially.
3. The room details (images, amenities, description, general policies) are displayed, and the stay-parameters bar shows empty or default date and guest inputs.
4. User enters check-in and check-out dates and selects adults and children in the stay-parameters bar and submits the form.
5. The page updates the URL with checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children, then calls getRoomRates(roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children).
6. Once rates are loaded, the rate-plan list becomes populated; the user then follows Workflow 1 from rate selection onward.

Workflow 3: Change dates or guest counts while viewing a room
1. User is on room_detail.html with roomId and stay parameters already present in the URL and rate plans loaded.
2. User edits check-in/check-out dates or the number of adults/children in the stay-parameters bar.
3. On submit, the page updates the URL query parameters with the new checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children values.
4. After the URL is updated, the page re-calls getRoomRates(roomId, newCheckInDate, newCheckOutDate, newAdults, newChildren).
5. The rate-plan list is refreshed to reflect the new availability and prices; any previous rate-plan selection is cleared to avoid booking with stale data.
6. User selects a new rate plan and proceeds to booking using the same startRoomBooking flow.

Workflow 4: Return to search results with preserved search context
1. User arrives from rooms.html with roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, and optionally sortBy in the URL.
2. User reviews room details and rate plans, possibly changes nothing.
3. User clicks the Back to results link.
4. The page reads currentStay.checkInDate, currentStay.checkOutDate, currentStay.adults, currentStay.children, and any currentSearch.sortBy from the current URL.
5. The browser navigates to rooms.html with these values as URL parameters: rooms.html?checkInDate={currentStay.checkInDate}&checkOutDate={currentStay.checkOutDate}&adults={currentStay.adults}&children={currentStay.children}&sortBy={currentSearch.sortBy if present}.
6. rooms.html can then reconstruct the prior search context and show results consistent with the user’s last parameters.
interactions: On page load, the page controller parses roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, and sortBy (if present) from the URL query string and initiates getRoomDetails(roomId); if both dates are present, it also initiates getRoomRates with all stay parameters.

User interaction: Changing check-in or check-out date inputs in the stay-parameters bar and submitting the form updates the corresponding URL parameters and triggers a new getRoomRates call for the updated stay.
User interaction: Changing the number of adults or children in the stay-parameters bar and submitting the form updates the adults and children URL parameters and triggers a new getRoomRates call.
User interaction: Clicking on a rate plan row/card in the rate-plan list marks that plan as selected; any previously selected plan is deselected so that only one plan can be active at a time.

User interaction: Expanding a nightly price breakdown control on a rate plan (for example, a toggle or link) reveals or hides the nightlyPriceBreakdown data for that plan without additional API calls, using the breakdown already returned by getRoomRates.

User interaction: Viewing rate-specific cancellation details uses the cancellationPolicy field from the corresponding RatePlan object; this is displayed alongside or within a policies component when the user expands a details area on the rate plan.

User interaction: Clicking the primary booking button (Book Now / Continue) when a rate plan is selected calls startRoomBooking with roomId and selected ratePlanId along with the current stay parameters from the URL; while the call is in progress, the button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

User interaction: If startRoomBooking returns successfully, the booking action bar triggers a redirect to booking_details.html using the bookingId from startRoomBookingResponse.booking.id as a URL parameter; if it returns an error, an inline error state is shown using the returned message.

User interaction: Clicking the Back to results link navigates back to rooms.html with the current stay parameters and sortBy (if present) preserved in the URL so that the user’s search context is maintained.

User interaction: Clicking thumbnails or navigation controls within the image gallery updates the currently displayed room image using the imageGallery data from getRoomDetails without additional API calls.
User interaction: Expanding or collapsing amenity sections in the amenities list shows or hides the amenity items from amenitySections for better scanability without changing URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary source of cross-page and reload-persistent state on room_detail.html is the URL query string. At minimum, roomId must be present for the page to function. On initial load, the page controller reads roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, and sortBy (if present) from the URL. The controller always calls getRoomDetails(roomId) to fetch the room's descriptive content and policies. If both checkInDate and checkOutDate are present, along with adults and children, it also calls getRoomRates(roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children). If any of these stay-related parameters are missing, getRoomRates is not called until the user provides complete stay details via the stay-parameters bar. When the user changes stay details and submits them, the page updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new values (for example, by writing checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children back into the query string) before re-fetching rates. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL reproduces the same stay configuration. sortBy, if present, is treated as a read-only parameter on this page and is not modified; it is simply carried forward when linking back to rooms.html. Within the page, transient UI state (such as the currently selected ratePlanId, which amenity sections are expanded, and which image is active in the gallery) is maintained in memory in the front-end application state and is not encoded into the URL. startRoomBooking relies exclusively on the current URL parameters (roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) plus the in-memory selected ratePlanId to construct its request. After a successful booking initiation, the booking_details.html page is opened with bookingId passed in the URL (booking_details.html?bookingId={id}), transferring the booking context to the next step in the flow without using localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, parsing of URL parameters, and high-level state for room_detail.html. It ensures that getRoomDetails is always called with the roomId from the URL and that getRoomRates is called only when all required stay parameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) are present. It also provides shared state (room data, rate data, stay parameters, and selection state) to child components.

Displays the key identifying information of the room, including its name, view label, bed label, maximum occupancy, and any core attributes such as room size. It also surfaces concise policy highlights like standard check-in and check-out times using data from getRoomDetails.

Shows the room's photos as a primary image with optional thumbnails or navigation controls. Allows the user to switch between images using data from getRoomDetails.imageGallery. It maintains in-memory state of the currently active image and responds to user navigation without additional API calls.

Displays the detailed room description and any supporting textual information using the Room entity from getRoomDetails. This component is read-only and provides context for the user when evaluating rate plans.

Presents the room's amenities grouped into logical sections (for example, room features, bathroom, technology) using amenitySections from getRoomDetails. Each section can be expanded or collapsed to show or hide its amenities, improving readability for long lists.

Displays general room policies (such as check-in time, check-out time, general cancellation policy, and occupancy policy) from getRoomDetails.policies. It can also be used as a container for rate-specific policy details, such as the cancellationPolicy field from each RatePlan, when invoked by interactions within the rate-plan list.

Allows the user to view and adjust the stay configuration (check-in date, check-out date, number of adults, and number of children). It initializes its fields from the URL parameters if present. On submission, it updates the URL with the new stay values and notifies the page controller so that getRoomRates can be called with the updated parameters.

Lists all available rate options for the current room and stay, using the array returned by getRoomRates. For each rate plan, it displays the ratePlan.name, any inclusion labels (such as breakfast or spa access), totalPrice, averageNightlyPrice, and currency, along with controls to show the nightlyPriceBreakdown. The component manages per-plan expansion state for breakdown details and notifies the page controller when a rate plan is selected.

Maintains the currently selected ratePlanId in memory and exposes it to other components. Ensures that only one rate plan can be selected at a time, updating selection state in response to events from the rate-plan list.

Displays the currently selected rate plan summary (using data from getRoomRates and the selection controller) along with a primary action button that starts the booking. When the user clicks the button, it calls startRoomBooking with roomId from the URL, selected ratePlanId from the selection controller, and stay parameters from the URL. It handles loading, success, and error states and performs the redirect to booking_details.html on success.

Provides a navigational link back to rooms.html while preserving the current stay parameters and any sortBy parameter present in the URL. When activated, it constructs a URL to rooms.html using the current values from the URL state and triggers navigation.

Shows appropriate feedback when rate plans cannot be displayed. If getRoomRates has not yet been called because stay parameters are incomplete, it prompts the user (using non-hardcoded text derived from configuration or localization) to enter dates and guests. If getRoomRates returns an empty array or errors, it displays the error details from the API or a generic fallback, allowing the user to adjust their stay parameters instead of proceeding.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, vertically flowing hero-like top section for the room, followed by a structured content band below.
