Within a centered content container (about 60–70% of viewport width), elements are arranged top-to-bottom as: back-to-results-link → room-header-summary → room-image-gallery → stay-parameters-bar. Below this hero cluster, the layout splits into a two-column content band: the left column holds descriptive content (room-description-block, room-amenities-section, room-policies-section), and the right column holds the transactional content (rate-selection-state, rate-plan-list or rates-empty-or-error-state). The booking-action-bar sits fixed at the bottom of the viewport as a persistent CTA, echoing the original booking widget hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, vertically flowing hero-like top section for the room, followed by a structured content band below. Within a centered content container (about 60–70% of viewport width), elements are arranged top-to-bottom as: back-to-results-link → room-header-summary → room-image-gallery → stay-parameters-bar. Below this hero cluster, the layout splits into a two-column content band: the left column holds descriptive content (room-description-block, room-amenities-section, room-policies-section), and the right column holds the transactional content (rate-selection-state, rate-plan-list or rates-empty-or-error-state). The booking-action-bar sits fixed at the bottom of the viewport as a persistent CTA, echoing the original booking widget hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global/top header (outside these components) occupies roughly the top 10–12% of the viewport. The room hero cluster (summary + gallery + stay-parameters-bar) takes about 35–45% of initial viewport height, giving the room visuals similar prominence to the original bed hero. The content band (description and rates) then fills the remaining page height as users scroll. Horizontally, all content sits within a centered container that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, mapped to an implicit 12-column grid. Within that, the lower content band splits roughly 7/12 width for the left descriptive column and 5/12 for the right rate column, keeping the main transactional elements compact and visually manageable.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density mirrors the mockup’s moderately spacious feel. Major blocks (hero cluster, stay parameters, description, amenities, policies, rate list) are separated by 24–40 px vertical gaps, while internal elements within a block use 16–24 px padding. The stay-parameters-bar and each rate card mirror the mockup’s card and widget density, with substantial internal padding and clear separation between input fields or rate rows. Overall, the layout avoids clutter by allowing generous breathing room around the gallery, the stay-parameters-bar, and the rate-plan-list, while still keeping all booking-relevant controls within easy reach on the first screen.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a Z-pattern inspired by the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left back-to-results-link and room-header-summary, then moves horizontally to any price or quick facts aligned on the right side of that header. It then drops diagonally to the large room-image-gallery centered in the hero area, which acts as the visual anchor. From there, attention moves downward to the stay-parameters-bar, a horizontally oriented control bar akin to the original booking widget. Scrolling slightly, the gaze continues to the left column’s headings (description, amenities) and then diagonally to the right column where the rate-plan-list sits. Finally, once a rate is chosen, focus is pulled to the bottom booking-action-bar, echoing the original BOOK CTA as the end of the flow.

layout.overall_description: The Room Details & Rates page feels like an extension of the warm, boutique hero from the mockup: a full-width dark, cozy background with a centered content column floating in front. Below the site-wide header, a slim back link and refined room title area introduce the room before the eye drops to a generous, edge-to-edge room gallery framed within the central column, echoing the original bed hero. Slightly overlapping or sitting snugly beneath the gallery, a gold-outlined stay-parameters-bar stretches horizontally across the center, with date and guest selectors leading into a dark green action button, visually echoing the booking strip on the home hero. As users scroll, the layout resolves into a balanced two-column band: on the left, textual richness (description, amenities, policies) is structured with serif headings and subtle gold dividers; on the right, a vertical stack of rate cards presents available plans within a trimmed, hotel-card style panel. Throughout, generous vertical spacing and centered alignment keep the page feeling calm and premium. When a rate is chosen, a dark green booking-action-bar pins itself to the bottom of the viewport, mirroring the mockup’s strong booking CTA and completing a deliberate, top-to-bottom booking journey.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-page-controller acts as the structural shell for all room content beneath the global site header. It establishes a centered content container with a max width of approximately 1120–1200 px (about 60–70% of typical desktop viewport width), aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. All room-page components are placed within this container, which has generous top padding (about 64–80 px) below the site header to prevent any visual collision. The vertical sequence within this container is: back-to-results-link at the very top, followed immediately by room-header-summary, then room-image-gallery, then stay-parameters-bar. Below the stay-parameters-bar, the controller defines a lower content band that splits into two columns: a left column occupying roughly 7/12 of the container width for room-description-block, room-amenities-section, and room-policies-section, and a right column occupying roughly 5/12 for rate-selection-state, rate-plan-list or rates-empty-or-error-state. The booking-action-bar is positioned by this controller as a full-width element fixed to the bottom of the viewport, with its inner content aligned to the same central container width, ensuring consistent edges throughout the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the main scrollable layout, breakpoints, and grid without visible styling. It may also manage sticky behavior for components, such as making the stay-parameters-bar stick to the top of the viewport on scroll and ensuring the booking-action-bar remains fixed at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-results-link is positioned at the very top of the room content area, aligned left within the central content container. It sits on its own line, approximately 16–24 px below the bottom edge of the global header and about 8–16 px above the room-header-summary. The link is compact, taking up only as much horizontal space as its text and icon require, and does not span the entire width. It visually anchors the upper left of the Z-pattern and acts like a small breadcrumb, with ample whitespace above and below so it feels unobtrusive and elegant, consistent with the boutique style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in normal document flow, always at the top of the room content and scrolling away with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-header-summary sits directly beneath the back-to-results-link with a vertical gap of about 8–16 px, and spans the full width of the central content container. Its internal layout follows a subtle two-column feel: the left two-thirds of the width contain the room name, a short descriptor, and possibly occupancy cues, while the right one-third aligns pricing or a ‘from’ rate summary. Text is left-aligned overall, but right-side meta information is right-aligned within its area, creating a horizontal spread that echoes the top navigation vs. BOOK link alignment seen in the hero mockup. The header-summary block has around 16–24 px padding at its bottom through spacing before the next component, and may terminate with a thin gold underline or short divider aligned to the left two-thirds, referencing the mockup’s gold tagline lines and adding a refined, premium feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the scrollable page content, forming part of the initial viewport and pushing the subsequent gallery downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-image-gallery is placed immediately below the room-header-summary with a vertical gap of about 24–32 px, occupying the full width of the central content container. It has a generous height, roughly 40–50% of the viewport height on desktop, creating a visual hero that parallels the bed image in the original design. The gallery is horizontally centered and flush with the container edges, with subtle rounded corners and possibly a very soft shadow to feel like a framed window into the room. There is at least 32 px of space below the gallery to the stay-parameters-bar, and the gallery is visually dominant in this top section, serving as the main anchor before the eye drops to the booking controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width media block within the content container, scrolling with the page. On taller screens, it strives to remain mostly visible in the initial viewport alongside the header-summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stay-parameters-bar is positioned directly beneath the room-image-gallery, with a vertical offset of approximately 0–24 px. On larger desktops, it can slightly overlap the bottom of the gallery by about 16–24 px, creating the impression of a floating booking strip, like the booking widget overlay in the hero mockup. The bar is horizontally centered within the content container and spans roughly 80% of the container width (around 50–55% of viewport width), leaving breathing room on both sides. It is styled as a slim, rectangular card with a white or light background, a thin gold border, and subtle shadow, with 16–24 px internal padding. Inside, the layout is horizontal: date range inputs on the left, guest selector in the middle, and a dark green action button (e.g., Update or Check availability) on the right, mirroring the original horizontal booking widget. There is about 32–40 px of vertical space beneath this bar before the next content band begins, ensuring it reads as its own distinct, high-importance strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially appears in normal flow below the gallery, but on scroll it becomes sticky, pinning itself to the top of the viewport just under the global site header. This keeps the stay parameters always accessible while not obscuring content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-description-block begins the lower content band and is anchored in the left column defined by the room-page-controller. It starts roughly 32–40 px below the stay-parameters-bar, aligning vertically with the top of the rate column on the right. The left column occupies around 7/12 of the container width and is left-aligned, with the description spanning nearly the full width of this column. The block typically includes a heading, a short gold divider line or ornament beneath the heading (recalling the hero tagline dividers), and then body text laid out with comfortable line spacing. The description block is allowed to be text-heavy but uses generous vertical spacing (16–24 px between paragraphs) and margin of about 24–32 px at the bottom before the room-amenities-section, maintaining the moderately spacious density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; flows naturally with the page scroll and sets the vertical start point for the descriptive content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-amenities-section sits immediately below the room-description-block within the same left column, separated by about 24–32 px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 7/12 of the container) and is left-aligned. A section heading at the top echoes the typographic hierarchy of the room title but at a smaller scale, possibly with a short gold rule beneath to extend the design DNA. The amenities themselves are organized into 2–3 vertical lists or a multi-column grid, but the entire section remains visually contained within this left slice. There is at least 24 px of padding between amenity groups, and around 24–32 px of margin below before the room-policies-section, keeping the content orderly rather than crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component within the left column, growing vertically as needed, with its bottom edge defining where the policies section begins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-policies-section is the last major block in the left column, placed 24–32 px below the room-amenities-section. It spans the same 7/12 column width and is left-aligned, maintaining consistent edges with description and amenities above. Policies are packaged in an accordion format, with each accordion header styled as a full-width row and separated by 8–16 px vertical gaps, echoing the card rhythm from the rate list on the right. The section heading and any decorative gold dividers sit at the top of this block, visually anchoring it and tying it back to the boutique aesthetic. The policies section may extend quite tall on scroll, but its left column alignment ensures it does not overwhelm the rate content on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the left column, extending the page vertically and scrolling normally. No sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rate-plan-list is anchored at the top of the right column in the lower content band, beginning roughly at the same vertical level as the room-description-block. The right column occupies around 5/12 of the container width and is aligned flush-right within the central content area, creating a clear visual pairing: narrative content on the left, rates on the right. At the very top of this column, the list may begin with a narrow gold-tinted status strip (for availability messaging), spanning the full width of the right column and echoing the hero’s gold availability bar. Below this, individual rate plans are presented as stacked cards, each spanning the entire width of the right column, with 16–24 px vertical spacing between cards. Each card has 16–24 px internal padding and a subtle outline or shadow, creating a controlled, hotel-card feel. The list extends vertically downward, roughly filling 60–70% of the visible right column height in the initial viewport and continuing off-screen as needed. There is at least 32 px of padding below the last card before any page footer content, preserving the airiness of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column and scrolls normally. If very long, the list scrolls as part of the page rather than in its own scroll container, ensuring a simple, linear scroll experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rate-selection-state presents itself as a slim info band directly associated with the rate-plan-list. When inactive, it occupies minimal or no space. Once the user selects a rate, a horizontal strip appears immediately above the first rate card within the right column, spanning the full column width (5/12 of the container). This strip is about 48–64 px tall, with a soft gold or warm neutral background and 12–16 px internal padding. It briefly summarizes the selected rate (for example, Selected: Flexible Rate – Breakfast Included) and may include a subtle icon. This state bar visually bridges the individual rate card selection and the booking-action-bar at the bottom of the screen, reinforcing the user’s current choice. Vertical spacing of about 16 px separates this strip from the rate cards below to maintain clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in place above the rate-plan-list within the right column, shifting the list downward when visible. It scrolls away with the page; it is not sticky, allowing the bottom booking-action-bar to serve as the persistent selection reminder.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-action-bar is a full-width band fixed to the bottom of the viewport, mirroring the strength and clarity of the hero booking widget’s BOOK button. It spans the full browser width horizontally, but its inner content is constrained to the same central container width as the main page (around 1120–1200 px) and centered, so the left and right edges align perfectly with the main content column. The bar height is approximately 72–88 px, with a rich dark green background and a subtle top shadow or border to distinguish it from the content above. Inside, the layout follows a white-on-green, gold-accented scheme: on the left side, a concise summary of the selected rate and stay details; on the right side, a large primary BOOK button framed in gold or filled with a contrasting tone. The bar appears only when a rate has been selected, otherwise remaining hidden so the page feels open. Its presence creates a strong horizontal CTA anchor at the bottom of the Z-pattern, echoing the original site’s emphasis on the BOOK action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the bottom of the viewport with a slight elevation (shadow) above the page content. It stays in place as the user scrolls, ensuring the primary booking action is always within reach once a rate is selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rates-empty-or-error-state occupies the same right-column area as the rate-plan-list, maintaining layout consistency. When there are no available rates or when an error occurs, this component replaces the rate-plan-list within the right 5/12 column. It is centered vertically within the portion of the column visible in the initial viewport where possible (with at least 64 px margin from the stay-parameters-bar above), and horizontally it spans roughly 80–90% of the right column width, leaving soft margins on the sides. Visually, it appears as a compact panel or card with a light background and a thin gold or neutral border, containing an icon, a short message, and possibly a link or button to adjust dates. There is ample internal padding (16–24 px) and generous whitespace around it, emphasizing the state without feeling alarming or cluttered, and preserving the boutique calm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel within the right column that replaces the rate-plan-list in normal flow when active. It scrolls with the page and does not use any sticky or fixed behavior.

name: Compare Rooms
title: Compare Rooms
description: Side-by-side comparison page showing selected rooms with bed type, view, occupancy, amenities, and nightly prices for the chosen dates, allowing users to refine their selection and navigate to full room details to book.

core_features: Load the current comparison set using getRoomComparisonView with optional check-in/check-out dates and guest counts from URL parameters, and render each selected room as a column in a comparison table.

Display structured comparison rows for each room, including name, bed type, view, room size (if available), max occupancy, nightly price (base and/or computed for the selected dates), and amenity lists.
Highlight the room(s) with the largest bed size using a defined bed-type ranking so users can quickly identify the most spacious sleeping option.
Allow users to set an optional nightly price limit (stored in the URL) and visually indicate which rooms fall under that limit based on computedAverageNightlyPrice or basePricePerNight.
Enable users to remove individual rooms from the comparison via removeRoomFromComparison and update the table accordingly.
Enable users to clear the entire comparison set via clearRoomComparison and present an empty-state message with navigation back to the rooms listing.

Provide direct navigation from each compared room (room name and a 'View Room' action) to room_detail.html with all existing context parameters (roomId, checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) preserved in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Compare selected rooms from the Rooms & Suites page:
1. User selects multiple rooms to compare on rooms.html and clicks a 'Compare Rooms' control, which navigates to compare_rooms.html with optional URL parameters checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children.
2. On page load, compare_rooms.html reads these URL parameters and calls getRoomComparisonView(checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children).
3. The page receives the current RoomComparison (roomIds) and the rooms array containing room details, pricing information, and amenity labels.
4. The comparison table renders each room as a column and key attributes as rows, while the context bar shows the selected dates and guest counts.
5. The user scans the table to evaluate bed type, views, occupancy, amenities, and nightly prices for each room.

Workflow 2 – Adjust price limit and identify best options:
1. The user enters or adjusts a maximum nightly price in the price-limit control.
2. The page updates the URL with a maxNightlyRate parameter (without calling any back-end interfaces) and re-parses it to update local state.
3. Using the data already loaded from getRoomComparisonView, the page computes which rooms have computedAverageNightlyPrice (or basePricePerNight when computed is unavailable) less than or equal to maxNightlyRate.
4. The comparison table visually marks rooms within the limit, and a highlights panel summarizes which rooms are under the limit and which has the lowest qualifying price.
5. The user uses these highlights to narrow down their choice and then navigates to the chosen room’s detail page.

Workflow 3 – Remove rooms or clear the comparison:
1. In the comparison table, the user clicks 'Remove' on a specific room column.
2. The page calls removeRoomFromComparison(roomId) for that room.
3. On success, the page calls getRoomComparisonView again with the existing URL parameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) to refresh the comparison data and re-renders the table without the removed room.
4. If the user wants to reset completely, they click a 'Clear all' control.
5. The page calls clearRoomComparison() and receives confirmation that the comparison has been cleared.
6. The comparison table switches to an empty-state component indicating there are no rooms to compare and offering a navigation link back to rooms.html for starting a new comparison.

Workflow 4 – View full details and proceed toward booking:
1. After reviewing the comparison, the user clicks the room name or a 'View Room' action for their preferred room.
2. The page performs client-side navigation to room_detail.html with URL parameters: roomId (from the selected room), checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children taken from the current comparison context (derived from the initial URL parameters).
3. The user lands on room_detail.html with the same date and occupancy context, where the booking flow continues (e.g., selecting a rate plan and confirming a booking).
interactions: On initial page load or when the URL changes, the page reads checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children from the query string and calls getRoomComparisonView with these values to populate the comparison table.

When the user changes the optional nightly price limit in the price-limit form and submits it, the page updates or adds a maxNightlyRate parameter to the URL, re-derives local state from this parameter, and recomputes which rooms are considered inside or outside the price limit using the already loaded pricing fields.

When the user clicks 'Remove' on a room column, the page calls removeRoomFromComparison(roomId), then calls getRoomComparisonView again with the current URL parameters to re-render the table without that room.

When the user clicks 'Clear all', the page calls clearRoomComparison(), then either calls getRoomComparisonView with the same URL parameters (to confirm an empty comparison) or directly switches to rendering the empty-state component while keeping the URL parameters unchanged.

When the user edits date or guest fields in a context form (if present) and submits, the page constructs a new URL for compare_rooms.html with updated checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and/or children, then navigates to that URL, triggering a new getRoomComparisonView call on reload.

When the user clicks on a room name or 'View Room' button, the page navigates to room_detail.html with URL parameters roomId (from the clicked room) and the current context parameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) taken from the URL or derived comparison context.

When getRoomComparisonView returns no rooms (empty rooms array or comparison.roomIds length is zero), the main comparison table is hidden, and an empty-state component is displayed, offering a link back to rooms.html for the user to select rooms again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is derived from URL query parameters and the responses from the assigned interfaces. On every load, compare_rooms.html parses the query string for checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, and children. These parameters are passed directly into getRoomComparisonView(checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children). The response establishes the comparison state: the RoomComparison object (which includes the current set of roomIds) and the rooms array representing each column in the table. The same URL parameters are reused whenever the page needs to refresh data, such as immediately after removeRoomFromComparison or clearRoomComparison calls. An additional optional URL parameter, maxNightlyRate, is managed entirely client-side for filtering/highlighting: if present, it is parsed at load and used to determine which rooms are below or above that limit using their computedAverageNightlyPrice (falling back to basePricePerNight). Changing the price limit updates this parameter and triggers recomputation but does not change any back-end calls. The page does not use localStorage; all sharable and restorable state relevant to dates, guests, and price limit is encoded in the URL, while the actual set of compared rooms is controlled by the back-end via RoomComparison and surfaced through the getRoomComparisonView, removeRoomFromComparison, and clearRoomComparison interfaces.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and refreshes. On page load (and on relevant URL changes), parses URL parameters (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children, maxNightlyRate), calls getRoomComparisonView with the date and guest parameters, and injects the returned comparison and rooms data into downstream components. It also orchestrates re-calls to getRoomComparisonView after removeRoomFromComparison or clearRoomComparison.

Displays the current comparison context: check-in date, check-out date, number of adults, and number of children, derived from URL parameters. Optionally provides small input fields to adjust dates and guest counts. When the user submits changes, the component updates the URL query parameters and navigates to the same page, causing a fresh call to getRoomComparisonView with the updated context.

Lets the user specify an optional maximum nightly rate used only for client-side highlighting. On initialization, the component reads the maxNightlyRate value from the URL (if present) and populates an input field. When the user submits the form, it updates the maxNightlyRate query parameter in the URL and triggers a recomputation in the highlights and table components to mark which rooms are under or over this limit based on computedAverageNightlyPrice or basePricePerNight.

Summarizes key insights from the current comparison: identifies the room or rooms with the largest bed size by applying a predefined ordering over Room.bedType values, identifies the room with the lowest nightly price overall, and identifies the lowest-priced room(s) within the user’s maxNightlyRate (if set). It uses the already loaded rooms array and the maxNightlyRate value derived from the URL. It does not call any interfaces itself, but updates reactively when the rooms data or maxNightlyRate changes.

Renders the side-by-side comparison of rooms, with each room as a column. Rows include: room name, bed type (using bedLabel), view (viewLabel), room size (roomSizeSqM where available), max occupancy (adults and children), pricing (basePricePerNight and/or computedAverageNightlyPrice plus currency and cheapestRatePlanName if provided), and amenity lists (amenityLabels). It visually marks the room(s) with the largest bed type and those under the current maxNightlyRate. Each column also includes a 'Remove' control that removes that room from comparison, and a 'View Room' action and clickable room name that navigate to room_detail.html including the current context parameters.

Encapsulates the logic for removing a single room from the comparison. When invoked with a roomId (typically from the room-comparison-table), it calls removeRoomFromComparison(roomId). After a successful response, it triggers the page-controller to call getRoomComparisonView again with the existing context parameters, so all components re-render with the updated RoomComparison and rooms data.

Provides a 'Clear all' button for clearing the entire comparison and optionally a link back to the rooms listing page. When the button is clicked, it calls clearRoomComparison(). On success, it either triggers a new getRoomComparisonView call (to retrieve the now-empty comparison) or directly signals the UI to show the empty-comparison-state component while retaining existing URL parameters for dates and guests.

Displays when the RoomComparison has no roomIds or when the rooms array from getRoomComparisonView is empty. It explains that there are no rooms to compare and offers a navigation link back to rooms.html where the user can select rooms and start a new comparison. It is conditionally rendered based on the comparison data and does not make any interface calls.

Builds the correct outgoing links to room_detail.html for each room in the comparison table. For each room, it constructs a URL containing roomId from room.id and reuses the current comparison context values (checkInDate, checkOutDate, adults, children) from the page URL. It is invoked when the user clicks 'View Room' or a room name and performs client-side navigation without calling any back-end interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: Page content is organized in a single, top-to-bottom column that mirrors the hero’s linear stack: controller strip → contextual/status bar → filter strip → highlight summary → main comparison content → action bar/navigation. All functional elements sit inside a centered content container (about 60–70% of the viewport width) with the wider background (dark green or photography) framing them, just like the hero’s centered booking widget within a full-bleed image.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Page content is organized in a single, top-to-bottom column that mirrors the hero’s linear stack: controller strip → contextual/status bar → filter strip → highlight summary → main comparison content → action bar/navigation. All functional elements sit inside a centered content container (about 60–70% of the viewport width) with the wider background (dark green or photography) framing them, just like the hero’s centered booking widget within a full-bleed image.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the initial viewport is reserved for the control and context cluster (init controller, context bar, filter, highlights) with generous vertical spacing between each block (24–32px). The main comparison table occupies the central bulk of the page (roughly 45–55% of the scrollable height on first view), spanning nearly the full width of the content column. Below that, the clear-actions bar and room-detail navigation afford a final 15–20% as a bottom band, echoing how the booking strip sits toward the lower hero area. Background margins on the left and right ensure the content column feels centered and framed, not edge-to-edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density matches the mockup’s controlled richness: multiple utilities are visible at once but each sits in its own padded card or bar with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px gaps between blocks. The context bar and action bars are slim yet prominent strips (similar to the gold status bar), while the comparison table is information-dense but visually segmented using strong column headers, clear row groupings, and ample cell padding so it feels premium rather than cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a Z-pattern adapted for a data-heavy page. The eye starts at the top-left with the comparison-init controls, sweeps right across any inline utility actions. It then drops diagonally to the center-aligned context/status bar and filters, which act as the main anchor much like the tagline and logo cluster. From there, the gaze moves down into the comparison highlights panel and finally descends to the horizontally structured comparison table, where each room column has a clear top anchor (room name, price, and remove control). Final calls-to-action (clear comparison, go back, or view room details) sit at the bottom bar, echoing the hero’s booking button as the last step in the flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s persistent header, the Compare Rooms page opens with a centered content column sitting over a warm, subtly textured background that hints at the brand’s boutique aesthetic. At the very top of this column, a slim, understated control row provides comparison-level actions, aligned left, functioning like a quiet pre-header. Directly beneath it, a full-width gold-accented context bar spans the content column, clearly stating the chosen dates, number of guests, and number of rooms being compared—visually reminiscent of the hero’s availability status strip. A compact, card-like price filter sits below, stretching across about two-thirds of the column width and centered, much like the hero’s booking widget: white or very light, with a fine gold outline and dark green accents. Under that, a comparison highlights panel presents key takeaways side-by-side in a low-profile card, acting as a visual bridge into the main content. The room comparison table then dominates the central part of the page: a horizontally structured matrix where each room occupies a vertical column, with prominent top headers containing room name, representative price, and a small remove control. The table content is dense but well-spaced, with generous cell padding and clear dividers, evoking the same disciplined richness as the booking widget. As users scroll, the context bar remains pinned just under the global header, and a slim actions bar appears near the bottom, providing ‘Clear comparison’ and navigation back to browsing. If no rooms are selected, a centered empty state card replaces the table area, aligning with the main column and floating in the middle of the viewport with ample breathing room. Throughout, gold rules, dividers, and serif headings echo the original hero’s Parisian, boutique-hotel vibe, ensuring the compare page feels like a natural extension of the core design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-init controller sits at the very top of the page’s main content column, directly below the global site header and aligned flush with the left edge of the content container. It appears as a low-height horizontal strip or inline row (about 40–56px tall) spanning roughly 70–80% of the content column width, starting from the left and leaving a small margin of whitespace on the right for visual lightness. On the left, it may host breadcrumbs or a ‘Back to rooms’ link and a small label like ‘Compare rooms’, styled in the brand’s serif or small caps; on the far right of this same line, compact inline controls (like ‘Change dates’) sit, forming the first horizontal leg of the Z-pattern. Vertical spacing above it is minimal (12–16px below the main header), but there’s a clear 24px gap below it before the context bar, ensuring it reads as a discrete meta-control tier rather than part of the main status strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, scrolling away with the page while other elements (like the context bar) become sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-context bar is positioned directly below the init controller, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the centered content column (around 60–70% of the viewport), with slightly rounded corners and a gold or warm-metallic background akin to the hero’s availability status bar. Internally it has 16–20px of padding on all sides. Text is left-aligned, starting near the left edge with key info—selected dates, length of stay, guest count, and count of rooms being compared—laid out in a single line on desktop or wrapped neatly into two lines on smaller widths. On the right side of the bar, secondary controls like ‘Adjust dates’ or a compact pill button can sit, echoing how navigation elements align right in the header. The bar’s vertical height is moderate (about 56–72px total), making it a clear, central anchor without feeling bulky. As users scroll, this bar pins itself just below the global navigation, preserving a 8–12px gap, so that context is always visible while the table content moves beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top of the viewport once scrolled into position, remaining fixed just below the global header while other content scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-limit filter sits directly below the context bar, within the same centered content column, separated by a comfortable 24–32px of vertical space. It appears as a horizontal, card-like filter strip, about 60–70% of the content column width (roughly 40–50% of total viewport width) and centered to reinforce the hero-derived composition. The card uses a light background (off-white) with a subtle gold outline and 16–24px internal padding. Filter controls (such as a price range slider and max-price input) are arranged left-to-right, with labels stacked above controls on narrow widths and inline on larger desktop, maintaining a low profile height (around 64–80px). The left half generally holds the slider or primary price selector, while the right half may host a ‘Reset’ text link and/or a small dark green ‘Apply’ button aligned to the right edge. Ample whitespace above and below maintains breathing room so this component reads as a refined utility layer sitting between status and content, much like the booking widget below the status bar in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow; its horizontal layout gracefully collapses into stacked rows on narrower screens while staying centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-highlights panel is positioned beneath the price-limit filter, with 24–32px of vertical separation to maintain the tiered rhythm. It spans nearly the full content column width (about 90–100% of that centered column), and appears as a low, horizontally oriented card 120–180px tall with a light background and subtle gold top border or divider to echo the mockup’s gold accents. Inside, 16–24px of padding creates a comfortable frame for a short, bold heading on the left (e.g., ‘At a glance’) and 2–4 key highlight items distributed across the width. These highlights might be styled as small vertical stacks or chips (icon + label + short text), aligned in columns that mirror the columns of the comparison table below, helping the eye anticipate the matrix. The overall feel is informational but not heavy, acting as a gentle visual bridge between the filters and the dense table. There is another 32–40px gap beneath this panel before the top of the comparison table to clearly delineate the shift from summary to full detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main scroll; it does not stick, but its alignment and width visually prime the user for the column structure to follow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-comparison table is the dominant block in the mid-section of the page, starting about 32–40px below the highlights panel to signal a major content tier. It occupies the full width of the content column (60–70% of the viewport on desktop), extending edge-to-edge within that container, with 24px of padding on the left and right inside the table wrapper so content does not feel cramped against borders. Each room is represented as a vertical column, with the leftmost column reserved for row labels (e.g., Bed type, View, Occupancy, Amenities, Nightly price for selected dates). The table’s header row is visually strong: a slightly taller band (96–128px high) where each room column’s top cell contains the room name in a prominent serif type, a thumbnail or small image, and the leading price, all centered within that column. Beneath that, attribute rows are grouped with subtle horizontal separators and generous 16–20px vertical padding per cell, ensuring the information density remains comfortable. The table itself scrolls vertically within the page; on narrower screens it may also allow horizontal scrolling, but on desktop it fits within the center column without extending to full-screen width. The overall effect is a well-balanced grid, reminiscent of the structured booking widget: functional, central, and visually grounded by consistent gold or dark green cell borders and dividers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll, with the header row remaining visible only until it scrolls off-screen; inside each header cell, other components like remove buttons and room-detail navigation are positioned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-room handler is embedded within each room’s column header inside the room-comparison table. In each column’s top cell, it is aligned to the top-right corner, offset inward by about 12–16px from the right edge and 8–12px from the top to avoid visual collision with the column border. It appears as a small, pill-shaped chip or icon button (such as an ‘×’ or ‘Remove’ label), using a dark green outline or text against a light background so it is visible but does not overpower the room name. The handler sits on the same visual plane as the room’s title and price, allowing users following the Z-pattern across the header row to easily see and use it. Extra padding in the header cell ensures that even with the remove handler present, the content feels spacious and not cramped. When hovering on desktop, the handler becomes slightly more prominent (e.g., stronger gold outline) to indicate actionability, but spatially it always occupies that small top-right corner territory within each column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative within the table header cell; it scrolls with the table and is not sticky or floating independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The clear-comparison-actions component forms a horizontal actions bar positioned immediately below the room-comparison table wrapper. It is separated from the table by 24–32px of whitespace to distinguish it as a footer-level control, and it spans the full width of the content column. Visually, it is a slim strip (56–72px tall) with a light background, a fine top border or gold rule, and 16–20px internal padding. Within the bar, primary actions are distributed along a horizontal line: on the left, a text link or subtle button labeled ‘Clear comparison’ (possibly outlined in dark green) aligns to the left margin; on the right, a more pronounced button like ‘Back to all rooms’ or ‘Continue booking’ aligns to the right, echoing the header’s right-aligned ‘BOOK’ link. When the page is scrolled so that the table extends beyond the viewport, this bar can gently dock itself to the bottom of the viewport, ensuring actions remain accessible; it retains a small 8–12px margin above the actual viewport bottom to maintain a feeling of lightness and avoid covering content excessively.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Becomes sticky at the bottom of the viewport after the user has scrolled past its original position, remaining fixed until the user scrolls back above it; otherwise, it sits statically just below the table.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-comparison-state replaces the entire region where the highlights panel and table would normally appear when there are no rooms selected. It is centered within the content column both horizontally and, as much as possible, vertically within the first viewport, beginning roughly 64–80px below the sticky context bar to preserve breathing room. The empty state appears as a refined, card-like panel (around 50–60% of viewport width and 240–320px tall) with a light background, soft shadow, and a thin gold border, echoing the look of the booking widget in the hero. Inside, content is center-aligned: an illustrative icon or monoline badge at the top, then a bold heading (‘No rooms added to comparison’) and a short explanatory line, followed by a single primary CTA button in dark green (‘Browse rooms’) centered at the bottom. Generous internal padding (24–32px) and ample whitespace around the card ensure it feels calm and inviting, making the absence of data feel intentional rather than empty. When this state is active, the clear-comparison-actions bar is either hidden or reduced to a minimal, non-distracting line to keep focus on the call to start comparing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow; it occupies the main content area beneath the context bar and moves with the scroll, but because it is compact and centered, it typically remains fully visible without requiring scrolling on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-detail-navigation is integrated into each room column within the room-comparison table, appearing as a compact CTA area either directly below the header block or as the final row in each column. On desktop, the primary placement is just under the header: a small, dark green ‘View details’ button (or text link with an arrow) is center-aligned in the column, sitting 16–24px beneath the room name and price, occupying roughly 60–70% of the column width to leave generous side margins. This alignment creates a strong vertical anchor at the top of each column, guiding the eye from the room name down to the action before scanning detailed attributes. Optionally, a secondary placement can mirror this CTA in the last row, aligned similarly, making it easy for users to jump into full details after comparing. The buttons are consistent in height with other CTAs on the site (around 40–48px) and use the same padding and typography as the hero’s ‘BOOK’ button, reinforcing the site’s primary conversion path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative within each room column; it scrolls with the table content and does not remain fixed, ensuring it is discovered naturally as users scan each room’s information.

name: Booking Details
title: Booking Details
description: Page for reviewing a started room or package booking, collecting guest contact details, and moving the booking to the payment step.

core_features: Load and display booking details (room or offer, rate plan, dates, guests, and price breakdown) using getBookingDetails based on the bookingId URL parameter.
Show a structured summary of the selected room or package, including associated rate plan information and applied offer if present.
Display a price breakdown list and booking policies (cancellation and payment) from the getBookingDetails response.
Provide a guest details form with required fields (first name, last name, email, phone) and optional fields (arrival time, special requests) bound to the Booking model.
Update the booking’s guest information via updateBookingGuestDetails when the guest details form is submitted or when proceeding to payment.
Validate mandatory guest fields on the client before calling updateBookingGuestDetails and show server-side validation or update errors when returned.
Move the booking status from 'initiated' to 'pending_payment' by calling proceedBookingToPayment after guest details are successfully saved.
Navigate to checkout.html with bookingId and checkoutType=booking when proceedBookingToPayment indicates success and redirectToCheckout is true.
Support deep-linking to resume an existing booking by using the bookingId in the URL to reload and repopulate all booking and guest details.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Start booking details from room or offer flow: 1) User selects a room or offer and starts a booking on room_detail.html or offer_detail.html; 2) Those pages create a Booking and navigate to booking_details.html?bookingId={created_booking_id}; 3) Booking Details page reads bookingId from the URL and calls getBookingDetails; 4) Page displays booking summary, price breakdown, and guest details form populated from the returned Booking; 5) User completes or edits guest details; 6) User clicks the 'Continue to Payment' action; 7) Page validates required fields and calls updateBookingGuestDetails; 8) On successful update, page calls proceedBookingToPayment; 9) If proceedBookingToPayment indicates success and redirectToCheckout, page redirects to checkout.html?bookingId={booking.id}&checkoutType=booking.

Workflow 2 – Resume an in-progress booking via deep link: 1) User opens or returns to booking_details.html?bookingId={existing_booking_id} from an email or saved link; 2) Page reads bookingId from URL and calls getBookingDetails; 3) If booking is in a state that still allows editing details (e.g., 'initiated'), the guest details form is populated with existing guest data; 4) User reviews or updates their information and any special requests; 5) User submits the form or clicks 'Continue to Payment'; 6) Page calls updateBookingGuestDetails with the latest values; 7) On success, page calls proceedBookingToPayment; 8) If successful, the user is navigated to checkout.html with bookingId and checkoutType=booking; 9) If getBookingDetails fails or returns a booking that cannot proceed to payment (for example, already 'confirmed' or 'cancelled'), the page displays an appropriate error or informational message and does not show the payment progression action.

Workflow 3 – Edit guest details without immediately proceeding to payment: 1) User is on booking_details.html with a valid bookingId; 2) User updates one or more guest detail fields; 3) User clicks a dedicated 'Save Details' action in the form; 4) Page validates required fields and calls updateBookingGuestDetails; 5) On success, updated Booking data returned from the interface is used to refresh the on-page booking state; 6) A non-intrusive confirmation message is shown that details were saved; 7) User may leave the page and later return via the same bookingId URL, and getBookingDetails will reflect the saved guest details.

Workflow 4 – Handling errors or invalid bookingId: 1) User opens booking_details.html without a bookingId query parameter or with an invalid one; 2) Page initialization detects missing or malformed bookingId and displays a blocking error message instructing the user to return to room_detail.html or offer_detail.html to restart the booking; 3) If bookingId is present but getBookingDetails fails (for example, returns an error or no booking), the page shows an error state while suppressing the editable form and payment progression actions; 4) If updateBookingGuestDetails or proceedBookingToPayment return errors, an inline error message is displayed near the relevant action, and no navigation to checkout.html occurs until the call succeeds.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the bookingId value from the URL query string and triggers the data-loading component that calls getBookingDetails(bookingId).

If getBookingDetails succeeds, the Booking, Room, RatePlan, Offer, priceBreakdown, and policies objects are stored in page state and passed to summary, breakdown, and policy components for display, as well as to the guest details form for initial field values.

If getBookingDetails returns an error or an unusable booking (e.g., missing, invalid status), the page state switches to an error mode, hiding interactive components and showing an error notification component.

When the user changes any guest detail field (first name, last name, email, phone, arrival time, special requests) in the guest details form, the component updates its internal form state, separate from the stored Booking object, until a save or proceed action is triggered.

When the user submits the guest details form using a 'Save Details' action, the form performs client-side validation for required fields and then calls updateBookingGuestDetails with bookingId from URL and the current form field values.
If updateBookingGuestDetails returns a successful response, the returned Booking object replaces the current booking state, and a confirmation notification is shown to the user.

If updateBookingGuestDetails returns an error (for example, missing required server-side validation or invalid contact formats), the guest details form component displays inline error messages and does not update the booking state.

When the user clicks the 'Continue to Payment' action, the page first validates that all required guest details are present; if validation passes, it calls updateBookingGuestDetails with the latest form values to ensure the Booking is up to date.
Only after a successful updateBookingGuestDetails call triggered by 'Continue to Payment' does the page call proceedBookingToPayment with the same bookingId.

If proceedBookingToPayment returns success and redirectToCheckout is true, the page navigates the browser to checkout.html, appending bookingId from the returned Booking object and checkoutType=booking as URL parameters.

If proceedBookingToPayment returns an error or indicates that redirectToCheckout is false, the page shows an error message indicating that progression to payment is currently not possible, and the user remains on the Booking Details page.

If the user edits the guest details after they have already saved them, the form allows further changes and subsequent calls to updateBookingGuestDetails or proceedBookingToPayment, while always using the bookingId from the URL and the most recent form state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the bookingId URL query parameter on booking_details.html. On initialization, the page reads bookingId from the URL. If bookingId is missing or empty, the page enters a 'missing-booking' error state and does not attempt to call getBookingDetails. If bookingId is present, the page triggers getBookingDetails(bookingId). While the call is in progress, the page is in a 'loading-booking' state, during which summary and form components display loading states and user actions that depend on booking data are disabled. When getBookingDetails resolves successfully, the page transitions to a 'booking-loaded' state, storing the returned Booking, Room, RatePlan, Offer, priceBreakdown, and policies in in-memory page state. These values are then bound as read-only data to the summary, price breakdown, and policies components, and as initial editable values in the guest details form component. The Booking.status value from the response may influence which buttons are enabled; for example, if status indicates that payment is not applicable, the 'Continue to Payment' action is disabled. The guest details form maintains its own local form state derived from booking.guestFirstName, booking.guestLastName, booking.guestEmail, booking.guestPhone, booking.arrivalTime, and booking.specialRequests. When the user submits the form or proceeds to payment, the form validates required fields and calls updateBookingGuestDetails with bookingId (always sourced from the URL) and the current form values. On success, the returned Booking object replaces the booking state, which in turn updates any bound components. During the call to updateBookingGuestDetails, the form enters a 'saving' state, disabling duplicate submissions. On 'Continue to Payment', once updateBookingGuestDetails succeeds, the page calls proceedBookingToPayment(bookingId). While proceedBookingToPayment is in progress, the 'Continue to Payment' action remains disabled to prevent repeated calls. If proceedBookingToPayment returns success and redirectToCheckout is true, the page constructs a navigation URL to checkout.html that includes the current bookingId and checkoutType=booking as query parameters and redirects the browser; no localStorage or other persistent client storage is used. If proceedBookingToPayment fails or redirectToCheckout is false, the page stays on the 'booking-loaded' state but sets an error flag that is surfaced via an error notification component. At any time, reloading booking_details.html with the same bookingId will repeat this flow and refetch all booking data fresh from getBookingDetails.

components.functionality: Reads the bookingId from the page URL, calls getBookingDetails with that ID, and manages the page-level loading, success, and error states for the booking data.

Displays a non-editable summary of the booking details returned by getBookingDetails, including room or package name, associated rate plan, check-in and check-out dates, number of nights, number of adults and children, and booking status.
Shows a line-by-line priceBreakdown from getBookingDetails along with the Booking.totalPrice and currency, allowing users to review how the total was calculated.
Displays the cancellationPolicy and paymentPolicy returned by getBookingDetails so that the guest can review important conditions before proceeding to payment.

Collects and edits guest contact information for the booking, including required fields (guestFirstName, guestLastName, guestEmail, guestPhone) and optional fields (arrivalTime, specialRequests). Pre-populates values from the Booking object and sends updates through updateBookingGuestDetails.
Provides an explicit action for users to save guest detail changes without proceeding to payment. Triggers validation in the guest-details-form and calls updateBookingGuestDetails when valid.

Main progression action that validates guest details, calls updateBookingGuestDetails to persist them, then calls proceedBookingToPayment. On success and when redirectToCheckout is true, redirects the browser to checkout.html with bookingId and checkoutType=booking in the URL.

Displays error messages related to loading booking details, saving guest information, or proceeding to payment. It subscribes to error states set by data-loader, guest-details-form, and continue-to-payment-button components.

Evaluates the Booking.status returned from getBookingDetails and determines whether certain actions (such as editing details or proceeding to payment) are allowed, disabling or hiding actions when the booking is not in an editable or payable state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The booking details page keeps a single, vertically flowing main content column centered on the page, echoing the hero’s linear stack. Within that, the top section is a two-panel band (booking summary on the left, price breakdown on the right), acting like the central logo + booking widget cluster from the hero. Below, a slim horizontal policies strip functions similarly to the gold status bar, and the guest details form with its button row plays the role of the main booking widget: a structured, horizontally aligned action area near the bottom of the content column. Error/status overlays sit above or over this column, not breaking the overall vertical rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The booking details page keeps a single, vertically flowing main content column centered on the page, echoing the hero’s linear stack. Within that, the top section is a two-panel band (booking summary on the left, price breakdown on the right), acting like the central logo + booking widget cluster from the hero. Below, a slim horizontal policies strip functions similarly to the gold status bar, and the guest details form with its button row plays the role of the main booking widget: a structured, horizontally aligned action area near the bottom of the content column. Error/status overlays sit above or over this column, not breaking the overall vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header/hero still occupies roughly the top 12–15% of the viewport. The booking details content is placed below, in a centered column occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width. Within that column, the summary + price band uses the upper 25–30% of the content zone, the policies strip a slim band of ~5–10%, and the guest details form plus action buttons occupy the remaining 60–70%. Major vertical separations between bands follow the mockup’s 24–40px rhythm, while each card (summary, price, form) has 16–24px internal padding. Overlays (loader, guard) cover the full height of this content area when active, echoing the way the booking widget sits over the hero image.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is medium and controlled: each main block (notification, summary/price band, policies strip, form, buttons) is visually distinct with generous breathing room. Cards use 24px-ish padding, and vertical gaps between blocks sit around 24–32px. The form fields are arranged in a compact but not cramped two-column layout, while text-heavy sections like policies are constrained in height with scroll or collapsible sections to avoid feeling cluttered. The net effect is rich and inviting but not overwhelming, consistent with a boutique-hotel feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left of the main content column (notification or booking summary), then moves horizontally to the price breakdown on the right, mirroring the header’s left-logo/right-nav pattern. From there, it drops diagonally to the centrally aligned policies strip, then flows downward through the guest details form. The visual journey ends at the lower-right with the primary 'Continue to Payment' button, echoing the hero’s progression down to the booking CTA. Sticky or at least visually persistent elements like the price breakdown help maintain peripheral awareness of cost as users scan and complete the form.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark green global header and hero band, the booking details content appears as a tall, elegant card-like column centered on the page, sitting over a subtly muted continuation of the warm interior background. This central column spans about two-thirds of the viewport width and is vertically offset from the header by a generous margin so it feels anchored but not cramped. At the very top of this column, a slim notification band appears when needed; otherwise, the first visible layer is a two-panel strip where the booking summary occupies the left two-thirds and a more compact, gold-accented price breakdown card sits on the right. Together they mirror the hero’s central logo and booking elements, giving an immediate, reassuring overview of the stay. Just below them stretches a full-width policies strip, visually similar to the gold status bar in the hero: low height, horizontally oriented, and lightly framed in gold so it reads as important but not dominant. Beneath that, a spacious guest details form fills the column, arranged in a two-column field layout with plenty of breathing room and warm white or cream backgrounds. At the bottom-right edge of this form, a prominent dark green 'Continue to Payment' button anchors the user’s next step, with a subtler 'Save Details' action to its left. When data is loading or the booking is invalid, a semi-opaque overlay blankets this central column and presents a centered message or spinner, momentarily becoming the main focus without hiding the overall boutique aesthetic. The whole composition feels vertically tiered and calm, guiding the user from overview to details to decisive action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking details loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire main content column (from just below the header down to the bottom of the booking details section), but does not obscure the global header. It uses the same centered content width as the rest of the page, with a slightly darkened veil in the brand’s deep green, allowing the underlying summary, price, and form cards to remain faintly visible underneath. In the exact center of this overlay, a compact circular loader or subtle animation sits, approximately 64px–96px tall, aligned perfectly to the center of the visible viewport area over the booking column. There is generous empty space around the loader to keep it feeling calm and premium, matching the controlled density of the main hero. When active, it visually floats above all other components, slightly softening the background content while signaling that user interaction is temporarily paused.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed-position overlay within the booking content region: it anchors to the boundaries of the central content column, covers the full height of that column regardless of scroll (locking the scroll position), and sits on the highest z-index above all cards and buttons until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking summary panel is the first substantive block of the booking details content, positioned near the top-left of the central column, roughly 48–64px below the global header or any page title. It occupies about 60–65% of the main column’s width (approximately 7 of 12 grid columns on desktop) and aligns flush with the left edge of the content column. Visually it appears as a warm white or cream card with rounded corners, framed subtly by a soft shadow and possibly a thin gold accent line at the top, echoing the premium badge styling. Internally, it has 24px padding on all sides, with its heading aligned to the top-left and key booking details (room type, dates, number of guests) arranged in a vertically stacked, well-spaced list. The right edge of this panel aligns horizontally with the left edge of the price breakdown panel, creating a cohesive two-panel band. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of this summary panel and the policies strip below, maintaining the mockup’s tiered vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the main content column; it participates in a two-column band alongside the price breakdown and resizes responsively to stack above the price breakdown on narrower viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price breakdown panel sits directly to the right of the booking summary panel, sharing the same top baseline so that both cards form a unified horizontal band at the top of the booking details area. It occupies the remaining 35–40% of the main content column’s width (around 5 of 12 grid columns), aligning its right edge with the right boundary of the central column. Visually, it is slightly more compact but more concentrated: a card with similar warm background and 24px padding, distinguished by a slightly stronger gold border or top rule and, optionally, a faint background tint to hint at its financial importance. The internal layout stacks key price elements top-to-bottom (nightly rate, taxes, total) with tight but readable spacing, so the card feels dense yet orderly. Its height generally matches or slightly exceeds that of the summary panel depending on the amount of detail. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of this panel and the policies strip that follows, mirroring the spacing used under the summary panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits as a static right-hand card within the two-panel band, and on taller pages it may adopt a sticky behavior: remaining pinned near the top-right of the viewport within the bounds of the main content column while the user scrolls through the guest details form, ensuring the price is always in peripheral view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies viewer is positioned directly beneath the combined booking summary and price breakdown band, spanning the full width of the central content column. It is set about 24–32px below the lower edges of both top cards, acting as a visual bridge between the overview information and the interactive form. Horizontally, it stretches from the left edge of the booking summary to the right edge of the price panel, reading as a single, low-profile strip. Stylistically, it echoes the hero’s gold status bar: a relatively shallow horizontal bar with 16–24px internal padding, a refined gold border or background tint, and concise text about cancellation, check-in time, or other key policies, possibly with a 'View full policies' link aligned to the right. The bar is not as tall as a full card, so it feels like a status indicator rather than a heavy content block. A vertical gap of about 24px is maintained below the policies strip before the guest details form begins, keeping the hierarchy clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width strip within the central column; it remains in the normal document flow, expanding vertically only if content length increases or if a collapsible "show more" section is opened.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest details form is the largest single block on the page and sits immediately below the policies viewer, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the entire width of the main content column, aligning with both the left edge of the summary panel and the right edge of the price breakdown panel. It appears as a substantial card with a warm white or cream background, rounded corners, and a subtle drop shadow, visually reminiscent of the booking widget but extended vertically. Inside, there is generous 24–32px padding on all sides. The form fields are arranged in a two-column grid on desktop (roughly 6 of 12 grid columns each), with related fields grouped into sections (e.g., contact info, address, special requests) that are separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. Labels are left-aligned, with fields sitting directly beneath or beside them, and there is comfortable vertical spacing (8–12px) between individual fields to maintain the boutique, uncluttered feel. The bottom of the form leaves an extra 24–32px clear area above the action buttons to create a visual pause before the primary CTA row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width card within the content column; it grows vertically with form complexity and collapses vertically on smaller viewports to a single-column layout while maintaining the same alignment within the overall page grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save details button is placed within the action row at the bottom of the guest details form, aligning horizontally with the continue-to-payment button but positioned to its left. This action row sits inside the form card, inset from the card’s edges by the same 24–32px internal padding. The row itself is anchored to the bottom-right of the form: buttons are right-aligned within a full-width horizontal strip that spans the card. The save button is visually lighter and secondary in style, likely with a simple gold-outline or a muted background and dark green text. It has comfortable padding (around 8–12px vertically and 16–24px horizontally) and sits 16–24px to the left of the primary continue button, creating a clear but not excessive gap. Above the button row, there is a consistent 24–32px vertical space separating it from the last form field, preserving the form’s airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of the anchored bottom action bar of the form card. On wide screens it stays to the immediate left of the primary CTA; on narrow screens it may stack vertically above the continue button while remaining right-aligned or center-aligned within the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue-to-payment button is the visual endpoint of the page’s flow, positioned at the far right of the action row at the bottom of the guest details form. It is right-aligned within the full width of the form card, sitting flush with the card’s right internal padding. This button is slightly taller and more visually substantial than the save button, with a filled dark green background, contrasting light text, and possibly subtle gold detailing (like a fine border or hover effect) to reinforce its primary status. It uses generous padding—approximately 12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally—making it clearly tappable and in line with the mockup’s rich but not oversized controls. The area directly around the button is intentionally uncluttered: there is at least 24px of whitespace above the button row and sufficient breathing room below before the end of the card, ensuring the CTA stands out without feeling aggressive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form’s bottom action bar on desktop; on smaller devices it can become fixed or sticky near the bottom of the viewport while the user scrolls the last part of the form, maintaining its role as the final step trigger without breaking alignment in the desktop view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking error notification appears at the very top of the main booking details column, directly beneath the global header and above the booking summary panel. It spans the full width of the central content column, forming a slim horizontal band that immediately catches attention without overpowering the layout. The bar is about the height of a compact card (roughly 48–72px tall depending on message length) with 16–24px internal padding and rounded corners that echo the other cards. Its background is a softly tinted warm red or deep tone that still harmonizes with the palette, accented by a thin gold border or icon to keep the premium feel. The error text is left-aligned, while an icon or close button sits on the far right, matching the header’s left-to-right visual language. When visible, this band pushes all subsequent content (summary, price, policies, form) downward, preserving the vertical rhythm with a small gap (around 16px) between the bottom of the notification and the top of the booking summary panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; it appears and disappears by expanding or collapsing its vertical space at the top of the content column, potentially with a brief slide-down animation to draw attention without feeling jarring.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking status guard operates as a gatekeeper over the entire booking details content area. When the booking is invalid, expired, or otherwise blocked, it overlays the main content column in a similar fashion to the loader but with a more structured message. A semi-opaque dark green veil covers everything from just beneath the header to the bottom of the booking section, gently dimming the summary, price, policies, and form beneath. Centered within this dimmed area is a medium-sized card (occupying roughly 40–50% of the content column’s width) with a warm light background, rounded corners, and a subtle shadow. Inside the card, a clear message about the booking status is presented in a centered stack: headline at the top, short explanatory copy beneath, and potentially a single button (e.g., 'Start a new booking') aligned to the center or right. Whitespace within and around this card is generous, in the 24–32px range, so it feels calm and authoritative, much like a refined modal in a boutique interface.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a fixed-position overlay for the main content column when active, sitting above all other components (including loader and notifications) and disabling interaction with underlying elements. When inactive, it is essentially invisible and does not occupy space in the layout.

name: Checkout & Payment
title: Checkout & Payment
description: Page for reviewing booking or gift card order details, entering billing and payment information, accepting terms, and submitting payment using the appropriate checkout flow.

core_features: Detect the checkout context (booking vs cart) from URL parameters and load the appropriate payment summary using getBookingPaymentSummary or getCartCheckoutSummary.
Display a structured order summary including line items, totals, and currency based on the loaded booking or cart data.
Collect payer billing details and chosen payment method information needed to construct the paymentMethod object for payment processing.
Require the user to review and agree to terms and conditions and privacy/booking policies before enabling payment submission.
Submit payment using processBookingPayment for room/package bookings or processCartPayment for gift card carts, and handle success or failure states.
Display post-payment feedback including confirmation status, transaction identifier, and updated booking status where applicable.
Provide navigation to return to the home page (index.html) after a successful payment confirmation.
user_workflows: Booking checkout workflow: 1) User arrives from booking_details.html to checkout.html with checkoutType=booking and bookingId in the URL. 2) On page load, the system reads the URL parameters and calls getBookingPaymentSummary with the bookingId. 3) The order summary is populated with booking, room, offer, lineItems, totalAmount, and currency returned by the interface. 4) The user reviews the summary and fills out billing details. Where available, guest information from the Booking (such as guest name and email) is used to prefill billing fields, which the user can edit. 5) The user selects a payment method and enters required payment details. 6) The user checks a required terms and conditions consent control. 7) The user clicks the button to confirm and pay. 8) The system validates required billing fields, payment method details, and terms consent, then calls processBookingPayment with the bookingId and the structured paymentMethod object. 9) If the response indicates success, the page shows a confirmation state with the updated Booking data, the transactionId, and any message returned. 10) The user may then click a 'Return to Home' control that navigates to index.html without additional parameters.
