Cart (gift card) checkout workflow: 1) User arrives from cart.html to checkout.html with checkoutType=cart in the URL (no bookingId is needed). 2) On page load, the system reads the URL parameters and calls getCartCheckoutSummary. 3) The order summary is populated with the current Cart, its CartItems, lineItems, totalAmount, and currency returned by the interface. 4) The user reviews the cart summary including gift card configuration details that are already stored in CartItem fields. 5) The user provides billing details for the payer. 6) The user selects a payment method and enters required payment details. 7) The user accepts terms and conditions. 8) The user clicks the confirm and pay button. 9) The system validates input and calls processCartPayment with the paymentMethod object. 10) If the response indicates success, the page shows a confirmation state using the returned transactionId and message; the cleared cart state is reflected in subsequent navigation. 11) The user can click a control to return to index.html.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads the bookingId and checkoutType URL parameters and triggers the appropriate summary-loading interface call.
If checkoutType indicates a booking checkout and a valid bookingId is present, the user sees booking-related details and cannot manually switch to cart checkout without changing the URL.
If checkoutType indicates a cart checkout, the user sees the cart-derived summary based on Cart and CartItem data returned by getCartCheckoutSummary.
The user can expand or collapse sections of the order summary (for example, to see or hide individual line items) without altering the underlying data; these actions only affect visibility.

The billing details form fields update internal page state as the user types or changes values, and the form provides validation feedback (e.g., required fields, format checks) before payment submission.
The payment method selector updates the paymentMethod object in state whenever the user changes method type or modifies method-specific fields (e.g., card details).
The terms and conditions consent control must be active before the primary payment button is enabled; toggling it updates a simple boolean in page state.

Clicking the primary payment button triggers front-end validation; if validation passes, it dispatches the correct payment interface call (processBookingPayment or processCartPayment) based on the checkoutType in state.
During payment processing, the page shows a loading state and disables the payment button to prevent duplicate submissions; these controls are reset when the API response is received.
If the payment interface returns success=false or an error, the page displays an error message using the returned message (if provided) and allows the user to correct inputs and retry.

If the payment interface returns success=true, the page transitions to a confirmation state where the main form is read-only or hidden, and a confirmation module displays the transactionId and any message returned.
Clicking the 'Return to Home' control after a successful payment navigates the browser to index.html with no additional checkout-related URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is derived primarily from URL parameters and the results of the assigned interfaces. On load, the page reads checkoutType and bookingId from the URL. If checkoutType is 'booking' and bookingId is present, the page sets an internal checkout context of type 'booking' and calls getBookingPaymentSummary with bookingId. If checkoutType is 'cart', the page sets the checkout context to 'cart' and calls getCartCheckoutSummary with no additional parameters. If checkoutType is missing but bookingId exists, the page may treat this as a 'booking' context; if both are missing, the page shows a blocking error state since the checkout context cannot be resolved. When the summary-loading call returns successfully, its data (Booking, Room, Offer, Cart, CartItem, lineItems, totalAmount, currency) is stored in page-level state under a normalized structure, preserving the original currency and all line item labels provided by the interfaces. Billing and payment form fields are not read from any data model; instead, they live in transient form state and are used to construct a paymentMethod object only at the moment of payment submission (and as the user edits). When the user clicks the pay button, the controller inspects the current checkout context: if it is 'booking', it calls processBookingPayment with the URL-derived bookingId and the in-memory paymentMethod; if it is 'cart', it calls processCartPayment with paymentMethod only. The responses from these interfaces (including success flags, Booking updates, transactionId, and message) update the page state to either a success or error mode. The success mode is independent of URL parameters; refreshing the page after a successful payment will re-run the summary loading logic based on the URL, which may then show an updated booking status or an empty/cleared cart according to the back-end implementation. Navigation away from the page (for example, to index.html) does not preserve any in-memory state, so resuming an incomplete checkout requires returning with appropriate URL parameters.

components.functionality: Determines whether the checkout is for a booking or a cart by reading URL parameters and loads the appropriate payment summary using getBookingPaymentSummary or getCartCheckoutSummary. It initializes page-level state with the checkout context, loading status, error status, and the returned summary data.

Displays the current order summary derived from either the booking payment summary or the cart checkout summary. It shows details such as booking information or cart item information, lineItems, totalAmount, and currency as returned by the interfaces, and updates whenever the checkout context loader provides new data.

Collects payer billing information (such as billing name, billing address, email, and phone) required for constructing the paymentMethod object. For booking checkouts, it can prefill relevant fields from the Booking entity (for example, guest name and contact details), allowing the user to edit them before payment. The component validates required fields and exposes a valid/invalid state used by the payment-submit-controller.

Allows the user to choose a payment method type (for example, a specific card or other supported method) and enter the method-specific details. It composes these values into a paymentMethod object in page state in a structure that can be passed directly to processBookingPayment or processCartPayment. It provides validation feedback for method-specific fields and exposes whether the current paymentMethod is valid.
Captures the user's acceptance of required terms and conditions and any relevant policies. The control must be activated before the payment-submit-controller enables the final payment action.

Coordinates final payment submission. When the user activates the primary payment action and both the billing-details-form and payment-method-selector report valid states and the terms-consent-control is accepted, this component inspects the checkout context from the checkout-context-loader. If the context is booking, it calls processBookingPayment using the bookingId taken from URL parameters and the current paymentMethod object. If the context is cart, it calls processCartPayment using the current paymentMethod object. It manages loading state, disables duplicate submissions, and routes success or error responses to the payment-status-panel.

Displays the outcome of the payment attempt. On success, it shows confirmation information using the returned Booking data (where applicable), transactionId, and message from processBookingPayment or processCartPayment. On failure, it shows error feedback using the message returned and allows the user to attempt payment again by interacting with the other components.
Provides a control to navigate back to the home page after a successful payment. When activated, it redirects the user to index.html without including checkout-related URL parameters.

Shows global error messages that prevent the checkout from proceeding, such as missing or invalid URL parameters, failed summary loading, or repeated payment errors not tied to a single field. It can be dismissed by the user after reading.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The checkout page inherits the hero-style, single-section composition from the mockup but replaces the tagline/logo stack with a checkout stack. Below the existing full-width dark green header, all checkout components sit inside a vertically centered, 12-column grid-aligned content column that occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width. Within this column, components are arranged in a strictly top-to-bottom sequence: global error (if present) → payment status strip → order summary card → billing details form → payment method selector → terms consent → payment submit controller. Post-payment navigation appears beneath this stack once payment is completed. A loader can temporarily float above the entire content area. Each element is treated as a distinct band or card with clear separation, mirroring the mockup’s header → tagline → logo → status bar → booking widget progression.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The checkout page inherits the hero-style, single-section composition from the mockup but replaces the tagline/logo stack with a checkout stack. Below the existing full-width dark green header, all checkout components sit inside a vertically centered, 12-column grid-aligned content column that occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width. Within this column, components are arranged in a strictly top-to-bottom sequence: global error (if present) → payment status strip → order summary card → billing details form → payment method selector → terms consent → payment submit controller. Post-payment navigation appears beneath this stack once payment is completed. A loader can temporarily float above the entire content area. Each element is treated as a distinct band or card with clear separation, mirroring the mockup’s header → tagline → logo → status bar → booking widget progression.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header consumes ~12–15% of the viewport height, with the main checkout stack occupying the central 50–60%, leaving comfortable top and bottom breathing room where the interior photo hero remains visible. Horizontally, all checkout elements are constrained to a centered content column spanning about 8 out of 12 grid columns (60–70% width). Within this column, the payment status strip and order summary occupy the upper third of the stack, the billing and payment forms fill the middle third, and the terms + submit controls anchor the lower third, echoing the hero’s hierarchy where the booking status and widget sit toward the lower center over the background image. Individual blocks respect the spacing scale: 24–40px vertical gaps between major blocks and 16–24px internal padding on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderately spacious. Each major block (status strip, summary, forms, submit area) is visually distinct with generous internal padding (16–24px) and clear vertical separation (24–32px between closely related blocks, ~32–40px between tiers). Forms are grouped logically so that fields do not feel cramped: label–field stacks have 8–16px internal spacing, and column splits (if any on wider viewports) still maintain ample gutters. The overall effect is rich and inviting rather than sparse, mirroring the boutique-hotel aesthetic, while avoiding clutter in a function-heavy checkout context.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow combines the site’s global Z-pattern with a strong central vertical axis. The eye starts at the top-left logo and right-aligned nav in the header, then drops diagonally into the centered payment status strip and order summary, which act as the initial anchors for the checkout context. From there, the eye naturally flows straight down through the billing form, then the payment method selector, and finally rests on the prominent, right-weighted primary payment button within the submit controller, echoing the booking widget’s role in the hero. The gold-accented status strip and card outlines subtly guide the eye downward, while the darker background and warmer edges frame the central content column much like the lamps framed the hero’s middle column in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the familiar full-width dark green header with the boutique logo and navigation, the checkout page opens onto the same warm, full-bleed interior photograph, slightly dimmed to let the content stand forward. Centered on this backdrop is a tall, elegant column of checkout elements aligned to an invisible central axis, spanning roughly two-thirds of the viewport width. At the top of this column a slim, gold-accented payment status strip quietly reassures the guest they’re in a secure, nearly-complete step. Just below it, a richly padded order summary card presents the stay or gift card details in a compact, well-framed block. The eye drifts down into a larger, softly elevated billing form card, followed by a distinct but closely related payment method selector section. Near the bottom of the column, a left-aligned terms consent row precedes a strong, horizontally oriented payment submit bar: light in background, outlined in gold, with the dark green primary pay button anchored to the right, visually echoing the original booking widget sitting over the bed image. If errors occur, a gold-framed alert spans the full content width just under the header; if loading is in progress, a semi-opaque overlay with a small central card gently masks the hero beneath. After successful payment, a concise status panel and post-payment navigation links appear toward the lower center, guiding the guest onward. Throughout, generous spacing, gold accents, and card-like groupings preserve the boutique-hotel, refined atmosphere while keeping the checkout process clear and reassuring.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout context loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above all other checkout elements and partially above the hero image, but below the fixed site header so the brand remains visible. It covers 100% of the width and height of the visible page (excluding the browser chrome), rendering the background content slightly dimmed using a translucent dark green or charcoal veil. At the visual center of the viewport, aligned with the main content column’s center, a compact card (approximately 280–360px wide and 160–200px tall) with rounded corners and 24px internal padding displays a spinner and short loading copy. This card echoes the aesthetic of the logo badge and booking widget: softly elevated, with a slim gold outline or gold accent line at the top. The loader respects the central axis of the checkout column, so when it appears, it feels like a focused modal sitting directly over the same central band where the forms and submit bar live.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: fixed overlay covering the entire viewport (except optional fixed header), horizontally and vertically centered content card

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary panel sits within the main centered content column, just below the payment status strip and above the billing details form. It spans roughly 100% of the column width (about 60–70% of the viewport), with 24px margin above and 32px margin below to establish it as its own content tier. The panel itself is a card with a light, slightly warm background that contrasts gently against the darker, dimmed hero image, framed by a very thin gold border and a subtle shadow, similar in visual weight to the booking widget’s frame. Internally, it has 24px padding on all sides. The summary content is arranged with a clear left-right hierarchy: key booking/gift card details aligned to the left, total amount emphasized and aligned to the right, mirroring the way the original booking widget spread content horizontally within a bounded strip. Vertically, the card occupies about 20–25% of the checkout stack’s height, making it prominent but not overpowering. On large screens it sits roughly in the upper third of the main checkout column, forming the first substantial block the eye settles on after the status strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static within the main scrolling flow, anchored centrally in the content column

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing details form is the largest and most vertically expansive card in the checkout stack. It is placed directly beneath the order summary panel, with approximately 32px of vertical space separating them to emphasize the transition from review to input. The form card spans the full width of the central content column (about 60–70% of viewport width), maintaining alignment with the order summary edges. The card background is a soft off-white or very light warm tone, with a thin gold top border or outline and 24px–32px internal padding, giving it a generous, breathing feel akin to an elegant paper form. Within the card, fields are arranged in a logical, linear top-to-bottom order, sometimes using two-column field rows (e.g., first/last name, city/postcode) on wider screens with a 24px–32px gutter between columns, all aligned to the underlying 12-column grid. Vertical spacing between field rows is about 16–24px, keeping content organized but not crowded. The top of the billing card aligns horizontally with the main column, and the card’s vertical extent typically occupies around 30–40% of the overall checkout stack height, positioning it in the middle band of the hero, comparable in emphasis to the logo and central elements in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static within the main scrolling flow, expanding vertically based on field count

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method selector is positioned immediately below the billing details form, with a modest 24px vertical gap between them to signal close functional relationship while still visually separating the sections. It spans the same width as the billing form and order summary, aligning its left and right edges to maintain a strong, clean column. The selector is implemented as a smaller card or sub-section: either a distinct card with its own light background and 20–24px padding, or a visually separated lower segment of the billing card with a gold divider line at the top. In both cases, its height is more compact, typically 15–20% of the stack’s height, enough to house payment method tabs, radio buttons, and any logos without visual noise. Payment options are arranged horizontally across the width (e.g., card, Apple Pay, PayPal), echoing the horizontal structuring of the original booking widget elements, with evenly spaced clickable areas aligned along the central axis. On smaller screens, these stack vertically with 16px spacing. The selector’s design uses gold accents and iconography to signal interactivity while blending into the refined aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static within the main flow, visually grouped with billing details as part of a single functional tier

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms consent control appears directly beneath the payment method selector, with approximately 24–32px of vertical space, acting as a slim yet important row before the final action. It is left-aligned within the same central column, spanning about 80–100% of the column width, but visually reads as a single line or two of text plus a checkbox or toggle. There is minimal vertical height—just enough to house the control and its label (about 40–56px tall). The checkbox/toggle is aligned with the left edge of the other cards’ content area, and the text flows to the right, wrapping if necessary. To preserve visual rhythm, the baseline of the consent text lines up with the internal text of adjacent cards, and the space beneath this control to the payment submit controller is about 16–24px, tying it closely to the final button the way the status bar tied closely to the booking widget in the hero. Subtle gold-highlighted links (for terms and privacy) echo the understated gold dividers used elsewhere.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static inline element within the main column, occupying minimal vertical space

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment submit controller functions as the checkout page’s analog to the original booking widget and occupies the lower portion of the central hero area. It is positioned directly beneath the terms consent control and serves as the visual culmination of the vertical flow. The controller spans the full width of the main content column (about 60–70% of the viewport), with 16–24px of vertical spacing above it and 40–56px of additional breathing room below before the end of the hero section. This component is styled as a horizontal bar-like card with a light background, a thin gold outline, and 20–24px internal padding. Inside, content is arranged in a two- or three-column horizontal layout: on the left, a stacked summary of the total amount and any reassuring copy ("Secure payment" or accepted cards); on the right, the prominent dark green primary payment button, slightly larger and more saturated than other CTAs, aligning to the right edge of the card. On wider screens, the button may sit in the right third of the card; on narrower screens, elements stack vertically but remain centered within the card. The card’s lower edge visually aligns with the lower third of the hero, floating over the bed/background imagery much like the booking widget, reinforcing the sense of a focused, anchored action area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static within the main flow but visually anchored toward the bottom of the hero; can stick near the bottom of the viewport on very long forms if desired, but default behavior is non-sticky

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment status panel occupies a slim, full-width band at the top of the main checkout column, immediately below the global header (and global error banner if present) and above the order summary panel. It mirrors the booking status bar from the hero: a horizontally oriented strip spanning 100% of the central column width, with a warm gold or muted gold-tinted background and 16–20px of internal vertical padding. The panel’s height is modest—roughly 48–64px—enough to accommodate a single line of status text and an optional icon or step indicator. The text is centered horizontally within the strip to reinforce the central axis, with possible supporting text aligned slightly to the left or right depending on layout. A 24px vertical gap separates it from the order summary card below, clearly grouping it as contextual information for the entire checkout process rather than any single card. When reflecting dynamic states (e.g., processing, success, failure), the background tint and iconography adjust subtly, but it remains visually lighter than a full error banner, maintaining the refined, understated feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static at the top of the checkout stack; optionally can become sticky just under the header on taller pages, but by default scrolls with content

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-payment navigation appears only after a successful payment, occupying the lower-central area beneath the main submit controller and/or updated payment status panel. It is placed with about 32–40px of vertical space above it, ensuring it feels like the next step rather than part of the payment form. The component spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, aligned with the central column, but its internal elements are horizontally centered for a calm, resolved feel. Navigation options—such as buttons or links to view booking details, add to calendar, or return to the homepage—are arranged in a horizontal row with 16–24px spacing between them, or stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing on small screens. The visual treatment is lighter and less framed than the main cards: subtle text buttons with gold accents, or small bordered buttons with minimal elevation, indicating that the intense action moment has passed. The surrounding breathing room allows this section to sit comfortably above the fold on most screens, framed by the background image as a gentle conclusion to the checkout journey.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static within the main flow, appearing conditionally after successful payment

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global error banner is positioned directly beneath the site’s full-width dark green header, spanning the entire viewport width from edge to edge, independent of the central content column. It appears above all other checkout components, pushing the main checkout stack downward. The banner has a height of approximately 64–88px depending on message length, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Its background is a rich, warm tone (such as deep red or dark amber) with gold or off-white text and possibly a subtle gold border at the top or bottom, making it strongly noticeable yet still aligned with the premium aesthetic. The error icon and primary message are aligned to the left, following the header’s left anchor for logo and navigation, while the right side can hold a close icon or actionable link. A 24–32px vertical gap separates the bottom of the error banner from the payment status panel, ensuring the two bands don’t visually merge. When present, this banner becomes the immediate focal point upon load or error occurrence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: fixed position relative to the top of the page content: typically just below a sticky header; it scrolls away with the page unless explicitly configured as sticky, but visually behaves as a top alert band

name: Restaurant & Bar
title: Restaurant & Bar
description: Page to view the hotel’s restaurant & bar information and make a table reservation, with navigation to detailed menus and restaurant gift cards.

core_features: Load and display restaurant overview content (concept, description, hours, sample dishes) using getRestaurantOverview.
Display a reservation form configured by getRestaurantReservationConfig, including party size limits, seating areas, and table location preferences.
Allow users to select reservation date, time, party size, seating area, table location preference, and enter special requests and contact details.
Query and present available reservation time slots based on user-selected criteria using getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots.
Create a restaurant reservation for a selected time slot and contact details using createRestaurantReservation, and show feedback to the user.
Support deep linking and pre-filled reservation criteria based on URL parameters (reservationDate, partySize, fromTime, toTime, seatingArea).
Provide navigation to the detailed menu and dinner planner page (menu.html) with appropriate mealType and dietaryPreference URL parameters.
Provide navigation to the gift card page (gift_cards.html) pre-filtered for restaurant gift cards.
user_workflows:
View restaurant overview:
1) User navigates to restaurant.html via header or footer link without specific URL parameters.
2) Page calls getRestaurantOverview on load.
3) Restaurant overview section displays the returned name, description, hours list, and sample dish items.
4) User can read concept, opening hours per day, and sample dishes.

Start a reservation from a deep link with pre-filled criteria:
1) User clicks a ‘Reserve a table’ call-to-action on index.html that links to restaurant.html with reservationDate, partySize, fromTime, toTime, and/or seatingArea in the query string.
2) On load, restaurant.html parses these URL parameters and initializes the reservation form’s internal state with them.
3) Page calls getRestaurantReservationConfig to obtain valid seatingAreas, tableLocationPreferences, party size limits, and time range.
4) Page validates and normalizes the incoming URL parameters against the configuration (e.g., adjusting partySize if above maxPartySize, clamping times to minReservationTime/maxReservationTime if needed).
5) Using the resolved reservationDate, partySize, seatingArea, fromTime, and toTime, the page calls getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots.
6) Available time slots are listed; the corresponding fields in the reservation form are pre-populated so the user can immediately select a time slot and proceed.

Make or adjust a reservation directly on this page:
1) User lands on restaurant.html (with or without URL parameters).
2) getRestaurantReservationConfig loads and initializes default reservation form values when URL parameters are missing.
3) User selects or changes reservation date, party size, seating area, and optionally adjusts a time window (fromTime/toTime) within the allowed minReservationTime and maxReservationTime.
4) On each relevant change (date, partySize, seatingArea, time window), the page updates the URL query parameters to reflect the current selections and calls getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots with the updated values.
5) The time-slot list component displays available slots for the chosen criteria, or an appropriate no-availability state if no slots are returned.
6) User selects a time slot, then fills in contact fields (name, email, phone) and optional specialRequests and tableLocationPreference.
7) User submits the reservation form; the page calls createRestaurantReservation with timeSlotId and the current input values.
8) On success, the reservation confirmation panel displays details from the returned RestaurantReservation and message; on failure, an error message is shown and the user can adjust inputs and resubmit.

Change criteria after seeing availability:
1) After an initial availability search, the user decides to change party size, date, seating area, or time window.
2) User updates the relevant field(s) in the reservation form.
3) The reservation state manager updates the URL parameters and triggers a new call to getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots.
4) Time-slot list refreshes with the new set of available slots.
5) Any previously selected timeSlotId is cleared if it is no longer valid; the user selects a new time slot and can resubmit the reservation.

Navigate to menu and dinner planner:
1) In the restaurant & bar page, user opens the menu navigation panel.
2) User selects a desired meal type (aligned with MenuItem.mealType) and optionally a dietary preference (e.g., vegetarian vs. no specific preference) via controls in this panel.
3) User clicks a ‘View Menu’ or equivalent link.
4) The page navigates to menu.html with mealType and dietaryPreference encoded as URL parameters, derived from the user’s selections.
5) The menu.html page (outside this specification) uses these parameters to filter and display the appropriate menu and dinner planner options.

Navigate to restaurant gift cards:
1) User clicks a ‘Give a restaurant gift card’ link in the restaurant & bar page.
2) The page navigates to gift_cards.html with typeCode=restaurant and amount left null/omitted in the URL.
3) The gift_cards.html page (outside this specification) reads typeCode to pre-select restaurant-specific gift card options.
interactions: On page load: parse URL query parameters (reservationDate, partySize, fromTime, toTime, seatingArea); call getRestaurantOverview; call getRestaurantReservationConfig; derive initial reservation form state; optionally call getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots if a valid reservationDate and partySize are present.

On reservation date change: update internal reservationDate state; update reservationDate URL parameter; clear any previously selected timeSlotId; call getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots with the new reservationDate, current partySize, seatingArea, and current or default time window.

On party size change: enforce numeric limits using maxPartySize from getRestaurantReservationConfig; update partySize state; update partySize URL parameter; clear selected timeSlotId; call getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots with updated partySize.
On seating area change: update seatingArea state; update seatingArea URL parameter; clear selected timeSlotId; call getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots with the selected seatingArea.

On time window (fromTime/toTime) change: ensure the chosen times lie within minReservationTime and maxReservationTime from configuration; update internal state and URL parameters; re-call getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots using the adjusted time window.

On time-slot selection: user selects one available time slot; component stores the selected timeSlotId in its state and passes it to the reservation form; form validation requires a selected timeSlotId before submission.
On table location preference change: user selects one of the allowed tableLocationPreferences from configuration; value is stored and later passed to createRestaurantReservation.
On contact field changes: user enters guestName, guestEmail, guestPhone, and optional specialRequests; these are validated locally (e.g., required fields, email format) before allowing submission.

On reservation form submit: perform front-end validation (required fields, selected timeSlotId, partySize within range); if valid, call createRestaurantReservation with timeSlotId, partySize, seatingArea, tableLocationPreference, guestName, guestEmail, guestPhone, and specialRequests; handle loading/disabled state during API call.

On reservation creation success: read the returned reservation object and message; show confirmation with key details (reservationDate, reservationTime, partySize, seatingArea, status) from the RestaurantReservation; optionally clear or lock the form to prevent duplicate submissions.
On reservation creation failure: display an error state and message; allow user to correct inputs or re-select a time slot and resubmit.

On ‘View Menu’ or ‘Dinner Menu’ click: read current mealType and dietaryPreference selections from the menu navigation panel; construct a URL to menu.html including mealType and dietaryPreference in the query string; navigate to that URL.
On ‘Give a restaurant gift card’ click: navigate to gift_cards.html with typeCode=restaurant and no amount parameter set so that the gift card page can prompt for the amount.

On browser back/forward navigation: URL-based state manager reads the updated query parameters and re-syncs reservation form fields and time-slot list by re-calling getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots as needed.
page_functionality.state_logic: URL parameters directly control the initial and ongoing state of the reservation search and are kept in sync when the user interacts with the form. On load, reservationDate (if present) is used as the initial date for the reservation form; if absent or invalid according to getRestaurantReservationConfig constraints, the form uses a configuration-defined default date (e.g., current date) but does not write this default back to the URL until the user interacts. partySize from the URL is validated against maxPartySize from getRestaurantReservationConfig; if it exceeds the limit, the UI coerces it to maxPartySize in state and updates the partySize URL parameter to match. fromTime and toTime from the URL define the initial time window for querying getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots. These values are checked against minReservationTime and maxReservationTime: if they fall outside the allowed range, the page clamps them into the valid interval before using them in API calls and updates the URL parameters to the clamped values. seatingArea from the URL is matched against seatingAreas from getRestaurantReservationConfig; if the value does not exist in the configuration, the page chooses a default seating area from the configuration and removes or replaces the invalid value in the URL. After initialization, every change to reservationDate, partySize, seatingArea, fromTime, or toTime via user interaction updates the corresponding query parameters, so that the current search context is reflected in the URL. The latest URL parameter values are always used as the single source of truth when calling getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots. The user’s selection of a specific time slot (timeSlotId) is kept in component state only and is not written to the URL, because it is tightly coupled to a specific availability result set and could become invalid if reloaded; if the user modifies any of the search criteria or navigates away and back, a new availability search is performed and the previous selection is cleared. Navigation to other pages uses URL parameters in a similar way: when the user leaves to menu.html, the page passes the currently selected mealType and dietaryPreference via query string; when navigating to gift_cards.html, the page passes typeCode=restaurant and intentionally omits an amount parameter so that the next page can manage its own state. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used; all state that must survive reloads or deep-linking is derived from the current URL parameters and the responses from the defined interfaces.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays the restaurant’s overview information, including name, description, hours by day, and sample dishes. It calls getRestaurantOverview once on page load, then renders the returned text and lists in a structured way. The component is read-only and does not modify any reservation state.

Initializes all configuration-driven aspects of the reservation form and search behavior. On page load, it calls getRestaurantReservationConfig to obtain seatingAreas, tableLocationPreferences, defaultPartySize, maxPartySize, timeIntervalMinutes, minReservationTime, and maxReservationTime. It then merges these configuration values with any reservation-related URL parameters to produce the initial reservation state (reservationDate, partySize, seatingArea, fromTime, toTime) and passes this state down to the reservation form and time-slot list components.

Maintains two-way synchronization between the reservation-related UI state and the browser URL query parameters. On initialization, it reads reservationDate, partySize, fromTime, toTime, and seatingArea from the query string and provides them to the reservation-config-loader. Whenever the user changes any of these fields in the reservation form, this component updates the query parameters accordingly without reloading the page. It also listens to browser history events (back/forward) and re-emits updated state to the reservation form and time-slot list, which may then re-call getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots.

Provides inputs for reservationDate, partySize, seatingArea, tableLocationPreference, guestName, guestEmail, guestPhone, and specialRequests, and receives a selected timeSlotId from the time-slot list component. It validates the user input (required contact fields, partySize within maxPartySize, presence of a selected timeSlotId) and, on submission, calls createRestaurantReservation. It also reacts to configuration data (e.g., available seatingAreas and tableLocationPreferences) supplied by reservation-config-loader and keeps the criteria fields synchronized with reservation-url-state-sync.

Displays the set of available reservation time slots for the selected date, party size, seating area, and time window. Whenever reservation criteria change (reservationDate, partySize, seatingArea, fromTime, toTime) and are valid, it calls getRestaurantAvailableTimeSlots with those values. The component renders each returned item (timeSlotId, reservationDate, reservationTime, seatingArea, availableCapacity) and allows the user to select exactly one as the active timeSlotId, which it passes up to the reservation-form component. If no slots are returned, it displays a no-availability state and suggests modifying criteria.

Encapsulates the call to createRestaurantReservation and coordinates state updates on success or failure. It receives timeSlotId, partySize, seatingArea, tableLocationPreference, guestName, guestEmail, guestPhone, and specialRequests from the reservation-form. It calls createRestaurantReservation with these parameters, then forwards the returned reservation object and message to the reservation-confirmation-panel. It also provides loading and error state back to the reservation-form so that the submit button can be disabled and errors shown appropriately.

Shows feedback to the user after an attempt to create a reservation. On success, it displays key details from the RestaurantReservation returned by createRestaurantReservation (such as reservationDate, reservationTime, partySize, seatingArea, and status) along with the message field. On error, it displays an error message and allows the user to close the panel and make changes. This component is passive with respect to APIs and only consumes data supplied by reservation-submit-handler.

Allows the user to choose a mealType and dietaryPreference before navigating to the detailed menu and dinner planner page. The component presents selectable options corresponding to the mealType enum of MenuItem and to supported dietary preferences. When the user clicks the ‘View Menu’ or equivalent trigger, it constructs a URL to menu.html including mealType and dietaryPreference query parameters derived from the current selections and initiates navigation. It does not call any APIs directly.

Provides a single action for users to purchase or learn about restaurant-specific gift cards. On click, it navigates to gift_cards.html with typeCode=restaurant and omits the amount parameter so that the destination page can handle amount selection. This component is purely navigational and does not interact with the restaurant APIs.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the hero-based, vertically stacked center-column arrangement from the mockup. Within a full-width restaurant interior hero image, all page-specific content is organized in a single, linear column: restaurant overview at the visual center → optional reservation confirmation/status banner → reservation form and time-slot list as a unified booking block → menu navigation row with a right-aligned gift card link. The technical components (loader, URL sync, submit handler) are non-visual and support this main vertical flow without occupying visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the hero-based, vertically stacked center-column arrangement from the mockup. Within a full-width restaurant interior hero image, all page-specific content is organized in a single, linear column: restaurant overview at the visual center → optional reservation confirmation/status banner → reservation form and time-slot list as a unified booking block → menu navigation row with a right-aligned gift card link. The technical components (loader, URL sync, submit handler) are non-visual and support this main vertical flow without occupying visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A full-width hero image spans the viewport, while a centered content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the page width. Vertically within the hero, about the upper 30–35% is dedicated to the restaurant overview text block, the middle 30–40% to the reservation confirmation banner and the reservation form + time-slot list, and the lower 15–20% to the menu navigation panel and gift card link. Each major block is separated with 24–40px vertical spacing, and each card-like element (form, banner) has 16–24px internal padding, matching the spacing rhythm from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout aims for a rich but controlled density: multiple functional elements share the hero (overview copy, booking controls, menus), but each is given ample breathing room. The reservation form and time-slot list form a single, slightly denser functional cluster, while the overview text above and menu links below are more relaxed, with extra whitespace to preserve the boutique, premium feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow inherits the Z-pattern: the user enters via the global header (logo left, navigation right), then their eye drops diagonally into the center of the hero where the restaurant overview sits. From there it flows down to the horizontally structured reservation form and attached time-slot list, which act as the primary interactive focus. Finally, the eye continues downward to the horizontal menu navigation panel and the right-aligned gift card link, providing a secondary decision path after considering reservations.

layout.overall_description: The Restaurant & Bar page opens on a full-width hero image of the restaurant interior, with the familiar dark green header and navigation overlaid at the top. Within this hero, a centered content column about two-thirds the width of the viewport holds all page-specific content, visually decoupled from the edges by generous side margins. Near the upper-middle of the hero, the restaurant overview section sits centered, echoing the tagline treatment from the mockup: a title and short description stacked with comfortable spacing, framed by the warm background. Just below this, when active, a slim gold-toned confirmation/status banner spans the same central column, signaling booking outcomes or important messages without overwhelming the scene. Directly underneath, a horizontally oriented reservation form card, outlined in gold and filled with a light surface, overlays the lower portion of the hero, with date, time, and party-size inputs arranged in a single row leading into a dark green primary action button on the right. The time-slot list is visually attached to the bottom of this form, either as the lower half of the same card or as an immediately adjacent panel, presenting selectable time chips in a neatly spaced grid. Further down, at the bottom of the hero or just into the next section, a discrete menu navigation row runs horizontally across the same center column, with menu links aligned from the left and a smaller, right-aligned gift card link, echoing the header’s left-to-right navigation and CTA pattern. The overall effect is a warm, boutique hero where storytelling (overview) and action (reservation) are vertically layered, with clear tiers of importance and ample whitespace preserving a premium, Parisian-inspired atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The restaurant overview section is positioned in the upper-middle of the hero, centered within the main content column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width. It sits approximately one header-height (about 64–80px) below the global navigation bar, acting as the first page-specific focal point. The text (title, subtitle, short description) is stacked vertically with 16–24px internal spacing and aligned centrally, similar in spirit to the centered tagline in the mockup, with generous whitespace both above and below (24–40px) so it does not visually collide with the reservation block beneath. The background remains the full-bleed restaurant image, with no hard container around this section, allowing it to feel integrated yet clearly separated by spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main hero column; it scrolls with the page and does not overlap or float beyond its vertical zone.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-config-loader has no direct visual representation; it operates behind the scenes to prepare data for the reservation experience. Conceptually, it is tied to the reservation area in the middle of the hero, but it occupies no visible width or height and does not introduce any gaps or extra spacing between elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it runs before or alongside the rendering of the reservation form without affecting layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-url-state-sync component is also non-visual and has no explicit footprint in the layout. It is logically associated with the reservation form and time-slot list, synchronizing state with the URL while leaving the visual stack of components and their spacing unchanged.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it does not alter positioning or occupy space in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation form is the main interactive card in the hero, positioned below the restaurant overview (and below the confirmation banner when present), centered in the content column. It spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides and a gold outline, visually echoing the booking widget from the mockup. On desktop, its inputs are arranged in a single horizontal row: date picker on the left, then time selector, party size selector, and finally a dark green primary 'Reserve' button aligned to the right edge; each field has 16–24px horizontal spacing between them. Vertically, there is 24–32px of space above the form (separating it from the overview/confirmation) and 16–24px below (connecting it to the time-slot list), and it sits slightly over the lower third of the hero image so that the card feels anchored in the scene without covering key imagery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero content column but visually layered as an overlay card on top of the background image, with a subtle elevation/shadow; it is not sticky and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot list is visually attached to the bottom of the reservation form, forming a unified booking block. It aligns perfectly with the form’s width (60–70% of the viewport) and is either rendered as the lower section of the same card or as an immediately adjacent panel sharing the same left and right edges, with a small 8–16px gap from the form’s bottom edge. Inside, time slots appear as pill-shaped chips or compact buttons arranged in horizontal rows, wrapping to additional rows as needed, with 8–12px gaps between chips and 16px vertical gaps between rows, preserving a controlled but active density. There is 24–32px of whitespace below the time-slot list before the menu navigation panel begins, ensuring the booking block reads as a distinct tier.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands or contracts vertically based on available time slots, pushing subsequent content down; remains fully within the main content column and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-submit-handler does not render any visible UI; it is associated with the reservation form’s submit action. It has no width, height, or spacing in the layout and does not influence the visual arrangement of other components, apart from triggering changes like showing the confirmation panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it executes logic on submission without affecting layout geometry.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-confirmation-panel appears as a slim, prominent banner within the main content column, directly between the restaurant overview section and the reservation form when active. It spans the same width as the form (60–70% of the viewport), centered on the page, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and uses a gold or warm-toned background to echo the availability status bar in the mockup. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing above it (separating it from the overview) and another 16–24px below it before the reservation form, so it reads as a distinct, but closely related, layer in the booking flow. The text is left-aligned within the banner, with an optional close icon on the right edge, reinforcing the linear reading from overview → message → form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically in the flow; when visible, it pushes the reservation form downward but otherwise behaves as a normal, scrollable banner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The menu-navigation-panel is placed below the complete reservation block (form plus time-slot list), still within the main central column, forming the next visual tier. It sits roughly 24–40px beneath the time-slot list, spanning the same 60–70% width, and is laid out as a horizontal row of text links (e.g., Dinner Menu, Bar Menu, Brunch) aligned to the left within this column. The links have about 16–24px horizontal spacing between them and 8–12px vertical padding if they are in pill-like containers, maintaining the boutique, slightly classic feel while remaining understated compared to the reservation card. The panel’s background is either transparent over the hero image or on the solid page background just below the hero, but in either case, it retains ample whitespace above and below to avoid crowding the booking controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow; it scrolls normally and does not stick to the viewport, but visually acts as a sub-navigation tier following the booking section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The restaurant-gift-card-link shares the horizontal band with the menu-navigation-panel but is aligned to the far right within the same central column, creating a subtle left-to-right tension similar to the header’s navigation and CTA balance. On desktop, the menu links occupy the left two-thirds of this row, while the gift card link sits on the right third, right-aligned and slightly set apart with a 16–24px gap from the panel’s right edge. The link itself is styled as a refined text link or small pill with understated emphasis, so it is discoverable but secondary to the reservation and menus. There is consistent vertical spacing (24–32px) above this combined row relative to the booking block and a bit less below when transitioning into subsequent page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the horizontal row of the menu navigation area; it does not float independently and scrolls with the rest of the content.

name: Menus & Dinner Planner
title: Menus & Dinner Planner
description: Page to browse restaurant menus by meal and course, apply dietary filters, build a temporary dinner plan, and save it for future reference.

core_features: Load available meal types and dietary filter options using getMenuFilterOptions and expose them as selectable filters.
Read incoming URL parameters mealType and dietaryPreference to preselect the menu context and dietary filter when the page loads.
Retrieve and display menu items for the current mealType and filters using getMenuItems, grouped by courseType (e.g., appetizers, mains, desserts).
Allow users to add menu items (with quantity) to the current dinner plan using addMenuItemToDinnerPlan from the menu list.
Retrieve the current dinner plan with selected items and total price using getCurrentDinnerPlan and display it in a 'Your Selections' panel.
Allow users to enter an optional name and notes for their current dinner plan and save it using saveDinnerPlan.
Provide a 'Back to Restaurant' navigation link that takes users to restaurant.html.
user_workflows: Browse menus with URL-preselected context: (1) User arrives from restaurant.html or index.html with mealType and optional dietaryPreference in the URL. (2) Page controller reads URL parameters. (3) Page calls getMenuFilterOptions to obtain valid mealTypes and dietaryOptions. (4) Controller validates the incoming mealType/dietaryPreference against the options; if invalid or missing, it selects a default from the returned options and updates the URL. (5) Controller calls getMenuItems with the resolved mealType and filters, then renders menu items grouped by course. (6) User scrolls and inspects menu items.

Build a dinner plan from menu items: (1) On initial load, controller calls getCurrentDinnerPlan to retrieve any existing plan and its items, then populates the 'Your Selections' panel. (2) User clicks an 'Add to plan' control on a menu item and optionally adjusts quantity. (3) Menu item component triggers addMenuItemToDinnerPlan with the selected menuItemId and quantity. (4) On success, the component updates the dinner plan state using the returned dinnerPlan, items, and totalPrice. (5) The 'Your Selections' panel reflects the latest items and summed total price.

Filter menus by meal and dietary preference: (1) User changes mealType in the filter bar. (2) Filter bar updates the URL parameter mealType and clears or preserves dietaryPreference as configured. (3) Page controller reacts to the change, calls getMenuItems with the new mealType and current filters, and updates the menu list. (4) When user toggles a dietaryPreference option, the filter bar updates the URL dietaryPreference parameter. (5) Controller calls getMenuItems again with updated filters and refreshes the menu list while keeping the current dinner plan intact.

Save a dinner plan with name and notes: (1) After adding desired items, user clicks 'Save dinner plan' in the 'Your Selections' panel. (2) Panel opens a simple save form (or inline fields) for plan name and notes. (3) User fills in name and/or notes and submits. (4) Save form triggers saveDinnerPlan with the provided name and notes. (5) On success, the UI displays success feedback using response.success and response.message and updates any stored dinnerPlan metadata with the returned dinnerPlan. (6) User may continue adjusting the plan by adding more items; subsequent saves overwrite the current plan metadata via saveDinnerPlan.

Navigate back to the restaurant overview: (1) User clicks 'Back to Restaurant' link. (2) Page navigates to restaurant.html. (3) If required by global navigation rules, the link may include the current mealType and dietaryPreference in the target URL so the restaurant page can show coherent context, but no additional interfaces are called from this page.
interactions: Filter selection: Changing the mealType or dietaryPreference controls in the filter bar triggers an update of the URL parameters and a refresh of menu items via getMenuItems. No page reload is required; state is maintained in memory and in the URL.
Menu item inspection: Clicking on a menu item row or card can expand or collapse additional details (description, dietaryLabels) using only client-side state derived from getMenuItems results.

Add to dinner plan: Clicking an 'Add to plan' button or similar, after selecting a quantity, calls addMenuItemToDinnerPlan. The response is used to update the 'Your Selections' panel and total price. Repeating the action for the same item adds additional quantity according to backend behavior defined for addMenuItemToDinnerPlan.

Dinner plan viewing: The 'Your Selections' panel listens to current dinner plan state (initially from getCurrentDinnerPlan, then from addMenuItemToDinnerPlan responses) and renders all items grouped by courseType, with itemPrice and quantity from DinnerPlanItem plus totalPrice.

Save plan flow: Clicking 'Save dinner plan' reveals inputs for name and notes and a submit button. Submitting calls saveDinnerPlan. On success, the panel displays confirmation text using the returned message, and may disable or re-label the save button until further changes are made. On failure (success = false), an error message is shown and the user can retry.

URL-driven refresh: Manually changing URL parameters mealType or dietaryPreference (e.g., via browser navigation or shared links) and reloading the page causes the controller to re-apply those parameters, call getMenuItems and getCurrentDinnerPlan, and restore a consistent view.
Back navigation: Clicking 'Back to Restaurant' triggers navigation to restaurant.html with no API calls from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters mealType and dietaryPreference as the canonical external state for the menu context and primary filter. On initial load, the page controller reads these parameters. It then calls getMenuFilterOptions to determine valid mealTypes and dietaryOptions. If mealType is missing or not found among the returned mealTypes, the controller selects a default (such as the first available option from mealTypes) and updates the URL to include that value. If dietaryPreference is present in the URL but not among dietaryOptions, it is removed from the internal filter state and from the URL. Whenever the user changes the mealType or dietaryPreference via the filter components, the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters using the browser history API (without reloading) and calls getMenuItems with current mealType and a filters object representing the selected dietaryPreference (if any). The sortBy parameter for getMenuItems is managed as an internal state value (e.g., default sorting when no user sorting is provided) and can optionally be reflected in an additional URL parameter if a sorting control is later added. The dinner plan itself is not encoded in URL parameters; instead, its state is maintained on the backend and exposed through getCurrentDinnerPlan and addMenuItemToDinnerPlan. On page load, getCurrentDinnerPlan is called once to initialize the 'Your Selections' panel. Subsequent modifications to the dinner plan rely on responses from addMenuItemToDinnerPlan to keep the in-page state in sync with the backend. Calling saveDinnerPlan does not affect URL parameters; it only enhances server-side state for the current dinner plan by adding name and notes and returning the updated DinnerPlan object and success/message metadata.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, reacts to URL parameter changes, manages in-memory page state for current mealType, dietaryPreference, menu items, and dinner plan summary.
Displays selectable mealType and dietaryPreference filters based on options from getMenuFilterOptions, and updates URL parameters and triggers menu reload when filters change.
Displays menu items returned from getMenuItems, grouped by courseType. Each item shows name, description, priceFormatted, and dietaryLabels, and exposes controls to add items to the dinner plan.
Represents a single menu item row or card with details from getMenuItems and provides quantity selection and an 'Add to plan' action that calls addMenuItemToDinnerPlan.

Shows the current dinner plan, including each DinnerPlanItem linked to its MenuItem, grouped by courseType, with display of quantity, itemPrice, and overall totalPrice. It receives initial data from getCurrentDinnerPlan and subsequent updates from addMenuItemToDinnerPlan.

Collects an optional name and notes for the current dinner plan and submits them via saveDinnerPlan. Displays success or error messages based on the response and updates dinner plan metadata in the panel.
Provides a clickable control to navigate back to restaurant.html, optionally including current mealType and dietaryPreference as URL parameters in the target if navigation rules require it.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom hero flow, then transitions into a two-column content area. Just below the global site header, the back-to-restaurant link sits at the top-left of the centered content column. Directly beneath it, the menu-page-controller acts like the hero’s tagline + logo cluster: it holds the page title and meal/time controls, visually centered within the main column. Below that, the menu-filter-bar mirrors the booking status + booking widget band: a prominent, horizontally-oriented filter strip that spans the content width. Under the filters, the layout splits: the menu-list-by-course runs down the left/main column, while the dinner-plan-panel sits to the right as a vertically-aligned card. The save-dinner-plan-form is integrated into the lower portion of this panel. The arrangement remains predominantly vertical in the user’s experience, but with a clear left-main / right-summary relationship in the central section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom hero flow, then transitions into a two-column content area. Just below the global site header, the back-to-restaurant link sits at the top-left of the centered content column. Directly beneath it, the menu-page-controller acts like the hero’s tagline + logo cluster: it holds the page title and meal/time controls, visually centered within the main column. Below that, the menu-filter-bar mirrors the booking status + booking widget band: a prominent, horizontally-oriented filter strip that spans the content width. Under the filters, the layout splits: the menu-list-by-course runs down the left/main column, while the dinner-plan-panel sits to the right as a vertically-aligned card. The save-dinner-plan-form is integrated into the lower portion of this panel. The arrangement remains predominantly vertical in the user’s experience, but with a clear left-main / right-summary relationship in the central section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered container occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, echoing the hero column in the mockup, with generous side margins showing the warm background or subtle texture. The top utility band (back link + menu-page-controller) uses roughly 15–20% of viewport height, creating a clear above-the-fold hero-esque header for this page. The filter bar occupies a shallow band directly beneath (around 10–15% of the visible height when first loaded). Below, the remaining height is dominated by the menu list and dinner plan: the menu-list-by-course takes about 65–70% of the content width (roughly 8 of 12 grid columns) on the left, while the dinner-plan-panel uses the remaining 30–35% (about 4 grid columns) on the right. Vertical spacing between major blocks (back link → controller → filters → list/panel) follows the 24–40px tiered rhythm from the mockup, preserving a rich but breathable layout.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is tuned to feel comfortably rich, similar to the original booking hero: multiple interactive elements are present but grouped and padded to avoid clutter. The menu-page-controller and menu-filter-bar are compact but not cramped, with clear internal padding and separation of controls. The menu-list-by-course uses a structured rhythm with ample vertical spacing between courses and between menu-item-cards, while each card has enough padding to feel premium. The dinner-plan-panel is dense in information (selected items, totals, actions) but housed in a single framed card with defined sections and gold dividers, which keeps it legible and luxurious rather than busy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow borrows the mockup’s Z-pattern. The eye first lands at the top-left on the back-to-restaurant-link and then immediately to the right into the menu-page-controller’s main title and controls. It then moves back toward the center where the filter bar sits as a strong horizontal band. From there, it drops diagonally into the left-hand menu-list-by-course (the primary browsing area), then shifts across to the right-side dinner-plan-panel, where users see their evolving dinner plan and the save form. Repeated horizontal elements (filter bar, panel header, panel footer/button) and subtle vertical alignments echo the original hero’s status strip and booking form, guiding the eye smoothly down the page.

layout.overall_description: The Menus & Dinner Planner page feels like a continuation of the warm, boutique hotel hero: a centrally anchored layout with rich dark green and gold accents, but adapted for content browsing. Just below the global header, a small, elegant back link on the top-left leads back to the main restaurant page. Directly beneath, a horizontally centered page controller block introduces the page title and key toggles (e.g., meal type, date), acting as the hero’s focal title area. A comfortable gap below reveals a gold-accented filter bar stretching across the width of the content column, mirroring the booking widget’s linear, functional strip. As users scroll, the layout opens into a two-column section: on the left, courses and dishes cascade downward in a structured, vertical list; on the right, a slightly elevated, gold-framed dinner plan panel floats like a refined card, summarizing chosen dishes and offering a clearly separated save form at its base. Everything sits within a generous central column—about two-thirds of the screen—leaving soft margins on either side so the content never presses against the edges. The result is a page that feels both functional and indulgent, guiding users smoothly from exploration (menus and filters) to intention (building and saving a dinner plan) with the same deliberate, tiered visual rhythm as the original booking hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The menu-page-controller is positioned near the top of the main content area, directly below the global site header and just under the back-to-restaurant link. It is horizontally centered within the main content container, which itself occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width and is aligned to the center of the page. The controller spans nearly the full width of this container (roughly 10–12 grid columns within the central 12-column grid), with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Vertically, it occupies a shallow band, similar in height to the hero’s tagline and logo grouping—approximately 12–15% of the above-the-fold area. Within the controller, the page title is center-aligned or slightly left-of-center, with meal-type toggles (e.g., Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner) and perhaps a date selector aligned horizontally beneath or to the right, maintaining a balanced composition. There is a clear vertical gap of 24–32px above it (to separate from the back link) and 24–32px below it (before the filter bar), following the tiered spacing pattern of the mockup’s tagline → logo → status bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow; it scrolls away with the page content. It anchors the top of the page’s hero-like section, but does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The menu-filter-bar sits directly below the menu-page-controller, separated by a comfortable 24–32px of whitespace. It is also centered within the main content container and spans nearly the full width of that container (around 90–100% of the central column, equivalent to roughly 10–12 grid columns). Visually, it echoes the booking widget: a horizontally oriented strip framed by a subtle gold outline or top-and-bottom dividers, with generous 16–24px padding on all sides. Within the bar, filter controls (dietary chips, cuisine dropdowns, price range, etc.) are arranged from left to right, with the primary filter toggles starting on the left and an optional ‘Clear filters’ or ‘Reset’ action on the far right, creating a slight horizontal tension reminiscent of the navigation + BOOK control in the header. The bar’s height is compact yet comfortable—about 64–80px high—so it reads as a single functional band. A 24–40px gap below the filter bar separates it from the main content area (menu list and dinner plan), mirroring the distance between the booking status strip and booking widget in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls with the content. On long pages, it may be visually treated as a distinct band so that when users scroll back to it, it feels like a stable control hub.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The menu-list-by-course begins immediately below the menu-filter-bar, offset downward by roughly 24–32px of whitespace. On desktop, this list occupies the left portion of a two-column layout within the central container. It uses about 65–70% of the container’s width, corresponding to approximately 7–8 of the 12 grid columns within the centered content area, with its left edge aligned to the left edge of the container. The right edge of the list aligns vertically with the inner edge of the dinner-plan-panel, creating a clean columnar structure. The courses are presented in vertical sequence: each course heading (e.g., Starters, Mains, Desserts) is left-aligned, with a slightly larger serif or refined type, and separated from the previous course by 32–40px of vertical space, echoing the generous spacing between major blocks in the hero. Under each course heading, individual menu-item-cards are stacked top-to-bottom, each separated by 16–24px. The list extends vertically as needed, forming the main scrollable body of the page. On narrower screens, this component expands to full width (100% of the content container) and the dinner-plan-panel stacks below it, preserving a primarily vertical reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow and scrolls normally. On desktop, it works in tandem with a sticky right-hand panel, so as users scroll down the list, the relative alignment to the dinner-plan-panel’s top edge changes while maintaining the left/right column relationship.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each menu-item-card sits inside the menu-list-by-course as a horizontally full-width card within the left column. The card width matches the course column width (about 65–70% of the central container on desktop, or 100% on mobile), with 16–24px padding on all sides, following the card and bar padding from the mockup. Cards have a subtle background contrast (e.g., slightly lighter dark green or soft off-white) and may be framed with an understated gold border or a single gold divider beneath the item name, echoing the refined gold accents of the hero’s logo and status bar. Layout inside the card follows a left-to-right, then top-to-bottom flow: the dish name is left-aligned at the top, with a small, elegant dietary icon set immediately to its right or below. The price is right-aligned in the same row as the name, analogous to how the BOOK button anchors the right side of the booking widget. Beneath this top row, a short description runs across most of the card width, with a comfortable 8–16px gap from the title line. At the bottom right of the card, an 'Add to dinner plan' button or control sits, aligning visually with the price anchor, creating a clear action point in the same way the booking button attracts attention. Vertical spacing between cards (16–24px) ensures the list feels structured but not crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, repeating unit. It participates in the scrollable list; no special fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dinner-plan-panel occupies the right-hand side of the main content area, starting aligned vertically with the top of the first course in the menu-list-by-course (or slightly above it, depending on viewport height). On desktop, it uses approximately 30–35% of the central content container’s width, equivalent to 4–5 of the 12 grid columns, and is aligned to the right edge of the container. The panel appears as a vertically oriented card with a refined gold outline and 24–32px of internal padding, echoing the visual weight and framing of the hero’s logo badge and booking widget combined. Its top edge sits roughly level with the first visible items of the menu list, leaving a 24–32px vertical gap below the filter bar to preserve the tiered rhythm. Inside, the panel is divided into sections: a header row at the top (e.g., 'Your Dinner Plan') with a subtle gold divider below; a scrollable or vertically stacked list of selected items in the middle with 12–16px spacing; and, at the bottom, the save-dinner-plan-form area. The panel’s height is sized to show several selected items and the form without feeling cramped—on larger screens, the panel will often be shorter than the viewport so it feels like a contained object floating above the background, similar to the booking widget floating on the bed image in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky on desktop within the viewport: its top edge stays approximately 80–96px below the browser top (or just below the site header) as the user scrolls down the menu list, ensuring the plan summary remains visible. On tablet and mobile, it becomes a full-width block that stacks below the menu-list-by-course and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-dinner-plan-form is nested within the dinner-plan-panel, occupying the full interior width of the panel beneath the list of selected items. It is visually separated from the plan items by a horizontal gold divider and 16–24px of vertical spacing, echoing the way the hero’s status bar and booking widget are clearly segmented. The form fields (e.g., plan name, date, contact details) are arranged in a simple vertical stack, left-aligned, with 8–16px vertical spacing between inputs. Each input stretches nearly the full internal width of the panel (minus padding), sustaining a clean columnar feel. At the bottom of the form, the primary 'Save dinner plan' button anchors the panel much like the dark green 'BOOK' button in the booking widget: it is horizontally centered or slightly right-aligned, spans about 60–80% of the panel width, and has generous vertical padding (12–16px) to feel substantial and inviting. There is a final 16–24px margin between the bottom of the button and the panel’s lower edge to avoid visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the dinner-plan-panel; its position is fixed relative to the panel’s internal scroll (if any). On smaller screens where the panel stacks, it simply appears as the lower subsection of that card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-restaurant-link is the first component in the page’s main content area, appearing directly below the global header and above the menu-page-controller. It is left-aligned to the main content container’s left edge, sitting within that centered column which spans about 60–70% of the viewport width. The link occupies minimal height—essentially a single line of text with an optional left arrow icon—but it is given breathing room: approximately 32–48px margin from the bottom of the global header and 16–24px margin before the menu-page-controller. This subtle spacing makes it feel like a gentle breadcrumb rather than part of the main hero content, similar in spirit to how the mockup’s phone number and logo in the top-left are present but understated compared to the central tagline and logo badge. The link’s position in the top-left corner of the content column acts as the starting point of the page’s Z-pattern visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls away with the content and does not stick. It functions as a lightweight navigation hint rather than a persistent control.

name: Offers & Packages
title: Offers & Packages
