event_handlers: onChangeRecipientName: Updates recipientName in state as the user types; marks it as required for all delivery methods and sets validation errors if left empty when submitting.

onChangeRecipientEmail: Updates recipientEmail in state; validates its format; for delivery methods that require an email (e.g., email or other digital methods defined by app logic), marks it as required and sets or clears validation errors accordingly.
onChangePersonalMessage: Updates personalMessage in state; optionally validates length or other constraints if defined by app logic; updates the summary to indicate presence of a custom message.

onDeliveryMethodChanged: Responds to delivery-method-selector changes; adjusts whether recipientEmail is required or optional based on deliveryMethod; re-validates email and updates error states where needed.

components.functionality: Collects purchaserName and purchaserEmail to associate the gift card with the buyer and to use in the addGiftCardToCart call.
event_handlers: onChangePurchaserName: Updates purchaserName in state; marks it as required; clears or sets validation error if empty on submission.
onChangePurchaserEmail: Updates purchaserEmail in state; validates its format and marks it as required; sets or clears validation error accordingly.
onPrefillFromAuthenticatedUser: Optional handler that can prefill purchaser details if the broader site passes them into this page or state; ensures that any prefilled values still pass validation.

components.functionality: Displays a consolidated, read-only summary of the current gift card configuration (type, amount, quantity, currency, delivery method, send date, recipient and purchaser summaries) so the user can confirm details before adding the card to the cart.
event_handlers: onStateChange: Listens to changes in selectedGiftCardTypeId, amount, currency, quantity, deliveryMethod, sendDate, recipientName, recipientEmail, personalMessage presence, purchaserName, and purchaserEmail; recomputes any derived values (such as totalPrice = amount × quantity in display only, without persisting to the backend) and updates the summary view.
onValidationStateChange: Reflects whether the overall configuration is currently valid or has outstanding errors; may visually indicate readiness to add to cart.

components.functionality: Wraps all sub-forms (type, amount, delivery, recipient, purchaser) into a single submission action that validates the configuration and calls addGiftCardToCart. On success, navigates the user to cart.html.
event_handlers: onSubmit: Triggered when the user clicks the Add to Cart / Buy Gift Card button. Executes full validation across all sub-forms: checks that a gift card type is selected, amount is present and within allowed min/max, currency is selected, quantity is valid, a delivery method is selected and compatible with the type, sendDate is valid if provided, and that recipient and purchaser required fields are filled and valid. If any validation fails, sets error states in the respective components and aborts submission.

onSubmitValid: When validation passes, constructs the parameter object for addGiftCardToCart using current state: giftCardTypeId from the selected GiftCardType.id; amount from state; currency from state; deliveryMethod from state; recipientName, recipientEmail, sendDate, personalMessage, purchaserName, purchaserEmail from state; quantity from state. Calls addGiftCardToCart with these parameters and temporarily disables the submit button.

onAddGiftCardToCartSuccess: Handles a successful response from addGiftCardToCart (success = true). The returned cart and cartItem can optionally be stored in higher-level state if needed, but the main action is to navigate to cart.html as defined in outgoing_connections.

onAddGiftCardToCartFailure: Handles an unsuccessful response (success = false) or any network error by re-enabling the submit button, keeping all current configuration values intact, and showing an error message (using the response.message if provided). No navigation occurs.

onBeforeUnload: Optional handler that can warn the user if they have started a configuration but not yet submitted, depending on broader site behavior; this does not use any persistent storage and only relies on in-memory state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page mirrors the hero-centric, linear stack from the mockup by placing all gift card configuration elements inside a single, vertically oriented 'gift card configurator' card that sits in the visual center of the hero, where the booking widget would normally be. Within this card, components are arranged top-to-bottom in a logical flow: type selection at the top, then amount and quantity, followed by delivery method, recipient details, purchaser details, and finally a gold-toned summary strip directly above the primary add-to-cart controls. The global site header and hero (logo, tagline, background image) remain as in the mockup, with this configurator card visually replacing the booking widget at the bottom of the hero stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page mirrors the hero-centric, linear stack from the mockup by placing all gift card configuration elements inside a single, vertically oriented 'gift card configurator' card that sits in the visual center of the hero, where the booking widget would normally be. Within this card, components are arranged top-to-bottom in a logical flow: type selection at the top, then amount and quantity, followed by delivery method, recipient details, purchaser details, and finally a gold-toned summary strip directly above the primary add-to-cart controls. The global site header and hero (logo, tagline, background image) remain as in the mockup, with this configurator card visually replacing the booking widget at the bottom of the hero stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header still occupies the top 12–15% of the viewport. The central hero area with tagline and logo badge occupies roughly the next 25–30%, leaving the bottom 40–50% of the initial viewport for the gift card configurator card. Within that card, each component section uses clear vertical separation (~24–32px) to avoid crowding, and the entire card sits in a centered content column that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop. The configurator card itself has generous internal padding (20–24px on small screens, up to 32px on large screens) so that form fields and labels have breathing room and don’t visually collide with the warm photographic background. As the content grows, the card extends downward beyond the first viewport but maintains the same centered width and spacing rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is tuned to feel premium and unhurried, similar to the booking layout: not sparse, but comfortably spaced. Major logical groups (selector, each form section, summary, add-to-cart) are separated by 24–40px of vertical whitespace, while related controls inside each component sit closer together with 8–16px gaps. This produces a tiered rhythm: section heading → closely grouped fields → a slightly larger gap → next section. The summary panel and add-to-cart area are visually denser, echoing the functional richness of the booking bar, but still padded enough (16–24px inside) to feel refined.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow still follows a Z-pattern rooted in the mockup. The user’s eye first hits the top-left logo in the header, then sweeps right to navigation. From there, it drops diagonally to the center of the hero where the page title/tagline and logo badge are, then continues downward to the top of the gift card configurator card. Inside the card, the flow becomes a controlled vertical scan: type selector at the top, then amount, delivery, recipient, and purchaser sections. At the bottom of the card, a gold-toned summary strip catches the eye, followed immediately by the darker, high-contrast add-to-cart button area on the right side of that strip, creating a final left-to-right sweep (summary text on the left, total and primary CTA on the right) that reinforces completion of the flow.

layout.overall_description: The Gift Cards page sits within the same warm, boutique-hotel hero as the main site: a full-width dark green header anchored at the top, overlaid on a cozy, edge-to-edge photographic background. The header retains the left-aligned badge logo, centered language control, and right-aligned navigation with a prominent booking link, forming the familiar top bar. Below it, in the visual center of the hero, a gold-accented tagline and the hexagonal logo badge still serve as the brand anchor. Directly beneath this, a large, vertically oriented gift card configurator card appears where the booking widget would normally be, centered on the page and spanning roughly two-thirds of the viewport width. This card has a subtle, light interior, framed by a refined gold outline and soft shadow so it floats above the background image without feeling heavy. Inside, the configuration steps cascade top-to-bottom with clear breathing space: card type options, amount and quantity, delivery method, recipient and purchaser details, and then a slim gold summary bar that runs the full width of the card. At the very base of the card, a horizontally structured add-to-cart area echoes the booking strip from the mockup, with copy and pricing aligned to the left/middle and a dark green, gold-accented primary button anchored to the right. The overall effect is a single, elegant vertical flow centered within the hero, framed by lamps and decor in the background, guiding the user calmly from initial selection to confident purchase without visual clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader overlays the entire gift card configurator card when data is being fetched. It sits centered within the same central content column that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, aligned with all other gift card components. A semi-transparent dark green veil fills the interior of the configurator card, subtly dimming any underlying content while preserving the card’s gold outline. In the exact middle of this area—both horizontally and vertically—sits a compact loading indicator (spinner or skeletal lines) occupying roughly 25–30% of the card’s width. There is ample empty space around the loader so it feels calm rather than busy, in keeping with the hotel’s premium aesthetic. Once loading completes, this overlay disappears, revealing the stacked components beneath in their fixed positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a centered, semi-opaque overlay fixed to the bounds of the gift card configurator area, not the entire viewport; it scrolls with the page but always remains on top of the underlying components while active, intercepting interaction until data has loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift card type selector is placed at the very top inside the central configurator card, just below any card-level heading or introductory copy. It spans the full internal width of the card, which itself occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width and is horizontally centered. There is a generous top margin inside the card (about 24–32px from the card’s top edge) before the selector starts, giving it breathing room under the hero logo/tagline area. The selector’s title is left-aligned, with a warm serif heading, and sits above the options by roughly 8–12px. Below the title, the card type options are arranged in a horizontal row of 2–3 tiles (depending on how many types exist) that collectively fill about 80–90% of the card’s width, centered within the column. Each tile is a compact card with 16–20px of internal padding, subtle gold hover/selection states, and 16–24px gaps between tiles. On wider screens, the row maintains a single line that mirrors the horizontal composition of the navigation; on smaller screens, the tiles stack vertically but remain centered. A vertical gap of 24–32px separates this selector from the amount-and-quantity form below, maintaining the tiered vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the configurator card; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the content and anchors the start of the configuration flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the type selector, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace, the amount and quantity form occupies the full internal width of the configurator card, continuing the centered column that spans 60–70% of the viewport. It is treated as a distinct sub-section with a subtle divider line or increased top padding to visually differentiate it from the selector above. Inside this section, there is generous padding on all sides (around 20–24px), creating a calm block that feels like a refined form card within the larger card. The section heading sits at the top-left, aligned with the selector’s heading above, and beneath it the amount input and quantity selector are laid out horizontally on desktop: the amount field to the left occupying about 60% of the section width, and the quantity field to the right occupying about 40%, with a 16–24px gap between them. This horizontal arrangement echoes the balanced left-right structure of the original booking controls. On smaller screens, these two inputs stack vertically while maintaining the same left alignment and spacing. A 24–32px vertical gap separates this form from the delivery-method selector below, preserving the comfortable density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming a central step in the vertical flow, with responsive internal layout that shifts from a two-column to a single-column stack on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery method selector sits immediately below the amount-and-quantity section, separated by a clean band of whitespace approximately 24–32px high. It continues to fill the full internal width of the configurator card within the same centered column. This section visually echoes the card type selector, reinforcing the idea of choosing between modes: it has a left-aligned section title and, beneath it, a horizontal arrangement of delivery options (such as email or physical card). These options appear as pill-shaped or compact card-style toggles laid out in a single row that spans about 70–80% of the section width, centered horizontally with 12–16px gaps between each option. The selection state uses gold outlines or fills to reflect the brand’s warm, premium visual language. Internal padding of 16–20px surrounds the content so the toggles don’t press against the edges. The overall height of this section is visually lower than the preceding amount form, signaling it as a relatively quick choice. There is a slightly larger gap (around 32px) below this section before entering the more detailed recipient information, creating a visual pause in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical stack, with horizontally arranged options on desktop that collapse into a vertical list on smaller screens while maintaining centered alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipient details form sits in the middle of the configurator card stack, directly beneath the delivery method selector with about 32px of vertical space, marking the transition from quick selections to more detailed inputs. This section spans the entire width of the card interior, with a slightly taller vertical footprint than the selector sections above. It is framed as a soft rectangular block with 20–24px internal padding and, optionally, a subtle top border or gold accent line to anchor it visually, reminiscent of the gold status bar but in a lighter, form-appropriate tone. The heading is left-aligned, and underneath, the fields (such as recipient name, email, personalized message) are arranged in a two-column layout on larger screens: core identity fields (name, email) on the left and optional or secondary fields (message, occasion) on the right, each column covering about 48–50% of the section width with a 16–24px gap. Inputs stack vertically within each column with 8–16px gaps. On narrow viewports, the layout collapses into a single column, preserving the order and generous spacing. This section ends with about 24–32px of vertical spacing before the purchaser details form begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the central, more detailed body of the configurator; it grows vertically as needed but always respects the same internal padding and column behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly below the recipient details, separated by roughly 24–32px of whitespace, the purchaser details form mirrors the width and internal structure of the recipient block to create a visually balanced pair. It fills the same centered column inside the configurator card, with 20–24px padding around the content and a left-aligned section title. Fields such as purchaser name, email, and possibly billing or contact information are arranged in a similar two-column grid on desktop (about 48–50% width per column, 16–24px between columns) to maintain alignment with the section above. This repetition anchors the eye and keeps the interface feeling orderly and premium. Vertically, inputs in each column are separated by 8–16px, matching the established rhythm. The bottom of this section is given a slightly larger vertical gap (around 32–40px) before the summary panel begins, signaling the end of data-entry steps and preparing the user for a review/confirmation phase.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the stack, aligned to create a continuous vertical column of forms that transitions naturally into the summary and add-to-cart area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift card summary panel sits directly below the purchaser details form, separated from it by a visible band of whitespace (approximately 32–40px) to clearly mark the shift from form entry to review. It spans the full internal width of the configurator card but is shallower in height, visually echoing the gold status bar from the booking hero. The panel is a low-profile bar with a warm gold or gold-tinted background and 12–16px of padding on all sides, running horizontally across the card. Text within is left-aligned and may be organized into two or three concise lines summarizing the selected card type, amount, quantity, and chosen delivery method. On larger screens, brief key-value pairs can be aligned in a single row, with subtle spacing, making the bar feel information-rich but not overloaded. To the far right of the bar, aligned with the card’s right edge, a slightly bolder line of text (such as the current total value) is placed, creating a subtle left-to-right emphasis that pulls the eye toward the final action area below. The summary panel visually connects to the add-to-cart form beneath it, with a smaller vertical gap (about 16–20px), forming a tight cluster akin to the status strip plus booking widget arrangement from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, acting as a fixed checkpoint in the vertical flow right before the primary action; it scrolls with the content but could optionally be designed to remain visible at the bottom edge of the configurator card on very long forms without changing its basic alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart form is positioned immediately beneath the summary panel with a modest 16–20px vertical gap, forming the final, most action-oriented block in the configurator card. This component echoes the original booking widget both in placement and structure: it spans the full internal width of the card, is slightly taller than the summary bar, and is framed by a clear gold outline with a subtle drop shadow, making it feel like a distinct, elevated control bar resting on the lower portion of the hero image. Inside, padding of 20–24px ensures comfortable separation between elements. On desktop, its internal layout is horizontal: on the left, concise explanatory text (e.g., a short reassurance line or label like "Your gift card") occupies about 40% of the width; in the middle, the key price or total along with any small supporting notes (such as tax or currency) occupies another ~30%; on the right, a prominent dark green primary button with gold accents labeled "Add to Cart" or similar occupies the remaining ~30%, right-aligned and slightly taller than the text blocks. This creates a strong Z-shaped final glance: from the summary text above, down to the left label, across to the price, and finally to the rightmost CTA. On smaller screens, these elements stack vertically: informational text first, then total, then the full-width button at the bottom, with 8–12px vertical gaps. The bottom of this component has extra breathing room (32–48px) before the end of the hero/content area, allowing it to feel decisively "final" without being cramped against the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static but visually anchored to the bottom of the configurator card; it scrolls with the page, maintaining its position as the last element in the central column, and acts as the culmination of the entire flow without any floating or sticky behavior by default.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Page to review and manage current gift card items in the cart, adjust quantities, remove items, see order totals, and proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load and display the current cart contents using getCartSummary, including all gift card CartItem entries and computed totals.
Show detailed information for each gift card item such as item type identifier, gift card amount, recipient details, delivery method, send date, quantity, unit price, and line total.
Allow users to change the quantity of existing cart items by calling updateCartItemQuantity and then refreshing the cart summary.
Allow users to remove cart items entirely by calling removeCartItem and then refreshing the cart summary.
Display subtotal, taxes (if returned), and grand total for the cart based on the totals object from getCartSummary.
Provide navigation controls to proceed to checkout (checkout.html?checkoutType=cart) or continue shopping on the gift cards page (gift_cards.html).
Handle empty-cart states by indicating that the cart has no items and guiding users back to the gift cards page.
Surface operation feedback (e.g., success or error messages related to updates/removals) based on URL parameters and interface responses.
user_workflows:
View and review cart contents:
1. User navigates to cart.html (from site header cart link or after adding a gift card).
2. cart-page-controller component calls getCartSummary with no parameters on initial load.
3. On success, the cart-item-list and cart-totals-summary components render the returned items and totals.
4. If the items array is empty, the cart-empty-state component is shown with a link to gift_cards.html.
5. If URL parameters indicate a recently added or updated item, the cart-item-list component highlights that specific item row for the current session.

Update quantity of a gift card in the cart:
1. User locates a gift card item in the cart-item-list component.
2. User changes the quantity via a quantity input control associated with that item.
3. On quantity change confirmation (e.g., blur or explicit update action), cart-item-list triggers updateCartItemQuantity with parameters: cartItemId (from the item) and quantity (from the input).
4. On successful response, cart-page-controller calls getCartSummary again to obtain updated items and totals.
5. cart-item-list and cart-totals-summary re-render with updated data; cart-feedback-banner may display a success message, and URL parameters can be updated to reflect the completed operation.
6. On error, cart-feedback-banner displays an error message; no local changes are persisted until a successful retry.

Remove a gift card item from the cart:
1. User clicks the remove control on a specific item in the cart-item-list component.
2. cart-item-list triggers removeCartItem with parameter: cartItemId of the selected item.
3. On successful response, cart-page-controller calls getCartSummary to refresh the cart state and totals.
4. If the removed item was the last one, cart-empty-state is shown instead of the item list, and cart-actions-bar updates accordingly (e.g., disabling checkout navigation if needed).
5. cart-feedback-banner can display a removal confirmation; URL parameters can be updated to represent the outcome (e.g., status=removed and lastRemovedItemId in the query string).
6. On error, cart-feedback-banner shows an error message and the item remains visible.

Proceed to checkout from the cart:
1. After confirming the cart contents, the user clicks the 'Proceed to Checkout' control in the cart-actions-bar component.
2. cart-actions-bar verifies that at least one cart item exists and that totals are available.
3. The browser is redirected to checkout.html with the URL parameter checkoutType=cart.
4. Any additional context for that step is handled on checkout.html; the cart page does not modify cart contents at this stage.

Continue shopping / add another gift card:
1. User clicks the 'Continue Shopping' or equivalent control in cart-actions-bar or cart-empty-state.
2. Browser navigates to gift_cards.html with no additional URL parameters required by this page.
3. The server/session maintains cart state so that returning to cart.html later via the header cart link or other navigation will still show current items using getCartSummary.

Handle initial state and reloads:
1. On every navigation to cart.html, cart-page-controller reads URL parameters relevant to state (e.g., operation status, highlightItemId).
2. cart-page-controller calls getCartSummary to retrieve the current cart, items, and totals.
3. Based on both the loaded data and any URL parameters, the page decides whether to display normal cart view, empty-cart view, and which feedback messages or highlights to show.
4. When the user performs operations (update/remove), the page may update the current URL query string via history APIs to reflect the latest operation state without reloading the page.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- On page load of cart.html, cart-page-controller triggers an automatic call to getCartSummary.
- While waiting for the response, cart-loading-state shows a loading indicator.
- On success, the resulting cart, items, and totals are passed down to child components (cart-item-list, cart-totals-summary, cart-actions-bar, cart-empty-state). On failure, cart-error-state displays an error and a retry option.

Quantity change interaction:
- User adjusts quantity in an input associated with a CartItem row within cart-item-list.
- onQuantityChange/onQuantityBlur handler validates the new value according to allowed business rules (e.g., must be a valid positive integer) without hardcoding business-specific numeric thresholds.
- If valid, cart-item-list calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity).
- On success, cart-page-controller refreshes the cart via getCartSummary; on error, cart-feedback-banner displays an error and the input is reverted to the last confirmed quantity.

Remove item interaction:
- User clicks a remove or delete control in a CartItem row.
- onRemoveClick may prompt the user for confirmation according to product rules.
- On confirmation, cart-item-list calls removeCartItem(cartItemId).
- On success, cart-page-controller refreshes data via getCartSummary and cart-feedback-banner can show a confirmation; on error, an error message is shown and the item remains visible.

Proceed to checkout interaction:
- User clicks 'Proceed to Checkout' button in cart-actions-bar.
- onClick handler checks that the current items array (from state) is not empty and that totals.total is available.
- If cart is non-empty, the browser navigates to checkout.html?checkoutType=cart.
- If cart is empty or totals are unavailable, the handler prevents navigation and may display a message via cart-feedback-banner.

Continue shopping interaction:
- User clicks 'Continue Shopping' link or button.
- onClick handler navigates directly to gift_cards.html without altering the cart data.
- No API calls are needed for this interaction from the cart page.

Retry on error interaction:
- If getCartSummary fails (e.g., network or server error), cart-error-state shows a retry control.
- User clicks retry; onClick handler triggers getCartSummary again.
- Success re-renders the normal cart view; failure can update URL parameters with an error code to support diagnostics.

URL-based feedback and highlighting:
- When cart.html is loaded with certain query parameters (e.g., status, highlightItemId, errorCode), cart-page-controller parses them.
- highlightItemId is passed to cart-item-list, which then emphasizes the corresponding CartItem row once data is loaded.
- status and/or errorCode are passed to cart-feedback-banner to show context-specific messages (e.g., indicating that an item was recently added or that a previous checkout attempt failed).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The cart page relies on URL parameters to manage and reflect state transitions between navigations, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage. Example parameters that cart.html can interpret include: 
- highlightItemId: When present (e.g., cart.html?highlightItemId=<cartItemId>), after getCartSummary resolves, cart-page-controller passes this identifier to cart-item-list. If an item in the loaded items array has a matching id, that row is highlighted to draw attention (for instance, when returning from gift_cards.html after adding an item). The highlighting is a view concern only and does not affect API calls.
- status: When present (e.g., status=added|updated|removed|error), cart-page-controller forwards this value to cart-feedback-banner to determine which informational or error message to display. This allows other pages such as gift_cards.html or checkout.html to communicate outcome messages back to cart.html when redirecting. The status parameter does not alter the API calls themselves; getCartSummary remains the source of truth for cart contents.
- errorCode: When present (e.g., errorCode=<server-defined-code>), cart-feedback-banner can show more specific error details, and the combination of status=error and errorCode may indicate why a previous action failed. These codes are purely descriptive from the cart page's point of view and do not change the behavior of getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, or removeCartItem.
For outgoing navigation, the cart page uses URL parameters as defined by the navigation configuration: proceeding to checkout always navigates to checkout.html with checkoutType=cart (checkout.html?checkoutType=cart), and continuing shopping always navigates to gift_cards.html without additional required parameters. Within the current page, after successful quantity updates or item removals, the cart-page-controller may update the current URL's query string (e.g., setting status=updated or status=removed and lastAffectedItemId=<id>) using history APIs to keep the URL in sync with the user's last operation, but all authoritative cart data continues to come from the assigned interfaces.
Overall, page state is derived from a combination of: (1) the most recent responses from getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, and removeCartItem; and (2) the current URL parameters interpreted as hints for UI messaging and focus. No assumptions are made via hardcoded values about prices, currencies, totals, or available item types; all such information is taken from API responses.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, listens to URL parameter changes, orchestrates calls to getCartSummary, and propagates cart data and operation results to child components.
Displays a loading indicator while getCartSummary is in progress and hides it once data or an error is available.
Shows an error message and a retry control when getCartSummary fails or data cannot be rendered, allowing the user to retry loading the cart.
Displays contextual feedback messages related to cart actions (e.g., item added, quantity updated, item removed, or errors), driven by both URL parameters and recent API results.

Renders the list of CartItem entries returned by getCartSummary, including gift card details, quantity controls, item identifiers, pricing, and action controls for updating quantity and removing items.
For each CartItem row, provides an input for changing the quantity and triggers updateCartItemQuantity when the user confirms a new valid quantity.
For each CartItem row, provides an action to remove that item from the cart by calling removeCartItem.

Displays the subtotal, taxes, and total for the current cart using the totals object from getCartSummary and keeps totals in sync after updates/removals by re-rendering when cart-page-controller data changes.
If the items array from getCartSummary is empty, replaces the item list and totals with a simple message indicating that the cart is empty and a control to navigate back to gift_cards.html.

Provides primary actions at the bottom or side of the cart: a control to continue shopping (navigating to gift_cards.html) and a primary button to proceed to checkout (navigating to checkout.html with checkoutType=cart), with enable/disable behavior based on cart contents.

Encapsulates calls to the updateCartItemQuantity interface, mapping user interactions (cartItemId and new quantity) into API requests and propagating success or error states back to cart-page-controller and cart-item-list.

Encapsulates calls to the removeCartItem interface, mapping user interactions (selected cartItemId) into API requests and propagating success or error states back to cart-page-controller and cart-item-list.
Handles the invocation of getCartSummary on page load and on explicit refresh requests, normalizes the returned cart, items, and totals, and updates cart-page-controller state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The cart page uses the same single-hero, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. Global header/nav remains a full-width dark green bar at the top (outside these components). Within the hero, content is centered in a middle column (about 60–70% of viewport width) and stacked vertically: error/feedback banners near the top, then a centered cart title area (mirroring the tagline treatment with subtle gold dividers), followed by a slim status/feedback strip if present. Below that, a large, softly elevated cart panel sits over the interior-photo background; inside that panel, the cart item list occupies the upper portion and the totals + actions band anchors the lower portion, echoing the original booking-widget band. Empty and loading states take over the central panel region as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The cart page uses the same single-hero, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. Global header/nav remains a full-width dark green bar at the top (outside these components). Within the hero, content is centered in a middle column (about 60–70% of viewport width) and stacked vertically: error/feedback banners near the top, then a centered cart title area (mirroring the tagline treatment with subtle gold dividers), followed by a slim status/feedback strip if present. Below that, a large, softly elevated cart panel sits over the interior-photo background; inside that panel, the cart item list occupies the upper portion and the totals + actions band anchors the lower portion, echoing the original booking-widget band. Empty and loading states take over the central panel region as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero (from the bottom of the global header to just above the fold) fills roughly 80–90% of the viewport height on desktop, with the main cart panel visually centered vertically. The top 15–20% of that area is reserved for the title and banners; the main cart panel takes about 40–50% of the viewport height, leaving comfortable background breathing room above and below. Horizontally, the central content column is constrained to 60–70% of the viewport, aligning with a 12-column grid. Within the cart panel, the item list uses roughly the top two-thirds of the panel; the totals-summary plus actions-bar share the bottom third in a horizontal band. Vertical gaps between major blocks (title ↔ banners, banners ↔ cart panel, item list ↔ totals band) follow the 24–40px rhythm from the mockup; internal padding inside cards and bands sits around 16–24px on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains the mockup’s moderately spacious density. Only one major focal block (the cart panel) competes for attention within the hero, with clear separation between support elements (banners, status) and primary interactive content (item list, totals, actions). Each cart item row has enough vertical padding (12–16px) to feel premium and uncramped, but rows stack tightly enough to keep several items visible without scrolling. The bottom totals-and-actions band is clearly defined but not oversized, balancing functionality against visual calm. Overall, the feeling is rich and considered rather than minimal or busy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The page preserves the Z-pattern from the mockup. The eye enters at the top-left logo in the global header and moves to the top-right navigation/BOOK CTA. From there, it drops diagonally toward the centered cart title and decorative gold dividers, then moves down to any status/feedback strip. The next anchor is the central cart panel, where attention first lands on the top-left of the item list (item names and thumbnails), then travels rightwards across each row to the quantity and remove controls. Finally, the eye is pulled down to the bottom of the panel where the totals-summary appears on the left and the primary checkout button in the actions-bar sits to the right, echoing the original booking widget’s strong rightmost CTA emphasis.

layout.overall_description: The cart page appears as a refined, single-section hero over the same warm interior-photo background used across the site. A dark green header with the boutique badge logo and navigation floats at the very top, slightly overlapping the hero. Below this, within a centered content column, a cart title such as “Your Gift Card Cart” is horizontally centered with thin gold divider lines to its left and right, mirroring the home tagline treatment and anchoring the user’s focus in the middle of the image. Immediately beneath, any global feedback or error bars sit as slim, full-width strips within this column, subtly framed by gold or muted-red accents. A generous gap (around 32px) separates these from the main cart content panel: a large, soft-cream or off‑white card resting over the bed image, with rounded corners and a faint gold edge, occupying roughly two-thirds of the viewport width and around half its height. Inside this panel, the top section lists gift card items in a clean, vertical stack, with item titles and details aligned left and functional controls (quantity steppers and remove links) aligned to the right. A horizontal divider line then leads into a bottom band that spans the full width of the panel: the left side summarises totals and fees in a structured column, while the right side contains the primary checkout button and secondary cart actions, styled similarly to the original dark green BOOK button in the booking widget. If the cart is empty, the item list is replaced by a centered empty-state card that occupies the same footprint, with ample padding and an inviting message. Throughout, the central column alignment, vertical rhythm, and use of gold accents tie the cart page back to the original hero’s Parisian, boutique-hotel atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-controller occupies the full viewport width and height, sitting directly beneath the global dark green header bar. It establishes the hero background (the cozy interior photo), applies the 12-column centered grid, and defines the primary content column (about 60–70% of viewport width, centered). Within this column, it vertically stacks the cart-specific components in order: error state (if present), feedback banner (if present), the page title/decorative area (handled outside these components), then the main cart content zone, which houses either the cart panel (item list + totals + actions) or the empty state. The controller also manages overlay states: when loading, it dims or replaces the cart panel region with the loading indicator while keeping the header and background visible; when errors occur, it reserves space near the top of the content column for the error-display without shifting the global header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-page layout wrapper in normal document flow, defining the central content column and a hero-like section; coordinates conditional rendering and overlays for loading and error states but is itself non-visual.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-loading-state appears centered within the main cart content area controlled by the cart-page-controller. When active, it either replaces the cart panel or overlays it with a semi-opaque dark green or warm translucent scrim that covers roughly the central 60–70% content column area from just below the title region down to where the cart actions would normally sit. A spinner or subtle loading icon is placed precisely at the visual center of this scrim, aligned with the midpoint of the hero, ensuring the user’s attention remains in the middle of the page and in line with the logo/tagline axis from the mockup. Adequate padding (at least 40px) is left between the loading overlay edges and the panel border so the underlying card silhouette is still hinted at, preserving the structured feel of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the main cart content region as a centered modal-like layer, while the rest of the page (header, background image) remains visible and static beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-error-state is rendered as a prominent horizontal bar at the top of the centered content column, just below the global header and above the cart title area. It spans the full width of the column (about 60–70% of the viewport) and has generous vertical padding (16–20px) inside, echoing the status strip from the mockup but using a muted red or deep plum tone with a fine gold or off‑white border to remain on-brand. The text is left-aligned within the bar, while any close/dismiss icon aligns to the right, following the top bar’s left-to-right reading flow. Vertically, it occupies roughly 8–10% of the hero height when shown, with around 16–24px of whitespace separating it from the elements above and below (header above, title/banner area below), ensuring it is noticeable but not overwhelming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal document flow but is pinned to the top of the content column; when present, it pushes the cart title and main panel slightly downward rather than overlapping them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-feedback-banner is positioned directly beneath the cart title/decorative area and above the main cart panel, within the same centered content column. It spans the full width of that column and is visually reminiscent of the gold availability/status bar in the mockup: a slim horizontal strip with a warm gold background or gold border and off‑white interior, around 40–56px tall, with 16px internal padding. Its message is centered or left-aligned depending on copy length, but always aligned to the main vertical axis of the page. It sits with a comfortable 16–24px margin above and below, acting as a visual bridge between the airy title region and the more functional cart panel. When not needed, its space collapses so that the cart panel moves up and the vertical rhythm is preserved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed in normal document flow as a full-width strip within the central column; appears and disappears dynamically without overlaying other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list lives inside the main cart panel, occupying the upper portion of a large, centered card that sits over the background image. The parent panel spans about 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered; within it, the item list takes up roughly the top two-thirds of the panel’s vertical space on desktop. Items are stacked vertically with each row extending nearly the full width of the panel, minus comfortable horizontal padding (16–24px left and right). Each item row is divided into three logical zones: on the left, an area occupying about 60% of the row width for the gift card name, short description, and any thumbnail, aligned to the left edge; in the middle-right, a compact area for the cart-item-quantity-control; and on the far right, a narrow column for price and the cart-item-remove-control, aligned flush right. Vertical spacing between items is around 16–24px, with a light divider line or subtle separation to keep the list readable without feeling crowded. When the list is long, this upper section can scroll within the panel while the bottom totals/actions band remains visible, preserving the structured, anchored feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow inside the main centered cart panel, occupying the upper section; may become internally scrollable if its content exceeds the available panel height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each cart item row, the cart-item-quantity-control is placed in the middle-right zone, between the main item details on the left and the price/remove area on the right. It is aligned vertically to the center of the row’s content and horizontally occupies about 15–20% of the row width. Visually, it appears as a compact horizontal stepper (e.g., minus button, numeric value, plus button) or a styled numeric input, enclosed in a small chip-like container with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, echoing the button sizing specified in the spacing system. Its background is light (off‑white) with a thin, refined border in a muted gold or soft grey to keep it premium. There is a consistent 8–12px gap between this control and both the item details to the left and the price/remove controls to the right, maintaining a clear three-column rhythm in each row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the natural flow of each list row; it does not float or stick, but maintains its middle-right position even as rows wrap responsively (stacking under item details on smaller screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-remove-control sits within the rightmost portion of each cart item row, sharing space with the item’s price. It is right-aligned, positioned either directly beneath the price or to its right, depending on layout width, but always anchored to the row’s right padding edge to reinforce the right-hand action convention seen in the mockup’s BOOK CTA. The control is styled as a subtle tertiary action—such as a text link or small icon button—with minimal yet precise padding (around 8px horizontally) so it is easy to tap without dominating the visual hierarchy. There is a small vertical gap (4–8px) between the price and the remove control if stacked, or a horizontal gap of similar size if placed side by side. This keeps the row clean while making the delete action discoverable at the far end of the user’s left-to-right scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each item row’s rightmost column; follows normal flow without special positioning, but remains visually anchored to the right edge on all screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-totals-summary is located at the bottom of the main cart panel, forming the left side of a horizontal band that echoes the booking widget from the mockup. This band spans the full width of the panel and is separated from the item list above by a thin divider line and 16–24px of vertical padding. The totals summary itself occupies approximately 55–60% of the band’s width on desktop and is left-aligned, displaying subtotal, taxes, fees, and total in a compact vertical stack. Each line has 8–12px vertical spacing, with the grand total styled slightly larger or bolder, but still within the refined aesthetic. The background of this band can be subtly distinct (e.g., slightly warmer or lighter than the rest of the panel) to visually group the summary and actions together, reminiscent of the gold-outlined booking widget frame. The right portion of this band is reserved for the cart-actions-bar, with the totals-summary and actions forming a clear two-column layout within the band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the bottom of the centered cart panel in normal document flow; remains visible as the item list above scrolls within the panel if there are many items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the cart-empty-state replaces the usual cart panel contents and becomes the primary focal card in the hero. It is centered both horizontally within the 60–70% width content column and vertically within the hero area, occupying roughly 40–50% of the viewport height. The empty state appears as a refined card with generous padding (24–32px on all sides), a soft-cream background, and a thin gold border or accent, echoing the logo badge’s premium feel. Its message and any illustrative icon are center-aligned along the same vertical axis as the header logo and page title, ensuring continuity with the mockup’s central hero focus. Below the main message, a single primary button (e.g., “Browse Gift Cards”) can be centered, using a dark green fill and the same visual weight as the BOOK button in the booking widget. There is ample breathing room between the empty-state card and the edges of the background image to maintain the boutique, airy aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in normal document flow but conditionally replaces the item list + totals + actions inside the main content region; remains centered within the hero when shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-actions-bar shares the bottom band of the main cart panel with the cart-totals-summary, occupying the rightmost 40–45% of that band on desktop. It is horizontally aligned so that its primary action (e.g., “Proceed to Checkout”) sits at the far right edge, mirroring the strong rightmost BOOK CTA from the mockup’s top navigation and booking widget. Secondary actions (like “Continue Shopping” or “Clear Cart”) are aligned to the left of the primary button within this same right-hand area, separated by 8–16px horizontal gaps. Buttons follow the spacing guidance: 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, with the primary button in solid dark green and perhaps a subtle gold outline, and secondary actions more subdued. On smaller screens, the actions-bar stacks below the totals-summary within the band but remains visually grouped through consistent background and padding, preserving the feeling of a single cohesive strip of controls at the base of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in normal document flow inside the bottom band of the centered cart panel; effectively anchored to the lower-right corner of the main content area and remains visible as the item list scrolls above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This interface adapter runs entirely behind the scenes, with no direct visual representation. It is conceptually tied to the cart-item-quantity-control, triggering updates whenever the quantity control is used. Visually, its effects manifest through subtle UI changes—such as a brief state change in the associated item row, an update to the cart-totals-summary, and possibly a short-lived cart-feedback-banner—but the adapter itself does not occupy any space on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background process with no spatial footprint; it only influences the layout indirectly by causing visible components to update or briefly show loading/feedback states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This adapter is linked logically to each cart-item-remove-control but has no visible UI of its own. When a remove action is triggered, it coordinates the removal of the corresponding row from the cart-item-list and prompts updates to the cart-totals-summary and possibly the cart-feedback-banner. The only visible spatial effects are the smooth collapse of the removed item’s row within the cart-item-list and any updates in the totals band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; does not occupy layout space. Its impact on space is indirect, through the addition or removal of rows from the cart-item-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This adapter underpins the cart-totals-summary and initial cart state but does not appear as a visual component. It fetches and refreshes the data that populates the summary panel and may trigger the cart-loading-state during initial load or major updates. Any spatial change comes from its influence on visible components, such as revealing the totals band once data is loaded or updating values within the same footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background adapter; it does not render any UI and has no direct spatial behavior beyond causing other visible components to appear, update, or hide.

name: Meetings & Events
title: Meetings & Events
description: Page for viewing meeting and event spaces, related amenities, and starting a meeting or event proposal request.

core_features: Load meetings and events overview content using getMeetingsOverview, including introductory text, a list of spaces, highlight amenities, and call-to-action text.
Display a structured list of meeting and event spaces with capacity and descriptions from getMeetingsOverview.spaces.
Display a list of highlight amenities for meetings and events from getMeetingsOverview.highlightAmenities.

Provide one or more call-to-action buttons that use getMeetingsOverview.callToActionText and navigate to meeting_inquiry.html with the appropriate URL parameters (including eventType=corporate_meeting).
Allow users to focus on a specific space using a URL parameter so direct links can open the page with a given space already selected.
user_workflows:
View meeting and event spaces:
1. User navigates to meetings_events.html.
2. The page calls getMeetingsOverview with no parameters.
3. When the response arrives, the page displays introText, spaces, highlightAmenities, and callToActionText.
4. The user scrolls through the spaces list. Each space item shows name, capacity, description, and image (all from the spaces array).
5. The user may click a space in the list to mark it as selected; the selected space is highlighted or expanded.

Open the page with a preselected space:
1. An external link points to meetings_events.html with a query parameter spaceIndex set (e.g., meetings_events.html?spaceIndex=1).
2. On load, the page reads spaceIndex from the URL.
3. After getMeetingsOverview resolves, the page validates that spaceIndex is within the bounds of the spaces array.
4. If valid, that space is set as the selected/focused space in the spaces list; otherwise the first space in the list is selected by default.
5. The UI scrolls or focuses to the selected space so the user immediately sees the relevant details.

Focus the amenities view:
1. An external link (or internal navigation element) points to meetings_events.html with query parameter section=amenities.
2. On load, the page reads section from the URL.
3. After data is loaded, if section=amenities, the page scrolls to or emphasizes the amenities list area.
4. If the user navigates back to the spaces section via a control in the UI, the page updates the section URL parameter (e.g., section=spaces) using history.pushState or history.replaceState.

Request a proposal / plan an event from the overview page:
1. User browses spaces and, optionally, selects one space as the current focus.
2. User clicks the primary call-to-action button that displays text from getMeetingsOverview.callToActionText.
3. The click handler constructs a URL to meeting_inquiry.html.
4. The URL always includes eventType=corporate_meeting as required by the navigation specification.
5. If a space is selected, the handler optionally appends additional URL parameters (e.g., spaceIndex or spaceName encoded) so the inquiry page can prefill or understand which space the user is interested in.
6. The browser navigates to meeting_inquiry.html with the constructed parameters.

Change the selected space and keep it shareable:
1. While viewing the spaces list, the user clicks a different space entry.
2. The click handler updates the internal selectedSpaceIndex state.
3. The handler updates the page URL’s query string to reflect the new selection (e.g., updating spaceIndex without a full page reload).
4. If the user shares or bookmarks the URL, another visitor opening that URL will see the same space selected after getMeetingsOverview loads.
interactions:
Page load:
- Trigger: Browser loads meetings_events.html.
- Action: Call getMeetingsOverview.
- When resolved: Render introText, spaces list, amenities list, and CTA text. Apply initial selection and section based on URL parameters (spaceIndex, section).

Select a space in the list:
- Trigger: User clicks on a space item in the spaces list.
- Action: Set selectedSpaceIndex to the index of the clicked space; update the URL query parameter spaceIndex to match this index; adjust the view to highlight or expand that space.

Navigate between main sections (spaces vs amenities):
- Trigger: User clicks a control to jump between the spaces list and amenities list (e.g., a simple tab or in-page link).
- Action: Scroll to the relevant section; update the section query parameter in the URL (section=spaces or section=amenities).

Click primary call-to-action (Request Proposal / Plan an Event):
- Trigger: User clicks the main CTA button, which displays getMeetingsOverview.callToActionText.
- Action: Build a navigation URL for meeting_inquiry.html including eventType=corporate_meeting and optionally spaceIndex or encoded information about the currently selected space; navigate to that URL.

Click secondary CTA per space (if implemented):
- Trigger: User clicks a per-space CTA within a space item.
- Action: Build a navigation URL for meeting_inquiry.html including eventType=corporate_meeting and a parameter indicating the space index or identifier; navigate to that URL.

React to URL parameter changes via browser navigation:
- Trigger: User uses browser back/forward which changes the URL query string (spaceIndex and/or section) without full reload when using history API.
- Action: The page reads the updated URL parameters and updates selectedSpaceIndex and active section accordingly, keeping the view synchronized with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is controlled via URL query parameters and the getMeetingsOverview response. On initial load, the page parses the current window.location.search for the following parameters: (1) spaceIndex: an integer index corresponding to a position in the spaces array returned by getMeetingsOverview.spaces. If present and valid (0 ≤ spaceIndex < spaces.length), this value is used as the selectedSpaceIndex for the spaces list; if invalid or missing, selectedSpaceIndex defaults to 0 when spaces is non-empty. (2) section: a string that can indicate which main section of the page should be emphasized (for example 'spaces' or 'amenities'). If section equals 'amenities', the page scrolls to or highlights the amenities section after data is loaded; any other value or absence of section results in the spaces section being considered primary. After getMeetingsOverview resolves, the page recomputes any derived state that depends on the spaces array length or structure, then applies spaceIndex and section as described.
Internal state variables (e.g., selectedSpaceIndex and activeSection) mirror these URL parameters. When the user interacts (by selecting a space or jumping sections), the page updates both the internal state and the URL query string using the History API (pushState or replaceState) without reloading the page. This ensures that the URL remains a shareable representation of the current state.
For outgoing navigation, when the user clicks any proposal-related CTA, the target URL meeting_inquiry.html is constructed with at least one mandatory query parameter: eventType=corporate_meeting. Additional parameters may be included, such as spaceIndex (carrying the currently selected space index) so the meeting_inquiry.html page can infer the user's space of interest. No state is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; the URL is the single source of navigational state, and data is always retrieved fresh via getMeetingsOverview when meetings_events.html loads.

components.functionality: Fetches meetings and events overview information using getMeetingsOverview on page load, parses URL parameters to initialize page-level state (selectedSpaceIndex and activeSection), and passes the loaded data and derived state down to child components.
Displays the introductory description text for the meetings and events page using the introText field from getMeetingsOverview; renders nothing or a placeholder until data is loaded.

Renders the list of meeting and event spaces from getMeetingsOverview.spaces, allowing selection of a space based on the selectedSpaceIndex and user clicks. Each list item shows data from its corresponding space object (name, capacity, description, imageUrl). When a space is clicked, it becomes the selected space and the component notifies parent state logic so the URL parameter spaceIndex is updated.

Shows more detailed information for the currently selected space, derived from getMeetingsOverview.spaces[selectedSpaceIndex]. It updates automatically when selectedSpaceIndex changes or when new data arrives. It may include a space-specific call-to-action button that navigates to meeting_inquiry.html with eventType=corporate_meeting and parameters corresponding to the selected space.

Displays the list of highlighted meeting amenities by iterating over getMeetingsOverview.highlightAmenities. Each item is rendered as a simple amenity entry. The component can be used as a scroll target when the URL parameter section is set to 'amenities'.

Displays a primary call-to-action button that uses getMeetingsOverview.callToActionText as its label. When clicked, it constructs a URL to meeting_inquiry.html including eventType=corporate_meeting and, if available, a parameter representing the currently selected space (e.g., spaceIndex). It then navigates to this URL.

Provides controls to switch focus between the spaces list and the amenities list. It reads and updates the activeSection state (derived from the section URL parameter). When a control is used, it scrolls to the relevant section and updates the URL parameter section accordingly.

Listens for changes in selectedSpaceIndex and activeSection and updates the browser URL query parameters (spaceIndex and section) accordingly without reloading the page. Also listens to browser navigation events (back/forward) to reapply state based on updated URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the hero-centric, vertically stacked structure from the mockup: a full-width hero band with an interior-photo background, within which the Meetings & Events intro text and primary event CTA panel are centered in a single main column. Directly beneath the hero, a slim, full-width in-page section navigation replicates the overlaid header/navigation bar pattern. Below that, the core content flows top-to-bottom inside a 60–70% width centered column: first the spaces list paired with the space detail panel in a master–detail band, then the amenities list as its own section, and finally the event CTA reappears or extends as a concluding prompt. All interactive content follows a linear vertical rhythm with occasional side-by-side arrangements that still stay within the central content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the hero-centric, vertically stacked structure from the mockup: a full-width hero band with an interior-photo background, within which the Meetings & Events intro text and primary event CTA panel are centered in a single main column. Directly beneath the hero, a slim, full-width in-page section navigation replicates the overlaid header/navigation bar pattern. Below that, the core content flows top-to-bottom inside a 60–70% width centered column: first the spaces list paired with the space detail panel in a master–detail band, then the amenities list as its own section, and finally the event CTA reappears or extends as a concluding prompt. All interactive content follows a linear vertical rhythm with occasional side-by-side arrangements that still stay within the central content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero (intro text + event CTA) takes roughly the top 45–55% of the first viewport height, leaving visible breathing room above and below the content cluster against the background image. The section navigation bar is shallow (about 8–10% of viewport height), hugging the top once the user scrolls. The spaces + detail band consumes most of the mid-page area, with the left column (spaces list) around 35–40% of the central column width and the right column (space detail panel) around 60–65%. The amenities list occupies one full-width section of the central column, about 20–30% of the scrollable page height depending on content. Vertical spacing between major blocks mirrors the original mockup: 24–40px gaps between sections, and 16–24px internal padding in each card/band.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains moderately spacious. Each component is separated by clear vertical whitespace and padded internally so individual blocks feel like distinct, well-framed cards or strips, echoing the status bar and booking widget from the mockup. No section feels cramped: the hero center stack (intro + CTA) has generous top and bottom padding within the hero, the master–detail band balances information density with breathing room, and the amenities chips/list items are grouped but not tightly packed. Overall the page feels rich and contentful without tipping into clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the Z-pattern from the mockup. On entry, the eye is first caught by the global header (outside these components) at the top, then drawn diagonally down to the centered Meetings & Events intro in the hero. From there, attention moves down to the prominent, horizontally oriented event CTA panel (analogous to the booking widget). As the user scrolls, the sticky section navigation bar anchors the top of the viewport, while the eye moves left-to-right across the spaces list and into the larger space detail panel on the right. Beneath that, the flow continues down into the amenities list and ends on a reaffirming CTA, encouraging an event inquiry. Background photography and generous side margins subtly recenter the gaze on the middle column throughout.

layout.overall_description: The Meetings & Events page presents as a warm, full-width hero with a cozy interior photo stretching edge-to-edge, overlaid by a centered content column on desktop. In this hero, the Meetings & Events title and a short, refined intro paragraph sit in the visual center, framed by subtle gold divider details that echo the original tagline treatment. Just below, a horizontally oriented event CTA panel, outlined in gold, rests over the lower portion of the image much like the booking widget in the mockup—its left side holding inviting copy and its right side featuring a prominent dark green action button. Scrolling slightly reveals a slim section navigation bar spanning the full width but aligning its links with the central column; it becomes sticky at the top of the viewport, echoing the overlaid header in the hero design. Below this, the content column hosts a master–detail band: a vertical list of spaces on the left and a larger detail card on the right, both sitting on a softly tinted background to distinguish them from the hero. Further down, the amenities section returns to a single-column layout, presenting grouped amenities with enough padding and gold accents to feel premium yet readable. The page concludes with the event CTA again accessible in the lower fold, keeping the primary action never far from view. Throughout, dark green, gold touches, and generous spacing maintain the boutique, Parisian-inspired aesthetic while guiding the eye smoothly from top navigation to final conversion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader occupies the same central zone as the hero and initial content, presenting as an overlay only when data is being fetched. It appears as a semi-transparent dark green veil that covers the hero and the first screenful of page content, stretching from the top of the content area down to just below the fold (effectively 100% of the viewport height on load). At the visual center of the screen—aligned horizontally with the main content column—sits a small, refined loading indicator (e.g., a circular spinner or a minimal badge with motion) with subtle gold accents, echoing the logo badge feel. The overlay dims but does not completely obscure the background hero image, maintaining the boutique atmosphere even during loading. Once loading is complete, this layer disappears entirely and does not leave empty space, allowing the hero intro and CTA panel beneath to be fully visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay relative to the viewport while active, covering the hero and top-of-page content; removed from the visual flow once loading completes and does not affect layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meetings-intro-text sits within the main hero section, centered both horizontally and vertically in the upper half of the viewport, mirroring the placement of the tagline and logo cluster in the mockup. On desktop, it is constrained to about 60–70% of the viewport width and centered, with equal left and right margins aligning with the overall site grid. The main heading (‘Meetings & Events’) is center-aligned and uses a large serif style reminiscent of the wordmark, with a thin gold horizontal rule or small divider above or below to echo the original gold dividers. Beneath the heading, a short paragraph of body copy (one to three lines) is also center-aligned, with about 24px vertical spacing between heading and paragraph. There is generous breathing room above (roughly 64px from the bottom of the global header) and below (32–40px before the event CTA panel), so the text block floats comfortably over the background interior image without colliding with visual details like lamps or the bed. On smaller screens, this block remains centered but stretches up to 90% of the viewport width, maintaining the same vertical spacing ratios.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; scrolls away with the page content. Its vertical position within the hero adapts slightly with viewport height to keep it roughly in the upper-middle region without covering critical focal points in the background image.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The spaces-list is positioned below the hero and section navigation bar in the main content region, forming the left column of a master–detail band. This band sits within the same centered 60–70% content column used above, but internally divides into two columns on desktop. The spaces-list column occupies approximately 35–40% of the central column width and is left-aligned within that area, starting about 48px below the hero/section-nav boundary. Each space item appears as a vertically stacked card or row with 16–24px internal padding and 16px vertical spacing between items, maintaining the controlled density of the mockup. The list has a subtle background tint (slightly lighter than the page background) and a right-side vertical separator or soft shadow to delineate it from the adjacent detail panel, echoing the gold-outlined booking widget. The top of the list aligns horizontally with the top of the space detail panel on the right, creating a cohesive band. On mobile and narrow tablets, the spaces-list becomes full-width, appearing above the detail panel, with consistent 24px spacing separating it from the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow. On taller viewports, the list may scroll with the page rather than within its own scroll container to preserve simplicity; if the list is very long, a subtle internal scroll behavior for just the list column can be introduced above a minimum height, while the detail panel remains aligned on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The space-detail-panel appears to the right of the spaces-list within the same horizontal band, acting as the main focus area for selected space information. On desktop, it occupies roughly 60–65% of the central content column and is right-aligned within that band, starting at the same top baseline as the first item in the spaces-list. It is rendered as a prominent card with a soft off-white or very light warm tone, framed by a thin gold border that visually echoes the booking widget frame from the mockup. The card has generous internal padding (24–32px), with the space name near the top left of the panel, descriptive text and key stats beneath, and any imagery aligned to maintain a balanced composition. Vertical spacing inside the panel follows the 16–24px rhythm to keep it rich yet uncluttered. The panel may extend to about 70–80% of the viewport height for detailed content, but leaving at least 32px of breathing room below before the next page section begins. On mobile, this panel stacks below the spaces-list and becomes full-width, keeping the same card treatment and padding but now centered within the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block aligned side-by-side with the spaces-list on wider screens and stacked below it on narrower ones. It updates content dynamically based on user selection but does not change its spatial position; no sticky behavior is required, though it can extend beyond the viewport height and scroll with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The amenities-list sits in its own section below the spaces master–detail band, preserving the linear, top-to-bottom flow. There is a clear vertical separation from the previous band—about 48–64px of whitespace above—and the section remains confined to the centered 60–70% content column. A section heading (e.g., ‘Amenities & Services’) appears left-aligned at the top of this block, with a subtle gold divider line underneath spanning roughly one-third to one-half of the content width, nodding to the hero’s gold dividers. Below the heading, amenities are arranged in a structured but airy layout: on desktop, items may form two or three columns within the central column (depending on copy length), each amenity presented as a short line or pill with 8–12px vertical spacing between rows. Padding between columns and rows adheres to the 16–24px spacing, ensuring the list looks rich but not dense. On mobile, the amenities collapse into a single column with consistent spacing, maintaining clarity and readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls with the page. It maintains consistent top and bottom padding relative to the central column, aligning with the same grid as other sections; no sticky or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-cta-panel embodies the role of the booking widget from the mockup and is positioned as a horizontally oriented strip overlapping the lower portion of the hero image, directly beneath the intro text. It is centered within the hero’s 60–70% content column and spans most of that column’s width (about 80–90%), leaving equal margins on each side. The panel appears as a low-profile card with a thin gold outline and a subtle drop shadow so it visually floats above the background photo. Internally, it is divided into two or three segments: on the left, concise copy such as ‘Plan your meeting or event’ and perhaps a short supporting line; on the right, a prominent dark green button labeled something like ‘Start Proposal’ or ‘Inquire’, given generous padding (12px vertical, 24px horizontal) and aligned vertically to the center of the panel. On larger screens, text and button are aligned on a common horizontal baseline, while on smaller screens the button may wrap below the text but remain centered. There is around 24–32px of space between the intro text block and the top edge of this panel, and about 32–48px of hero image visible beneath it before the section navigation bar or main content begins, preserving the breathing room seen around the booking controls in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; it scrolls away with the rest of the hero content. On certain breakpoints, it may slightly adjust its vertical position to avoid overlapping critical parts of the hero image, but it does not stick or float once the user scrolls beyond the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation appears immediately below the hero region, visually echoing the full-width header bar from the mockup but dedicated to in-page navigation. It spans the full browser width with a dark green background band about 56–72px tall, while its internal content is aligned to the same centered 60–70% column as the hero. Within this band, navigation items (e.g., ‘Overview’, ‘Spaces’, ‘Amenities’, ‘Plan Your Event’) are arranged horizontally from left to right, with each link padded by roughly 16px horizontally and 8–12px vertically. The active section is highlighted using a gold underline or pill-style background, mirroring the gold accents elsewhere. On desktop, the links are left-aligned within the column; on smaller screens, they may be center-aligned or converted into a horizontally scrollable pill row while still occupying the same bar. There is minimal vertical gap between the bottom of this nav bar and the top of the spaces master–detail band so that it feels like a continuous header into the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned just below the hero, then becomes sticky to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls past the hero’s bottom edge, mimicking the overlaid navigation behavior in the mockup.
