While sticky, it remains fixed at the top, spanning 100% width, with its internal content staying aligned to the central column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component operates invisibly behind the scenes and does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the page level, associated with the key interactive sections (space selection, section navigation, and CTA interactions). It ensures that the current section (e.g., ‘Spaces’, ‘Amenities’) and currently selected space in the spaces-list are reflected in the URL and restored when reloading or deep-linking, but it does not occupy any physical space on the page, nor does it introduce gaps or overlaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and does not affect spatial layout. It hooks into scroll and selection events to synchronize state with the URL, but the physical arrangement of components remains unchanged.

name: Meeting & Event Inquiry
title: Meeting & Event Inquiry
description: Page for submitting detailed meeting and event inquiries, including event details, amenities, and company contact information, and sending them to the hotel team.

core_features: Load available meeting and event amenities via getMeetingAmenityOptions and display them as a selectable checklist.
Display an inquiry form that captures event details: event type, attendee count, event date, start time, and end time.
Allow users to select required amenities from the dynamically loaded list and bind selections to the inquiry payload.
Collect company and contact information including company name, contact person, email, and phone number.
Provide an additional comments field for extra requirements and pricing-related notes.
Submit the completed inquiry using submitMeetingEventInquiry and surface the response message to the user.
Use the incoming URL parameter eventType to preselect or lock the event type field where applicable.
Provide a functional link back to meetings_events.html, optionally preserving context via URL parameters (e.g., eventType).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Standard inquiry from Meetings & Events page
1. User clicks a call-to-action on meetings_events.html (e.g., “Request Proposal”), which opens meeting_inquiry.html with or without an eventType URL parameter.
2. On initial load, the page calls getMeetingAmenityOptions to retrieve the current list of available amenities.
3. The form initializes. If an eventType URL parameter is present, the event type field is prefilled accordingly.
4. User enters attendee count, selects an event date, start and end times, and chooses amenities from the checklist.
5. User fills in company name, contact name, email, and phone, and optionally enters additional details.
6. User clicks the submit button. The form validates required fields and formats.
7. If validation passes, the page calls submitMeetingEventInquiry with the current form data.
8. On success, the page displays a confirmation message using the returned inquiry and message, and may update the URL (e.g., appending status and inquiryId parameters).
9. User can click a dedicated link to navigate back to meetings_events.html.

Workflow 2: Deep-linked inquiry with a preselected event type
1. User lands directly on meeting_inquiry.html with a URL that includes eventType in the query string.
2. On initial load, URL state is parsed; the event type form field is initialized to the provided value.
3. The page triggers getMeetingAmenityOptions to populate the amenities checklist.
4. User completes the remaining fields: attendee count, date, times, amenities, company and contact info, and additional details.
5. User submits the form, which calls submitMeetingEventInquiry.
6. On a successful response, a confirmation state is shown. The page may reflect this in the URL (e.g., status=submitted and inquiryId set from inquiry.id) so that a refreshed page can re-enter confirmation state.

Workflow 3: Returning to an already-submitted inquiry view state
1. User returns to meeting_inquiry.html via a link that includes status=submitted and inquiryId in the URL.
2. On load, the URL state manager component reads status and inquiryId.
3. If status indicates a successful submission, the form may be shown in a read-only or visually de-emphasized state, and the confirmation component displays a generic confirmation message (not relying on any static text tied to specific inquiries).
4. User can use the back link to navigate to meetings_events.html, optionally including eventType in that URL if it is present in the current query string.
interactions: On initial page load, the page parses URL parameters (eventType, status, inquiryId) and then triggers getMeetingAmenityOptions. The results are bound to the amenities checklist component.

When the user changes the event type field, the new value updates the in-memory MeetingEventInquiry eventType field. If eventType came from the URL and is configured to be locked, changes may be disabled or reverted.
When the user enters attendee count, date, and times, those values are validated for required presence and basic formatting before enabling the submit action.

When the user selects or deselects amenities in the checklist, the corresponding MeetingAmenity.code values are added to or removed from the MeetingEventInquiry amenities array bound to the form state.
When the user types into the additional details field, the text is bound to MeetingEventInquiry.additionalDetails in real time.

On submit button click, the page performs client-side validation and, if valid, invokes submitMeetingEventInquiry with the current MeetingEventInquiry-like state (eventType, attendeeCount, eventDate, startTime, endTime, amenities, companyName, contactName, contactEmail, contactPhone, additionalDetails).

On successful response from submitMeetingEventInquiry, the confirmation component displays the returned message and may optionally update the browser URL to include status=submitted and inquiryId set to inquiry.id.

On clicking the 'Back to Meetings & Events' navigation link, the user is taken to meetings_events.html. If eventType exists in the current URL, it can be forwarded as a URL parameter to meetings_events.html to maintain context.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters to control initialization and post-submission state:
- eventType: When present in the query string for meeting_inquiry.html, this value initializes the event type field in the form. The event-type handling component can optionally treat this value as read-only or editable based on configuration, but it must always prefer the URL value over any default form value.
- status: When status=submitted (or other states as defined), the page enters a confirmation-oriented state on load. In this state, the confirmation component is visible even before any new submission occurs, indicating that an inquiry has already been submitted in a previous interaction.
- inquiryId: When combined with status=submitted, this parameter represents the identifier of a previously submitted inquiry. The page does not fetch additional data using this ID (since no interface for that is available), but can include inquiryId in accessibility labels, confirmations, or as part of any outbound link parameters.
State transitions:
- Initial load: URL is parsed. If status is not present, the page shows the editable form in its initial state, prefilled with eventType from URL if available. Amenities are loaded via getMeetingAmenityOptions.
- After submission: Upon successful submitMeetingEventInquiry call, the page may push or replace the current URL with added parameters status=submitted and inquiryId set from the returned inquiry.id. The current in-memory form data is considered submitted; the form can either be cleared, locked, or left as-is depending on configuration, but the visible confirmation is controlled by the status URL param.
- Navigation back to meetings_events.html: The outgoing navigation does not rely on local state. Instead, it can append eventType (if available in the current URL or form state) as a URL parameter to meetings_events.html so that the originating context is preserved purely via query parameters.

components.functionality: Parses and manages URL parameters (eventType, status, inquiryId) on load and when the URL changes, and exposes them to other components as read-only state.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the current URL query string and extract eventType, status, and inquiryId. Provide these values to the form and confirmation components.
onUrlChange: Re-parse the URL if it changes (e.g., after push/replaceState) to keep the page state synchronized with the query parameters.

components.functionality: Loads available meeting amenities using getMeetingAmenityOptions and stores them in page state for rendering the amenities checklist.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getMeetingAmenityOptions with no parameters and store the resulting array of MeetingAmenity objects.
onReload: Allow other components (e.g., form) to request a refresh of amenities, re-calling getMeetingAmenityOptions and updating the bound data.

components.functionality: Handles input of all MeetingEventInquiry fields required for submission: event type, attendee count, event date, start time, end time, amenity selections, company details, contact details, and additional comments.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize an in-memory MeetingEventInquiry-like object with empty fields, then apply eventType from url-state-controller if present.
onEventTypeChange: Update the local MeetingEventInquiry.eventType when the user changes the event type field, unless the field is configured as read-only due to URL state.
onAttendeeCountChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.attendeeCount and perform basic validation (e.g., required and numeric) to inform form-level validity state.
onEventDateChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.eventDate and validate required presence and format.
onStartTimeChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.startTime and validate required presence and basic time format.
onEndTimeChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.endTime and validate required presence and basic time format.

onAmenityToggle: When a user selects or deselects an amenity from the checklist populated by amenity-options-loader, add or remove the corresponding MeetingAmenity.code from MeetingEventInquiry.amenities.
onCompanyNameChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.companyName and validate required presence.
onContactNameChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.contactName and validate required presence.
onContactEmailChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.contactEmail and validate required presence and email pattern.
onContactPhoneChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.contactPhone and validate required presence and basic phone pattern.
onAdditionalDetailsChange: Update MeetingEventInquiry.additionalDetails with the free-text content.
onSubmit: If all required fields are valid, forward the current MeetingEventInquiry-like state to the inquiry-submit-handler component; otherwise, trigger validation error messages.

components.functionality: Displays field-level and form-level validation messages based on the current form state before submission.
event_handlers: onValidationUpdate: Receive validation results from meeting-event-inquiry-form (per field and overall) and update displayed messages.
onFieldFocus: Optionally clear or adjust messages for the focused field to guide the user toward resolving errors.

components.functionality: Coordinates the actual submission of the inquiry using submitMeetingEventInquiry and handles the response.
event_handlers: onSubmitRequest: Receive the current MeetingEventInquiry-like data from the form and call submitMeetingEventInquiry with eventType, attendeeCount, eventDate, startTime, endTime, amenities, companyName, contactName, contactEmail, contactPhone, and additionalDetails.

onSubmitSuccess: When submitMeetingEventInquiry returns successfully, expose the returned inquiry and message to the confirmation component and request that the url-state-controller update the URL (e.g., to include status=submitted and inquiryId=inquiry.id).
onSubmitError: Capture any errors from submitMeetingEventInquiry and expose them to the form-validation-messages component or a generic error banner so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Presents the list of amenities retrieved by amenity-options-loader as selectable options, and synchronizes selected items with the inquiry form state.
event_handlers: onInit: Subscribe to amenity-options-loader data and render all available MeetingAmenity entries as selectable items.
onSelectAmenity: When an amenity is selected, invoke meeting-event-inquiry-form.onAmenityToggle with the amenity code to add it to MeetingEventInquiry.amenities.
onDeselectAmenity: When an amenity is deselected, invoke meeting-event-inquiry-form.onAmenityToggle with the amenity code to remove it from MeetingEventInquiry.amenities.

components.functionality: Shows confirmation or status information after an inquiry is successfully submitted, using the response from submitMeetingEventInquiry or URL state that indicates a prior submission.
event_handlers: onSubmitSuccess: Display the message returned by submitMeetingEventInquiry and optionally reference the inquiry.id in a non-static way (e.g., as part of a generic label) without fetching additional data.
onUrlStateInit: If url-state-controller indicates status=submitted, display a generic confirmation state, possibly including the inquiryId from the URL.
onDismiss: Allow the user to dismiss the visible confirmation panel while keeping the underlying status in the URL unchanged.

components.functionality: Navigates the user back to meetings_events.html, optionally preserving the current event type context via URL parameters.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to meetings_events.html. If eventType is available from url-state-controller or from MeetingEventInquiry.eventType, append it as a URL parameter so the meetings_events.html page can highlight or preselect the relevant event type.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the hero-centric, top-to-bottom linear flow from the mockup. The global header and hero (dark green bar, centered tagline, large logo badge over a full-bleed room image) remain structurally the same. Directly beneath the hero’s logo cluster, a slim, full-width status-style strip introduces the inquiry context (e.g., response time / reassurance), mirroring the original availability bar. Below that, a single, prominent inquiry form card takes the place of the booking widget, floating over the lower portion of the hero image but confined to the centered content column (about 60–70% page width). Within this card, content is arranged in a clear vertical sequence: title → optional validation messages → main form fields → amenities checklist section on the right (desktop) → submit controls. The confirmation panel later reuses this same central card footprint, replacing the form’s interior while preserving the overall hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the hero-centric, top-to-bottom linear flow from the mockup. The global header and hero (dark green bar, centered tagline, large logo badge over a full-bleed room image) remain structurally the same. Directly beneath the hero’s logo cluster, a slim, full-width status-style strip introduces the inquiry context (e.g., response time / reassurance), mirroring the original availability bar. Below that, a single, prominent inquiry form card takes the place of the booking widget, floating over the lower portion of the hero image but confined to the centered content column (about 60–70% page width). Within this card, content is arranged in a clear vertical sequence: title → optional validation messages → main form fields → amenities checklist section on the right (desktop) → submit controls. The confirmation panel later reuses this same central card footprint, replacing the form’s interior while preserving the overall hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header still occupies roughly 12–15% of the viewport height, the tagline + logo cluster about 30–35%, and the status strip + inquiry card together 25–30%, leaving visible hero image above and to the sides for breathing room. Horizontally, all main interactive content (status strip, form card, confirmation panel) is limited to a centered container spanning approximately 8 of 12 columns (~66% width), aligned with the logo badge and the hero’s visual center. Internal padding inside the card follows the 16–24px range on all sides, and vertical gaps between form sections and between stacked components follow the 24–40px tiered spacing to maintain a rich but controlled rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density matches the mockup’s ‘moderately spacious’ feel. The inquiry card contains a fair number of fields and options, but they are grouped into logical sections separated by 24–32px vertical spacing and gold-accented headings. The amenities checklist is visually grouped into its own right-side column area so that the left column (event and contact details) doesn’t feel cramped. Validation messages and confirmation content appear in their own padded stripes or blocks rather than overlaid inline labels, keeping the interface clear and calm despite complexity.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the same Z-pattern: top-left logo and top navigation, then across to the right navigation and BOOK CTA. From there, attention drops diagonally to the centered tagline and large badge logo. The next natural step is down to the slim status-style strip that introduces the inquiry, then to the large, central inquiry form card. Within the card, the user’s focus moves from the card title at the top, down through validation messages (when present), then down the main form column, and sideways to the amenities checklist on the right. Finally, it is guided downward again to the primary submit button, with a subtle back link positioned nearby to support secondary navigation without competing with the primary action. After submission, the eye lands in the same central zone but on a simplified confirmation panel, preserving familiarity and reducing cognitive load.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with the same warm, boutique-hotel hero: a full-bleed interior photograph with a deep green overlay and an overlaid top navigation bar, logo badge on the left and navigation links on the right. Centered over the hero image, a refined tagline with gold divider lines leads the eye down to the large hexagonal logo badge, which acts as the visual anchor. Directly beneath this logo, within the central content column, a slim gold-accented strip gently announces that the user is submitting a Meeting & Event inquiry, echoing the availability bar from the booking mockup. Sitting just below this strip and overlapping the lower portion of the hero, a generous, white or very light card framed with subtle gold or dark green touches contains the full inquiry experience. This card stretches across roughly two-thirds of the viewport width and is vertically spacious, with well-separated sections for event details, dates, guest counts, and company contact information on the left, and a structured amenities checklist on the right. Above the fields, any validation messages appear as a soft, full-width band, while the main submit button is anchored near the lower-right area of the card. A quiet ‘Back to Meetings & Events’ link sits in the upper-left area of the card or just above it, supporting the primary journey without disrupting the vertical flow. After submission, the same card area transforms into a confirmation panel with a centered success message and next steps, preserving the visual center and the cozy, premium aesthetic established by the hero image and brand elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits at the page level, coordinating which of the main screen states is displayed (form vs. confirmation). It operates behind the scenes, determining whether the central inquiry card shows the Meeting & Event Inquiry form or the submission confirmation panel, but it does not render any visible elements, borders, or spacing of its own. Its influence is purely structural and behavioral: it ensures smooth transitions within the same central card footprint without causing layout jumps or shifts of surrounding content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic controller with no spatial footprint; it only swaps which primary content component (form vs. confirmation panel) is mounted in the central card region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is also non-visual and occupies no distinct space on the screen. It effectively lives ‘inside’ or just above the amenities checklist area in terms of logic, ensuring that by the time the user sees the amenities checklist within the right side of the inquiry card, it is populated with the correct options. Loading states, if exposed visually, would appear as part of the amenities checklist presentation (e.g., a subtle skeleton list or spinner), but the loader itself does not introduce additional padding, margins, or blocks in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered data service; it has no visible layout but feeds data into the amenity checklist region within the central form card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Meeting & Event Inquiry form is the primary visual and functional block after the hero logo cluster. It appears inside a large, central card positioned directly beneath the slim status-style strip, overlapping the lower third of the hero image. The card is horizontally centered within the page’s 12-column grid, spanning roughly 8 columns (about 60–70% of the viewport width) and aligned with the center line of the logo and tagline above. There is generous whitespace above the card (about 32–48px) separating it from the status strip, and similar spacing below before the start of any additional page content. Inside the card, padding of about 24px on all sides creates a calm, premium feel. The form’s heading (e.g., “Meeting & Event Inquiry”) is aligned to the left within the card, near the top margin. Below the heading, any validation messages occupy a full-width band, followed by the structured form fields. At desktop widths, the form content is laid out in a two-column structure: the left two-thirds of the card (roughly 60–65% of the card width) contains the main event details (event type, date range, time, expected attendees, room setup) and company/contact information stacked vertically with 24–32px gaps between logical groups. The right one-third of the card is reserved for the amenities checklist component, visually separated by a subtle vertical divider or a difference in background tint. Near the bottom of the card, spanning the width of the left column, the primary submit button is right-aligned within its column, with sufficient breathing room (24–32px) above it. On smaller screens, the card collapses to a single column: the main fields appear first, followed by the amenities checklist, and finally the submit button, all maintaining comfortable vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling block element, visually anchored as a large floating card centered below the hero. It maintains its relative position in the flow, with the card height expanding as needed to accommodate form content while preserving consistent top alignment with the hero cluster.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form validation messages appear as a horizontally centered, full-width band within the top portion of the inquiry form card, directly beneath the form’s title and above the first input fields. They follow the card’s internal padding, so the message block does not touch the card’s edges but instead sits within the 24px side padding. Visually, this panel is a low-profile strip or stacked set of messages on a gently tinted background (e.g., warm off-white with a subtle colored border) to maintain the premium look while drawing attention. Vertical padding of about 12–16px inside the panel ensures readability without feeling cramped. When multiple messages exist, they stack vertically, still contained within this top area. This panel pushes the form fields downward but does not alter the placement of the card itself relative to the hero; it simply consumes some of the internal vertical space, preserving the clear, linear flow from title → messages → form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the top of the form card; appears and disappears dynamically. It expands the internal height of the card as needed, but does not affect the overall card width or its central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit handler is non-visual and conceptually bound to the lower portion of the inquiry form card, where the primary submit button resides. When the user presses the button, this handler processes the data and orchestrates the transition to the confirmation state. It does not render any visible UI of its own and does not occupy any extra spacing or blocks within the layout; instead, it triggers changes in the content of the central card (e.g., toggling from form to confirmation panel) while preserving the same spatial footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered action controller tied to the form’s submit button; no spatial footprint, only drives state transitions that swap the content inside the existing central card area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The amenities checklist sits as a distinct, visually cohesive section on the right side of the inquiry form card at desktop widths. Within the card’s 8-column footprint, this checklist occupies approximately 3 of the 8 columns (around 35–40% of the card width), starting after a subtle vertical divider or a vertical spacing gap from the main form column. It is aligned to the top of the main form fields so that its heading lines up horizontally with the left column’s first group of fields, reinforcing a tidy grid. Inside the checklist area, there is 16–24px padding, and the amenities are displayed in a vertical list of checkboxes or toggles, grouped by category with 16–24px gaps between groups. The top of this section may include a small gold-accented subheading (e.g., “Amenities & Services”) to echo the boutique aesthetic. The list is engineered so that if many amenities are available, the section can scroll internally while the card itself can expand moderately, preventing the page from feeling overly tall. On smaller screens, this checklist collapses into the main vertical flow of the form, appearing below the core event inputs but above the submit button, still spanning the full width of the card’s content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive sub-section within the main form card: a right-hand column on larger viewports and a full-width stacked block on smaller viewports. It scrolls with the rest of the page and may have its own internal scroll behavior if the list is long.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission confirmation panel reuses the exact spatial footprint of the inquiry form card, appearing in the same central position beneath the hero logo cluster and status strip. When active, it replaces the form content entirely: the outer card dimensions (width, alignment, and general padding) remain consistent, but the inside becomes simpler and more centered. The main confirmation message (e.g., “Thank you for your inquiry”) is centered horizontally within the card and placed slightly above the vertical center, with generous top margin (about 32–40px from the top padding) to create a sense of calm. Secondary text (e.g., details about response times or a summary) is stacked beneath the main heading with 16–24px spacing. Any follow-up actions (such as buttons to explore other pages or download event guides) align either centered below the text or right-aligned in line with the original submit button position, depending on hierarchy. The overall effect is a clean, open card that keeps the user’s focus in the same part of the hero where they previously filled out the form, maintaining continuity with the cozy, premium environment. Background hero image and header remain unchanged behind it, reinforcing that the user is still on the same Meeting & Event section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Content-swapping panel that occupies the same central card region as the form. It appears in place of the form after successful submission, with the card remaining centered and scrollable as needed but generally shorter and more compact than the full form state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ‘Back to Meetings’ link serves as a subtle secondary navigation element positioned close to, but visually subordinate to, the main inquiry card. On desktop, it appears just above the top-left corner of the card content area, aligned with the left edge of the central content column. It sits within the hero area’s lower region, about 16–24px above the card’s top edge, so it’s clearly associated with the Meeting & Event context without interfering with the hero tagline or logo. The link may include a small left-pointing chevron icon and is styled with understated typography—possibly a serif or refined sans-serif with a muted color (e.g., light gold or off-white over the darker hero background) to echo the boutique aesthetic. Adequate spacing (at least 16px) separates it from the card’s top edge to avoid collision. On smaller screens, this link can shift to align just inside the card’s top-left, above the form title, maintaining a clear hierarchy: back link → form title → messages → fields. In all cases, it remains unobtrusive, providing an escape route without detracting from the central focus on the form or confirmation message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline navigation element anchored to the left side of the central content column. It scrolls naturally with the page and remains close to the top of the primary card, adjusting from just outside to just inside the card on smaller viewports to conserve vertical space.

name: Newsletter Signup
title: Newsletter Signup
description: Page for subscribing to the hotel and restaurant newsletter, choosing interests and email frequency, and providing marketing consent.

core_features: Display a concise explanation of what the newsletter includes and how often it is sent, focusing on room offers, restaurant news, and events.
Render a signup form to collect subscriber name and email address, optionally prefilled from the URL `email` parameter.
Allow users to select interest categories mapped to newsletter preference fields (room offers, restaurant events/news, spa offers, meeting/event offers, and general news).
Allow users to choose email frequency from the supported `NewsletterSubscription.frequency` enum values.
Require users to explicitly agree to receive marketing emails via a consent checkbox mapped to `consentMarketing`, alongside configurable consent text.
Validate required fields (email, at least one interest, frequency, consent) before allowing submission.
Submit the form via the `createNewsletterSubscription` interface and handle the response (success state, error state, inline messages).
After a successful subscription, display confirmation state and provide a navigation link back to `index.html` (home).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Direct signup from footer or homepage teaser
1. User navigates to `newsletter.html` via the footer 'Newsletter' link or a homepage teaser link without URL parameters.
2. Page initializes and shows the explanation text and empty signup form fields.
3. User enters their name and email address.
4. User selects one or more interest categories (room offers, restaurant events, etc.).
5. User selects an email frequency option.
6. User reads the consent text and checks the consent checkbox.
7. User clicks the Submit button.
8. Frontend validates that email, at least one interest, frequency, and consent are provided and that email format is valid.
9. If validation passes, the page calls `createNewsletterSubscription` with the form data.
10. If the interface returns `success = true`, the page displays a confirmation message and shows a 'Return to Home' link that navigates to `index.html`.
11. If the interface returns `success = false`, the page displays the returned `message` as an error and keeps the form values so the user can adjust and resubmit.

Workflow 2: Signup with prefilled email from another page
1. User enters their email into a simple field on `index.html` or in the footer and clicks a 'Sign up' or similar link which navigates to `newsletter.html?email={user_email}`.
2. `newsletter.html` reads the `email` URL parameter on load.
3. If the provided `email` value is syntactically valid, it is used to prefill the email field in the form; if not valid, the field remains empty and standard validation applies.
4. User confirms or edits the prefilled email.
5. User completes interests, frequency, and consent as in Workflow 1.
6. User submits the form; the same validation, interface call (`createNewsletterSubscription`), and success/error handling apply.

Workflow 3: Handling validation errors and resubmission
1. User fills the form but omits a required element (for example, does not choose any interests or does not tick the consent checkbox).
2. User clicks Submit.
3. Client-side validation runs before calling `createNewsletterSubscription`.
4. Invalid fields are flagged (e.g., inline error messages for missing interests, invalid email, missing frequency, or unchecked consent), and the submit request is not sent until all required conditions are met.
5. User corrects the highlighted fields.
6. User clicks Submit again.
7. Once validation passes, the form calls `createNewsletterSubscription` and then follows the success/error path as above.

Workflow 4: Post-subscription navigation
1. After a successful call to `createNewsletterSubscription`, the page shows a confirmation state (e.g., message that signup is complete) using the returned `subscription` and `message` data.
2. A 'Return to Home' link is displayed.
3. When the user clicks this link, the browser navigates to `index.html` without additional required parameters.
4. If the user reloads `newsletter.html` afterward, it loads again as a fresh form, unless implementation chooses to keep a success indicator in the URL as described in state logic.
interactions: On page load: Read and parse the `email` URL parameter. If present and valid, pre-populate the email input field. If invalid or absent, leave the email field empty.
Name input interaction: User types their name into a text input field. The component updates its local form state but does not contact any interface at this step.

Email input interaction: User types or edits their email address. The component updates the email field in form state and may perform live format validation. If the prefilled email is changed, the new value overrides the initial URL-based value.

Interest selection interaction: User toggles interest checkboxes corresponding to `interestRoomOffers`, `interestRestaurantEvents`, `interestSpaOffers`, `interestMeetingOffers`, and `interestGeneralNews`. Each checkbox change updates the related boolean field in the form state.

Frequency selection interaction: User selects one of the available frequency options corresponding to `NewsletterSubscription.frequency` enum values. Selection updates the `frequency` field in the form state; only one option can be active at a time.

Consent checkbox interaction: User reads the consent text and toggles a checkbox bound to `consentMarketing`. The Submit button remains disabled (or submission blocked) until `consentMarketing` is true.

Submit button interaction (form submission): When the user clicks Submit, client-side validation runs. If any required field is missing or invalid, the submission is blocked and inline error indicators are shown. If all fields are valid, the `createNewsletterSubscription` interface is called with the current form state as parameters.

API response handling interaction: When the `createNewsletterSubscription` promise resolves, the page checks the `success` flag. On `success = true`, it displays a success state including the returned `message` and may show selected summary details from `subscription`. On `success = false`, it displays the provided `message` as an error and keeps the form editable without clearing user input.
Return to Home navigation: After a successful subscription, the user can click a 'Return to Home' link. This triggers a navigation to `index.html` via standard link navigation.

Optional retry interaction: If the interface call fails due to network or server errors, an error message is shown, and the Submit button remains available so the user can retry after checking inputs.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is primarily controlled by URL parameters and in-page form state, without using localStorage or other persistent browser storage.

URL-driven state:
- `email` (string): When present in the query string as `newsletter.html?email={value}`, this parameter is read on initial page load.
  - If the value passes email format validation, it pre-populates the email field in the signup form.
  - If the value does not pass basic email validation, it is ignored for prefill and standard validation rules apply when the user submits the form.
  - The parameter is not modified by the page; it is read-only and only used for initial prefill.

Form state:
- The form maintains internal state for:
  - `name`: optional text.
  - `email`: required, possibly initialized from the URL `email` parameter.
  - `interestRoomOffers`, `interestRestaurantEvents`, `interestSpaOffers`, `interestMeetingOffers`, `interestGeneralNews`: booleans reflecting interest checkboxes.
  - `frequency`: one of the available enum values from `NewsletterSubscription.frequency`.
  - `consentMarketing`: boolean reflecting the consent checkbox.
- The Submit button is only enabled (or allowed to proceed) when:
  - `email` is non-empty and valid.
  - At least one interest boolean is true.
  - `frequency` is set to a valid enum value.
  - `consentMarketing` is true.

Submission and response state:
- When the user submits, the page enters a 'submitting' state (e.g., disabling the Submit button) until the `createNewsletterSubscription` call resolves or rejects.
- On success (`success = true`):
  - A success state flag is set in memory.
  - The form can optionally be locked to read-only so the user does not accidentally re-submit, or it can remain but with a clear indication that the subscription is complete.
  - A confirmation message is displayed based on the returned `message` and/or `subscription` object.
- On error (`success = false` or network error):
  - An error state flag is set.
  - An error message is shown using the returned `message` (if provided) or a generic error from the client logic.
  - The form data is preserved so the user can adjust and resubmit.

No state is persisted via localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies. Any cross-page or shareable state is represented solely by the URL parameter `email`, which can be used by other pages (like `index.html`) to deep-link into this page with a prefilled email.

components.functionality: Coordinates overall page behavior: reads URL parameters, initializes form state, manages submission flow using the createNewsletterSubscription interface, and toggles between default, submitting, success, and error states.

Displays a concise explanation of the benefits and purpose of joining the newsletter, including that users can receive information on room offers, restaurant news and events, spa offers, meeting/event information, and general hotel updates.

Main container for all newsletter signup fields and submit behavior. It collects name, email, interests, frequency, and consent, performs validation, and triggers the createNewsletterSubscription interface via the page controller.
Handles input fields for subscriber name and email address, including prefill of the email field from the `email` URL parameter and inline email format validation.

Renders a list of interest checkboxes mapped to the newsletter subscription preference booleans (`interestRoomOffers`, `interestRestaurantEvents`, `interestSpaOffers`, `interestMeetingOffers`, `interestGeneralNews`). Ensures the form-level validation can check that at least one interest is selected.

Provides a control (e.g., radio buttons or dropdown) for selecting a single email frequency corresponding to the `frequency` enum in `NewsletterSubscription`. Updates form state and exposes the selected value for validation.

Displays legally required consent text (sourced from configuration or CMS, not hardcoded in this design) and a checkbox bound to `consentMarketing`. Validation ensures the checkbox is checked before allowing submission.

Submit trigger for the signup form. It checks validation state before allowing the createNewsletterSubscription call, reflects the 'submitting' state by disabling itself during API calls, and is re-enabled on error responses.

Displays inline validation errors, API error messages, and success confirmation messages based on form validation and the result of the createNewsletterSubscription call (using the returned `success` and `message` properties).

Visible primarily in the success state. Presents a 'Return to Home' link that navigates to `index.html`. May also optionally present a link or button to stay on the page and adjust preferences if the implementation supports it.

Isolates the call to the `createNewsletterSubscription` interface. It receives validated form data from the page controller, invokes `createNewsletterSubscription(email, name, interestRoomOffers, interestRestaurantEvents, interestSpaOffers, interestMeetingOffers, interestGeneralNews, frequency, consentMarketing)`, and returns the response object to the controller for state updates.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single hero-style section with a full-bleed photographic background and a centered content column, mirroring the booking hero. The global header (handled by the page controller) overlays the top of the hero with logo on the left and navigation on the right. Within the hero, content is stacked in a clear vertical sequence: first the newsletter info block as the equivalent of the tagline; directly beneath it a full-width status strip for newsletter status messages; and below that a framed newsletter signup form card where the booking widget would normally sit. Inside the form, fields are arranged in horizontal bands, echoing the original booking strip: first the name/email row, then interests, then frequency, then consent and submit at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single hero-style section with a full-bleed photographic background and a centered content column, mirroring the booking hero. The global header (handled by the page controller) overlays the top of the hero with logo on the left and navigation on the right. Within the hero, content is stacked in a clear vertical sequence: first the newsletter info block as the equivalent of the tagline; directly beneath it a full-width status strip for newsletter status messages; and below that a framed newsletter signup form card where the booking widget would normally sit. Inside the form, fields are arranged in horizontal bands, echoing the original booking strip: first the name/email row, then interests, then frequency, then consent and submit at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header occupies about 12–15 percent of the viewport height, the central newsletter info block and status messages about 20–25 percent, and the form about 30–35 percent, leaving generous breathing space of background image above and below. Horizontally, the main content is constrained to a centered column about 60–70 percent of the viewport width, with the hero image extending edge to edge. The form card and status strip maintain ample padding so they float comfortably over the background without colliding with detailed elements in the image.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious density: each major block (info block, status strip, form) is separated by 24–40 pixels of vertical space, and internal form padding is 16–24 pixels on all sides. Field groups inside the form sit close enough to feel connected but are visually separated with consistent vertical rhythm. Overall, the page feels refined and inviting rather than sparse or crowded, in line with a premium boutique hotel aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first follows the familiar Z-pattern: it lands on the top-left logo and brand in the header, moves across to the right-side navigation, then drops diagonally to the centered newsletter title and description. From there, attention is drawn down to the gold-accented status strip, then further down to the framed form card. Within the form, the eye scans left to right across the top row of name and email, then steps downward through interests and frequency, ending at the consent text and prominent submit button. Post-submit links sit subtly beneath, offering gentle next steps without competing with the main call to action.

layout.overall_description: The newsletter signup page presents itself as a warm hero scene over the same cozy, full-width bedroom or restaurant photo used across the site. A dark green header bar with the badge logo on the left and navigation including the main booking link on the right overlays the top edge of the image. Centered beneath this, within a column spanning roughly two-thirds of the viewport, the newsletter title and short invitation text are displayed like an elegant tagline, horizontally centered and possibly framed by thin gold dividers. Immediately below, a full-width gold-accented status strip waits to show success or error states, matching the booking availability bar’s visual weight. Floating slightly lower, over the middle-lower area of the hero image, a white or very light newsletter form card sits within a gold outline, echoing the original booking widget. Inside, fields are lined up in horizontal bands with balanced padding and subtle dividers, culminating in a dark green, right-aligned submit button. A small cluster of post-submit links rests just beneath, centered and understated. The overall composition keeps the focus on the middle column, uses generous vertical spacing, and maintains the boutique-hotel, premium atmosphere through consistent colors, typography, and gold accent lines.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the full hero canvas: a full-width, full-viewport-height section with the warm interior background image stretching edge to edge. At the very top, it places the dark green global header bar over the hero as an overlay, with the hotel logo badge on the left and the main navigation on the right, mirroring the booking page. Beneath this header, it establishes a centered content column occupying about 60–70 percent of the viewport width, aligned to the visual center of the page. All newsletter-specific components (info block, status messages, form, and post-submit actions) are stacked vertically within this central column, starting just below the header with sufficient top padding (roughly 64 pixels) so the content feels visually separated from the navigation bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a full-height hero section; header is overlaid at the top and may remain fixed on scroll, while the centered content column and newsletter components scroll within the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter info block is positioned near the top of the central column, directly below the header, serving as the visual equivalent of the hero tagline. It is horizontally centered and spans about 60 percent of the page width on desktop, shrinking on smaller screens while remaining centered. The block includes a prominent newsletter heading and a short introductory paragraph, aligned center, with generous padding above and below (approximately 32–40 pixels). Thin gold divider lines or accents can extend a short distance on either side of the heading to echo the original tagline styling. There is clear vertical separation of about 24–32 pixels between this block and the status messages strip below, creating a clear hierarchy between explanation and system feedback.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, remaining centered as the page scrolls; it does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter signup form occupies the main interactive area, positioned below the status messages strip and centered within the same column. It is presented as a horizontal card-like panel with a light background and a subtle gold outline or border, visually analogous to the booking widget. On desktop, the form width matches the central column (around 60–70 percent of the viewport) with 16–24 pixels of internal padding on all sides. It sits over the lower middle portion of the hero image with an air gap of about 32–40 pixels above it and another 32–48 pixels below it before the bottom of the hero. Inside, content is organized into stacked horizontal rows: name/email at the top, followed by interests, then frequency, then consent, and finally the submit button row. The outer corners are slightly rounded to match the boutique aesthetic, and the card casts a very soft shadow to separate it from the background photo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card element within the central column; it scrolls with the page without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the signup form, the name and email fields form the topmost row of inputs, echoing the arrangement of the date fields in the booking widget. On desktop, this row spans the full width of the form interior and is split into two equal columns: the left half for the name field and the right half for the email field. Each field has comfortable horizontal padding and sits on a shared baseline, with about 16 pixels of space between the two columns. Vertically, there is about 16–24 pixels of space above this row (from the top border of the form) and 16–24 pixels below it before the interests selector row. On smaller viewports, the fields stack vertically while preserving the same left and right margins, maintaining the centered column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as the first content row inside the form, adapting from a two-column horizontal layout on desktop to a single column on mobile while maintaining consistent padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The interest selector occupies the second row within the form, directly beneath the name and email fields. It spans the full internal width of the form and is left-aligned to the same text axis as the fields above. At the top of this row, a small, refined label (for example, Interests) appears, with the actual selection controls (checkboxes, pill buttons, or toggles) arranged in one or two horizontal lines beneath it. Horizontal spacing between individual interest options follows the base spacing units (8–16 pixels), with approximately 16–24 pixels of vertical spacing above (from the name/email fields) and 16–24 pixels below (to the frequency selector). This row is visually balanced so it feels like an integrated band within the card, not a separate section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row inside the form that expands vertically as needed to accommodate multiple options, pushing rows below downward while preserving outer padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The frequency selector row sits directly below the interest selector, aligned left with the same internal column grid. It spans the full internal width of the form and includes a concise label followed by a horizontal arrangement of frequency options (such as radio buttons or a segmented control) laid out from left to right. Space between options is consistent with the interest selector, but this row is slightly more compact to echo the feel of the original gold status bar: concise, informative, and easy to scan. There is about 16–24 pixels of vertical spacing above from the interest selector and 16–24 pixels below before the consent section, preserving the form’s vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band that adapts to smaller screens by wrapping options to the next line if necessary, while keeping the label aligned with the primary text axis.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consent section occupies the lower area of the form, just above the submit button row. It spans the full internal width and is left-aligned, with a small checkbox or toggle followed by concise consent text. Because this content is more text-heavy, it is given slightly more vertical breathing room: about 24 pixels above (from the frequency selector) and 16–24 pixels below (to the submit button row). The text uses a slightly smaller size and increased line-height to maintain readability without overwhelming the visual hierarchy. This section feels anchored to the left, mirroring legal or policy text in premium hotel collateral, and may include a subtle gold accent or link styling for references to privacy policy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text band within the form; if the consent text wraps into multiple lines, it simply increases the height of this band and pushes the submit button downward while maintaining outer padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit button is positioned at the very bottom of the form’s content, in a row that spans the full internal width. On desktop, the button is right-aligned within this row, echoing the rightmost BOOK call-to-action in the original booking widget, and occupies roughly 25–30 percent of the form’s width with generous horizontal padding (around 16–24 pixels). Vertical padding of around 12 pixels makes the button feel substantial but not oversized. The button uses the dark green brand color as its fill with gold or light text, possibly with a thin gold outline or hover effect. There is about 16–24 pixels of internal padding below the button before the bottom border of the form, ensuring the button has room to breathe. On mobile, the button can center itself horizontally while still aligning visually with the other form elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static call-to-action at the bottom of the form; its alignment may shift from right-aligned on larger screens to centered on small screens while maintaining consistent margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status messages component sits between the newsletter info block and the signup form, occupying a full-width strip within the central column similar to the gold availability bar in the hero. When present, it appears as a horizontal bar spanning the entire width of the content column with a gold or softly tinted background and clear, centered text indicating success, error, or informational states. The bar has 12–16 pixels of internal vertical padding and about 16–24 pixels of horizontal padding, with rounded corners that match the form’s styling. There is about 16–24 pixels of vertical space above and below the bar so it does not collide with the info block or form. When no message is active, this area collapses so the info block and form appear closer together but still respect the overall rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Dynamic strip that appears and disappears in place, pushing the form downward when visible and collapsing smoothly when hidden, without overlaying other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-submit actions appear just below the form card, centered within the same content column. They are laid out as a small cluster of understated text links (such as Back to home or View offers) arranged either in a single centered row with 16–24 pixels of horizontal spacing or in a short vertical list with 8–12 pixels between items, depending on the number of links. This block has a top margin of about 24–32 pixels from the bottom of the form, ensuring it feels like a secondary follow-up rather than part of the main form. The typography is smaller and lighter than the form labels, preserving a tertiary visual weight but still clear and readable against the background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that follows the form in the document flow; it remains centered and simply moves down as form height changes due to validation or responsive behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API adapter is a non-visual component that does not render any interface elements. It sits conceptually behind the signup form, handling data submission and responses for the newsletter. It has no direct position, size, or spatial relationship within the visual layout other than enabling the status messages and post-submit behaviors of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual or spatial behavior; operates purely in the background without occupying any visible space in the layout.

name: About the Hotel
title: About the Hotel
description: Provides dynamically loaded information about the hotel’s background, design, location, neighborhood, directions, and contact details, and offers navigation into room booking, restaurant, and meetings/events pages.

core_features: Load and display hotel background information (history and story) using getAboutHotelContent.historyText.
Load and display the hotel’s design concept using getAboutHotelContent.designConcept.
Load and display location details and a summary of the surrounding area using getAboutHotelContent.locationSummary.
Display a list of neighborhood highlights using getAboutHotelContent.neighborhoodHighlights.
Display directions and how to reach the property using getAboutHotelContent.directionsText.
Display contact information for the hotel using getAboutHotelContent.contactEmail and getAboutHotelContent.contactPhone.
Allow users to navigate to the rooms page (rooms.html) for viewing rooms or starting a booking, passing URL parameters to indicate the source page.
Allow users to navigate to the restaurant page (restaurant.html) for more details and reservations, passing URL parameters to indicate the source page.
Allow users to navigate to the meetings and events page (meetings_events.html) for planning events or meetings, passing URL parameters to indicate the source page.
Support deep-linking and section-focused views via URL parameters, so users or campaigns can open the About the Hotel page directly at a specific informational section.
user_workflows:
View the hotel story and then navigate to book a stay:
1. User opens about.html (optionally with a section URL parameter).
2. The page initializes and calls getAboutHotelContent with no parameters.
3. The response is stored in page state and bound to the relevant sections.
4. The active section is chosen based on the section URL parameter if present; otherwise a default section is shown.
5. User reads the hotel history and design concept.
6. User clicks a 'View Rooms' or 'Book a Stay' action.
7. The browser navigates to rooms.html with URL parameters such as source=about and optionally section=rooms to indicate the origin and intent.

Read about the location and neighborhood before choosing the restaurant:
1. User opens about.html?section=location.
2. The page loads, calls getAboutHotelContent, and sets the active section to the location section because section=location.
3. The locationSummary and directionsText are displayed, along with neighborhoodHighlights.
4. User explores the neighborhoodHighlights list.
5. User clicks a 'Discover the Restaurant' action.
6. The browser navigates to restaurant.html with URL parameters such as source=about&context=location to indicate the user came from the About page’s location context.

Obtain contact details and then inquire about meetings and events:
1. User opens about.html?section=contact.
2. The page loads, calls getAboutHotelContent, and sets the active section to the contact section.
3. The contactEmail and contactPhone are displayed, and are clickable for email or phone initiation.
4. User decides to learn more about meetings and events.
5. User clicks a 'Plan a Meeting' or 'Events' action.
6. The browser navigates to meetings_events.html with URL parameters such as source=about&context=contact to indicate the origin of the navigation.
interactions: On page load (about.html), the page triggers getAboutHotelContent once, without parameters, and stores the returned object in page state.

When the user clicks an internal section control (e.g., a tab or anchor) for history, design, location, neighborhood, or contact, the active section in state is updated and the URL is updated (or replaced) with the corresponding section URL parameter value (e.g., section=history).

When the URL changes (e.g., user navigates with a link including a section parameter, or uses browser back/forward), the About page reads the section URL parameter and updates the active section accordingly, without re-calling getAboutHotelContent.

When the user clicks 'View Rooms' or 'Book a Stay', the page navigates to rooms.html with URL parameters such as source=about and, optionally, context set to the currently active section (e.g., context=history or context=location).
When the user clicks 'Discover the Restaurant', the page navigates to restaurant.html with URL parameters such as source=about and context set to the currently active section.
When the user clicks 'Plan a Meeting' or 'Events', the page navigates to meetings_events.html with URL parameters such as source=about and context set to the currently active section.
When the user clicks on the displayed contact email, the client triggers a mailto: link using getAboutHotelContent.contactEmail.
When the user clicks on the displayed contact phone number, the client triggers a tel: link using getAboutHotelContent.contactPhone.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About the Hotel page uses URL parameters exclusively for controlling client-side state and for providing context to outgoing navigations. The key URL parameter is section, which can specify which informational section should be active when the page loads (for example, section=history, section=design, section=location, section=neighborhood, or section=contact). On initial load, the page reads section from the URL: if present and valid, the corresponding section is marked as active; if absent or invalid, a default section is activated. The page then calls getAboutHotelContent once to populate all sections. Changing the active section via user interaction updates the URL (using pushState or replaceState) to keep section in sync, enabling deep-linking and back/forward navigation without additional API calls. Additional URL parameters such as source and context are not used to modify the data loading for this page but may be read for analytics or ignored; when the user clicks outgoing navigation actions (to rooms.html, restaurant.html, or meetings_events.html), the About page appends source=about and context=<currentSection> to the destination URL. The getAboutHotelContent interface is always invoked without URL-dependent parameters, so all conditional behavior on this page is client-side, based solely on URL parameters and current state.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getAboutHotelContent to retrieve the hotel’s history, design concept, location summary, neighborhood highlights, directions, and contact information, and stores the result in the page’s in-memory state for other components to consume.

Controls which informational section (history, design, location, neighborhood, contact) is currently active, based on the section URL parameter and user clicks on section controls. Updates both the visible section and the URL when the active section changes.
Displays the hotel’s history and story when the active section is set to history, using the historyText data. This section can be directly targeted by the section URL parameter.
Displays information about the hotel’s design concept when the active section is set to design, using the designConcept data. This section can be directly targeted via section=design.

Displays the hotel’s location summary and directions when the active section is set to location, using locationSummary and directionsText. Intended to give users an overview of where the property is and how to reach it.
Displays a list of neighborhood highlights when the active section is set to neighborhood, using the neighborhoodHighlights array. Each list item represents a highlight drawn fully from dynamic data.
Displays the hotel’s primary contact email and phone number when the active section is set to contact, using contactEmail and contactPhone. Enables click-to-email and click-to-call interactions.

Provides functional navigation actions from the About page to the rooms, restaurant, and meetings/events pages. Each link appends URL parameters indicating the source page and current informational context.

Keeps the page’s internal state synchronized with URL parameters. On load and on browser navigation events, it reads the section URL parameter and updates state.activeSection. When the active section changes due to user interaction, it updates the section URL parameter without reloading the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page uses a single, vertically stacked main content column centered within the viewport (roughly 60–70% of the page width), echoing the hero’s linear top-to-bottom hierarchy. Immediately beneath the global header/hero, the data loader and URL state manager sit at the top of this column (mostly invisible), followed by a horizontally centered internal navigation (section switcher). Below that, the main content sections (history, design, location, neighborhood) are stacked in a clear, sequential order. The contact information panel and cross-link CTA group form the closing band at the bottom, mirroring the way the booking status bar and booking widget cluster at the bottom of the hero in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page uses a single, vertically stacked main content column centered within the viewport (roughly 60–70% of the page width), echoing the hero’s linear top-to-bottom hierarchy. Immediately beneath the global header/hero, the data loader and URL state manager sit at the top of this column (mostly invisible), followed by a horizontally centered internal navigation (section switcher). Below that, the main content sections (history, design, location, neighborhood) are stacked in a clear, sequential order. The contact information panel and cross-link CTA group form the closing band at the bottom, mirroring the way the booking status bar and booking widget cluster at the bottom of the hero in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header/hero still visually occupies the top 15–20% of the viewport. The About content column beneath it fills the remaining vertical space with generous spacing between sections: ~32–40px between major blocks and 24–32px internal padding within panels. Textual sections (history, design) each occupy roughly 25–35% of a typical desktop viewport height. The location + neighborhood block together use another 30–40%. The closing contact panel and cross-link CTA group share the final 20–25%, with the CTA group given extra vertical space and padding to visually echo the prominence of the homepage booking widget.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and controlled. Each major section reads as a distinct card or band with breathing room around it, following the mockup’s rich yet uncluttered feel. Text lines are kept to a comfortable width inside the central column. More functional strips (section switcher, contact info, CTA group) are denser horizontally but have strong padding (16–24px) and clear separation between elements to avoid clutter, similar to the booking bar in the hero.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow preserves the Z-pattern: users first scan the top-left logo/global header, then their eye moves to the right across the main navigation. Dropping into the About page content, the eye lands on the centered section switcher, then moves down through the stacked sections. Within each section, headings are centered or slightly left-biased with gold accents, then body content flows left-to-right. At the bottom, attention is pulled to the full-width contact info panel and then to the horizontally aligned cross-link CTA group, which acts as the functional end-cap in the same way the booking widget anchors the bottom of the hero.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the existing dark green header and hero, the About page content appears as a refined, centrally aligned column that feels like a continuation of the boutique-hotel hero aesthetic. A slim yet elegant band for internal section switching sits near the top of the content area, visually similar to the gold status bar in the hero and centered over the warm background. Below it, the hotel’s story unfolds in tiers: the history and design sections read like editorial spreads, with serif headings, gold dividers, and wide but not overwhelming text blocks. As you scroll further, the layout subtly opens up into a more informational structure for location and neighborhood, adding side-by-side columns and small highlight cards while keeping everything anchored to the central grid. Near the bottom, a gold-accented contact panel stretches across the content width, followed by a horizontally framed CTA group that mirrors the booking widget’s proportions—three or four pill-like links resting inside a gold-outlined bar. The result is a vertical narrative that starts with navigation and orientation, moves through storytelling and practical details, and concludes with a confident set of next-step actions, all while maintaining the warm, premium, and slightly vintage atmosphere of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader occupies the very top of the About page content area, directly beneath the global header/hero and spanning the full width of the centered content column (about 60–70% of the viewport on desktop). In its loading state, it overlays or replaces the main content column with vertical skeleton blocks that mimic the shape of the section switcher band and the first one to two content sections, extending down roughly one viewport height so the page never feels empty. It adheres to the same central alignment and vertical rhythm: a first skeleton strip where the section switcher will appear, followed by larger rectangular skeletons separated by 24–32px gaps, representing the history and design sections. Once data is loaded, this component collapses in height and becomes effectively invisible, allowing the rest of the components to occupy their defined positions in the vertical stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially overlays or replaces the main content column during data loading; otherwise collapses to zero height and does not occupy visual space. It scrolls with the page when visible but is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section switcher is positioned at the top center of the About page content column, immediately below any page title or introductory text (if present) and directly following the loader area. It spans roughly 70–80% of the content column’s width, effectively taking up about 50–60% of the full viewport width on desktop, and is horizontally centered. Visually, it appears as a low-profile horizontal band reminiscent of the gold availability status bar from the hero: a slightly raised, pill-like strip with a subtle gold outline or underlining, containing evenly spaced tabs or chips for sections like “History,” “Design,” and “Location & Neighborhood.” Internally, the band has 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, with each tab having about 16–24px horizontal breathing room. There is 24–32px of vertical space above it (from preceding content) and 32–40px below it, leading into the first active content section. On scroll, it visually anchors the top of the content area, keeping section navigation readily accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Becomes sticky to the top of the viewport once scrolled past its original position, sitting just below the global header. It maintains its centered width while sticking, ensuring continuous internal navigation without breaking the central-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hotel history section is the first major content block beneath the section switcher, occupying the upper third of the scrollable About content. It sits within the same centered content column, spanning roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (around 65–70% of viewport width), with equal left and right margins. The top of the section begins 32–40px below the section switcher to preserve the tiered vertical rhythm. At the top of this section, a centered or slightly left-biased serif heading is flanked or underlined by a delicate gold divider line, visually echoing the tagline treatment from the hero. Below the heading, the main body copy is arranged in a single column on narrow screens and a comfortable two-column layout on larger desktops, each column taking about 45% of the content width with a 10% gutter between them. Internally, the section has generous padding at its top and bottom (24–32px each), so the text doesn’t crowd the previous or next sections. The overall height of this section typically occupies about 25–35% of the viewport, depending on text length, maintaining a feeling of a rich, editorial block without overwhelming the user.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally in the document. It does not stick or overlap with other components; its height and layout can expand vertically as content grows while preserving the central alignment and column widths.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The design concept section sits directly beneath the hotel history section, separated by a clear vertical gap of about 32–40px to create the sense of a new tier. It continues to use the central content column but with a slightly more dynamic, visually rich layout to reflect the aesthetic focus: on desktop, the section spans 9 of 12 grid columns (about 75% of the viewport width) and is still centered, subtly breaking wider than the text-only history section to give design imagery more space. The top of the section features a heading with a gold accent similar to the history section, maintaining typographic continuity. Below the heading, content is typically arranged in two columns: a text-dominant column on the left (about 55–60% of the section width) and an image or moodboard-style block on the right (about 40–45%). The right column might include a framed interior photo or a collage with a thin gold border, visually echoing the hexagonal logo badge and other framed elements from the hero. The section has 24–32px of internal padding at the top and bottom, with about 24px of horizontal breathing room inside each column. Overall, this section occupies roughly 25–35% of the viewport height and feels visually denser than the history section while still respecting the mockup’s controlled richness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal flow with responsive behavior: on smaller screens, the right-side visual block flows below the text, maintaining the same vertical spacing and staying within the centered content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location overview section follows the design concept section, separated by a vertical gap of around 32–40px. It presents as a more informational band with a slightly lighter background tone (for example, a warm off-white) to subtly differentiate it from the storytelling sections above while still fitting the boutique color palette. The section spans about 10 of 12 grid columns (roughly 80% of the viewport width), centered, giving it a broader feel suited for maps or directional content. At the top, a clear heading is aligned left within the central column, with a fine gold underline to preserve the visual DNA. The content below is arranged in a two-column layout: on desktop, the left column (about 55–60% width) holds descriptive text about the hotel’s location, proximity to landmarks, and perhaps a short bullet list; the right column (about 40–45% width) is reserved for a static map, illustration, or summary card of key distances. Internal padding of 24–32px on all sides keeps the content from touching the edges, and there is about 24px spacing between the columns. The overall vertical height is moderate—around 20–30% of a viewport—so it reads as a concise, practical overview that sets up the more detailed neighborhood list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally with the page. Its background band extends the full width of the centered content block, but not necessarily edge-to-edge of the browser window, preserving the central content silhouette.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The neighborhood highlights list is positioned directly under the location overview section, with about 24–32px of vertical separation so it reads as a continuation of the location theme rather than a completely new topic. It remains within the same central column width as the location section (about 10 of 12 grid columns, centered), but internally, the content transitions into a more modular layout—echoing the way multiple functional elements coexist within the hero without feeling cluttered. At the top, a simple label or subheading may align left, followed by a grid of highlight items. On desktop, the list items are arranged in 2–3 columns (each card occupying roughly 30–45% of the content width with 24px gutters between them) and 1–3 rows depending on content. Each highlight card is a compact block with 16–24px of internal padding, a short title, 1–2 lines of description, and optionally a small icon or subtle gold accent line. Vertical spacing between rows of cards is about 24px. The overall block height is variable but typically occupies another 20–30% of viewport height, perceived as a dense but well-organized cluster of points of interest that naturally leads the eye downward toward practical contact details.
