component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to a single column of stacked highlight cards while maintaining the same internal padding and vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact info panel is positioned near the bottom of the About page, below the neighborhood highlights list, with a generous vertical separation of 32–40px to mark the transition from descriptive content to practical action. It visually echoes the gold status bar and framed booking widget from the hero: a horizontally oriented card that spans the full width of the central content column (about 60–70% of the viewport), centered, with a subtle gold border or top rule. The panel has 24–32px of internal padding, giving ample room for key contact details—phone, email, address, and possibly a small icon row—without feeling cramped. On desktop, the content may be arranged in a subtle two- or three-column layout within the panel: for example, address on the left, phone/email in the center, and a small “Get Directions” link or icon cluster on the right, each separated by 16–24px of horizontal spacing. Vertically, the panel occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, creating a clean, prominent block that sits just above the final CTA group. There is about 24–32px of vertical space beneath this panel before the cross-link CTAs, preserving the tiered, layered feel of the mockup’s hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and part of the normal flow. It remains centered and adjusts its height as contact content changes but maintains consistent padding and border treatment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cross-link CTA group is the final visual anchor at the bottom of the About page, occupying a position analogous to the main booking widget at the lower part of the hero. It sits directly beneath the contact info panel, with a clear vertical separation of about 24–32px, and is centered within the page. The group is contained within a horizontally oriented, gold-outlined strip that spans roughly 70–80% of the content column (about 50–60% of the viewport width) and feels like a refined action bar. Inside this strip, 2–4 primary link CTAs (e.g., “View Rooms,” “Restaurant,” “Meetings & Events”) are laid out horizontally with equal spacing, each appearing as a pill-shaped button or text-with-underline treatment. The strip has 16–20px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding, and each CTA has around 12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding, mirroring the button padding in the booking widget. The whole component floats slightly above the bottom edge of the viewport on initial load (e.g., with 48–64px margin from the page bottom) so it feels comfortably framed by background space rather than pressed against the edge. This group acts as the culmination of the page’s narrative, visually inviting users to continue into deeper sections of the site with a level of emphasis similar to the homepage booking call-to-action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow but designed to be visually prominent at the end of the scroll. On smaller screens, the CTAs stack vertically within the same outlined strip or become a 2x2 grid while preserving central alignment and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component has no explicit visual representation. Conceptually, it is positioned at the same level as the section switcher and dynamic sections, sitting ‘behind’ them to keep the current section state reflected in the URL. Its effects are visible only indirectly: for example, the correct tab in the section switcher appears active, and the corresponding section is scrolled into view when a URL parameter changes. It does not occupy any height or width in the layout and does not interfere with spacing or alignment of the visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial. It operates as an underlying behavior layer that updates URL parameters and scroll position but does not render any UI or affect the physical layout.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the dynamically loaded privacy policy, organized into sections with in-page navigation, deep-linkable sections, and URL-driven state.

core_features: Fetch and display the full privacy policy content (last updated date and all sections) using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Render a dynamic list of policy sections where titles and HTML bodies come entirely from the API response.
Provide an in-page table of contents that lists all sections and allows users to jump directly to a selected section.
Support deep linking to a specific section via URL parameters so that legal documents and confirmation emails can reference a particular part of the policy.
Indicate the policy’s last updated date using the lastUpdated field from the API response.
Allow users to expand/collapse individual sections to focus on specific topics while still keeping all content available on the page.
Allow users to copy a direct URL to the currently viewed section so they can share it or save it for reference.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View the entire privacy policy
1. User opens privacy.html (e.g., via the footer link) with no additional URL parameters.
2. On page load, the page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. When the response arrives, the page displays the lastUpdated value and all sections in the order returned.
4. The table of contents is generated from the returned section titles.
5. User scrolls through the content or uses the table of contents to navigate between sections.

Workflow: Follow a deep link to a specific section
1. User opens privacy.html?section={sectionId} where sectionId matches one of the sections derived from the API response (for example, an ID computed from its title).
2. On page load, the page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. After data is loaded, the page locates the target section whose identifier matches the section URL parameter.
4. The target section is automatically expanded (if collapsible), and the page scrolls to it.
5. The table of contents highlights the active section so the user understands where they are in the document.

Workflow: Navigate between sections using the table of contents
1. User views privacy.html and the policy content is loaded via getPrivacyPolicyContent.
2. User clicks a section link in the table of contents (e.g., a section related to cookies, data retention, or user rights, as defined in the API content).
3. The click updates the section URL parameter to the identifier of the selected section without reloading the page.
4. The page scrolls to the corresponding section and expands it if needed.
5. The selected section is visually marked as active in the table of contents, and the URL can now be shared as a deep link.

Workflow: Copy a direct link to a specific section
1. User scrolls to or navigates to a specific section on privacy.html.
2. User activates a "copy link" action associated with that section.
3. The page constructs a URL including the current section identifier in the section URL parameter.
4. The constructed URL is copied to the clipboard.
5. The user can paste and share this URL; anyone opening it will land on the same section using the deep-link workflow.
interactions: On initial page load, the page triggers getPrivacyPolicyContent to fetch lastUpdated and sections, then binds the response to the policy content components.
User clicks a section entry in the table of contents to navigate directly to that section; the page updates the section URL parameter and scrolls to the corresponding section.
User clicks a section header in the content list to toggle its expanded/collapsed state without affecting other sections.
User activates a "copy link" action within a section; the page generates a URL with the appropriate section identifier in the section parameter and copies it to the clipboard.
When the URL section parameter changes (for example, due to navigation within the page or the user pasting a new URL), the page updates the active section, scrolls to it, and ensures it is expanded.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is controlled using URL query parameters. The primary parameter is `section`, which represents the identifier of the section that should be focused when the page loads or when navigation occurs. Section identifiers are derived from the sections array returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent (for example, by generating a stable ID from each section's title or by using an ID field if provided in the response). When privacy.html is loaded without a `section` parameter, all sections are rendered in their default state, and no specific section is highlighted. If `section` is present, after getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, the page locates the matching section, ensures it is expanded, scrolls it into view, and marks it as active in the table of contents. When a user selects a section from the table of contents or toggles a section to "focus" it, the page updates the `section` parameter in the URL (using history manipulation without a full reload) to match that section's identifier so that the current state is shareable as a deep link.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getPrivacyPolicyContent once, stores the returned object in page state (e.g., privacyPolicyData), and signals child components when data is ready or if an error occurs.
Displays metadata about the privacy policy, primarily the lastUpdated value, so users can see when the policy was last changed.

Generates a navigable list of policy sections using the titles from the API. Each entry links to the corresponding section and updates the URL `section` parameter so users can jump directly between sections.

Iterates over all sections returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent and renders each one using the policy-section-item component, including the title and HTML body. It manages default expanded/collapsed states and responds to URL-driven active section changes.

Represents a single policy section, rendering the section.title and section.bodyHtml from the API. Supports expanding/collapsing, responds to an "active" state when its identifier matches the URL `section` parameter, and exposes a "copy link" control that generates a deep link to this section.

Synchronizes scroll position and active section state with the URL `section` parameter. On data load or when the URL changes, it finds the matching section identifier, requests that the corresponding policy-section-item become active and expanded, and scrolls it into view. When a user navigates via the table of contents or focuses a section, it updates the `section` URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The privacy policy page adopts the same single, centered content column used in the hero of the mockup. Below the existing global header, all policy-related components are stacked in a clear, top-to-bottom sequence within this central column: first the dynamically loaded content wrapper (invisible, but governing everything), then a compact page-intro block (page title/label – assumed), followed immediately by the policy-metadata-bar as a slim status strip, then the policy-toc as a navigational band, and finally the vertically stacked policy-section-list composed of multiple policy-section-item accordions. All elements align to a consistent center column roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with no sidebars, echoing the linear hero composition of the home view.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The privacy policy page adopts the same single, centered content column used in the hero of the mockup. Below the existing global header, all policy-related components are stacked in a clear, top-to-bottom sequence within this central column: first the dynamically loaded content wrapper (invisible, but governing everything), then a compact page-intro block (page title/label – assumed), followed immediately by the policy-metadata-bar as a slim status strip, then the policy-toc as a navigational band, and finally the vertically stacked policy-section-list composed of multiple policy-section-item accordions. All elements align to a consistent center column roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with no sidebars, echoing the linear hero composition of the home view.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the upper 25–35% of the page is devoted to a legal ‘hero’ zone: page title/label (assumed) plus metadata bar and TOC, with generous breathing room above and between them to match the mockup’s central logo/tagline cluster. The remaining 65–75% of vertical space is occupied by the policy-section-list and its items, which extend below the fold as needed. Horizontally, the content column is centered and constrained to about 60–70% of the viewport, with the policy-metadata-bar and policy-toc spanning the full width of this column, mirroring the full-width availability bar and booking widget inside the hero. Each interactive block uses 16–24px internal padding and 24–40px vertical spacing between blocks, following the established spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious density. Each major block (metadata bar, TOC, section list) is visually distinct with clear separation: 24–32px gaps between major components, 16–24px padding inside cards and bars, and slightly tighter spacing between individual accordion items within the section list. This echoes the mockup’s rich yet controlled density: the information-heavy legal content is broken into legible segments, avoiding clutter through consistent padding, generous line height, and clear typographic hierarchy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow still encourages a Z-pattern, adapted to textual content. After the user’s eye hits the global header logo top-left and nav top-right (from the mockup), it drops into the centered page title/label and then the policy-metadata-bar directly beneath it, functioning like the availability status strip. The eye then moves down to the policy-toc, which acts as the primary navigational anchor similar to the booking widget’s controls. From the TOC, the user is guided vertically down into the policy-section-list, scanning section headers left-to-right within each accordion and then reading downward into expanded content. This preserves the home hero’s top-center-to-mid-center-to-lower-center flow while replacing booking-focused content with legal-focused navigation and text.

layout.overall_description: The privacy policy page appears as an elegant legal ‘chapter’ within the same warm, boutique-hotel aesthetic. The familiar dark green header with the gold-accented brand and navigation remains fixed at the top, overlaying a subtly textured background rather than a dominant bed image. Below it, a centered content column—about two-thirds of the viewport width—anchors all policy components. At the top of this column, the page title and short legal intro (outside of the listed components) sit in a calm, typographic block, echoing the central hero tagline of the mockup. Immediately beneath, a slim gold-tinted policy-metadata-bar stretches across the column, resembling the booking availability strip and succinctly conveying last updated date and key metadata. A short gap below, the policy-toc forms a refined navigational band—either a soft card or pill-like row of section links—still centered and spanning most of the column width, visually substituting for the booking widget row of controls. Below the fold, the policy-section-list occupies the majority of the page, stacking multiple accordion-style policy-section-items with subtle gold borders and generous padding, preserving the rich yet uncluttered feel. As the user scrolls, the table of contents can gently pin beneath the header (on taller viewports), maintaining easy in-page navigation while the policy-scroll-and-url-manager silently synchronizes scroll positions and URL hashes. The overall effect is a text-forward but still luxurious layout that translates the hero’s structured, center-focused composition into a clear, navigable legal document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-content-loader sits conceptually at the top of the centered content column, just below the global site header and any page-level title/intro text. It does not present any visible UI itself; instead, it occupies a zero-height position in the normal document flow and governs the loading state for the components that follow (metadata bar, TOC, and section list). During loading, it may reserve the approximate vertical footprint of the policy-metadata-bar, policy-toc, and the first viewport’s worth of section items by triggering skeleton placeholders that follow the same centered, 60–70%-width column alignment. These placeholders align perfectly with the column edges used by all visible components so that once content is loaded, there is no visual shift—maintaining the stable, refined presentation seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller in standard document flow; it influences the presence and height of loading placeholders within the same centered column but does not render its own visible container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-metadata-bar appears as a slim, horizontally oriented strip positioned at the top of the policy content stack, directly beneath the page title/label and any brief introductory copy. It spans the full width of the centered content column (about 60–70% of the viewport, max-width constrained), with equal left and right margins framing it against the page edges. Visually, it echoes the mockup’s gold availability status bar: a low-height (roughly 48–64px tall) bar with a warm, slightly gold-tinted background, rounded corners, and 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. The primary metadata (e.g., “Last updated”) is left-aligned inside the bar, while secondary items (e.g., version, jurisdiction) align to the right, suggesting a left-to-right scan similar to the availability text in the hero. There is a comfortable 24–32px margin above the bar separating it from the title, and a 24–32px gap below before the policy-toc, giving it its own visual tier within the vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the normal flow; it does not stick or float, but its consistent width and horizontal alignment establish a clear baseline for the components stacked below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-toc is positioned immediately below the policy-metadata-bar, acting as the primary navigational band for the page. It shares the same centered alignment and width as the metadata bar, occupying the full width of the content column (roughly 60–70% of the viewport) with generous side margins. Visually, it reads like a refined variant of the booking widget row: a horizontally organized set of section links (either as pill-shaped buttons or neatly aligned text tabs) presented within a soft card or light panel with a subtle border or gold accent line. The container has 16–24px padding on all sides, and the individual TOC items are spaced 16–24px apart horizontally, keeping the density comfortable. On larger screens, the TOC height is approximately 64–96px, depending on number of lines, and there is a 24–32px gap above and below it, separating it clearly from both the metadata bar above and the policy-section-list below. Once the user scrolls past the initial intro area, the TOC gently pins below the global header, maintaining its full column width but acting like a sticky sub-header, so section navigation remains readily accessible while content scrolls beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially a standard block in the flow; after scrolling beyond its original position, it becomes sticky, fixed to the top of the viewport just under the global header, maintaining its centered column width and casting a subtle shadow to distinguish it from the content scrolling behind.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-section-list is placed directly below the policy-toc, filling the remainder of the central content column and extending vertically as the main body of the page. It is a full-width stack within the column, meaning each child policy-section-item aligns flush with the column’s left and right edges, preserving the strong vertical alignment that mirrors the hero’s central booking widget. A 32–40px gap separates the TOC from the first section, establishing a clear transition from navigation to content. Within the list, each section item is separated by 16–24px of vertical space, enough to perceive each as a distinct card-like block without feeling overly fragmented. The entire list respects generous vertical padding at the top and bottom of the page content area, ensuring that the last section doesn’t feel cramped against the footer. This stacked arrangement keeps the reading experience linear and calm, akin to scrolling down from the central logo to the booking area in the mockup, but extended into multiple text sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertical stacking container in normal flow; it does not scroll independently or fix in place but provides a stable backbone for the accordion sections, allowing the sticky TOC and global header to frame the scrolling content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each policy-section-item functions as an accordion-style card within the policy-section-list. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the content column, aligning perfectly with the metadata bar and TOC edges to maintain a strong, unified column shape. Vertically, each item is a self-contained block with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. The collapsed state is compact but not cramped: the section title sits in a horizontal header row at the top of the card, left-aligned in a slightly larger, serif or semi-serif type, while an expand/collapse icon (chevron or plus) is right-aligned, creating a subtle left-to-right focal line similar to the booking widget’s input-to-button flow. The header row itself is about 48–56px tall. A thin gold border or accent line outlines the card or separates the header from the body, echoing the gold outlines around the booking widget in the hero. When expanded, the body content flows downward beneath the header within the same card, preserving the card’s width and alignment; text has generous line height and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs, ensuring readability of dense legal content. There is a consistent 16–24px vertical gap between consecutive section items, so even with multiple expanded, the layout retains a balanced, rhythmical stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Interactive accordion within normal flow; expanding or collapsing changes the item’s height but does not affect width or alignment. As sections expand, the page scroll bar adjusts naturally; the item does not overlay other content but pushes sections below it downward, preserving the linear reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-scroll-and-url-manager is an invisible behavioral layer that spans the entire policy content area. Conceptually, it wraps or observes the policy-toc and policy-section-list, anchoring its logic to the same centered content column but without rendering its own visible container. It tracks the scroll position of each policy-section-item relative to the viewport and updates the URL hash and TOC active state accordingly. This preserves a sense of orientation similar to how the booking widget clearly reflects the user’s chosen dates and guests: here, the active section is subtly highlighted in the TOC as the user scrolls down the long legal text. The manager also interprets URL hashes on initial load or when users click TOC items, smoothly scrolling the document so that the corresponding policy-section-item aligns just below the sticky TOC, keeping the visual relationship between navigation and content tight and predictable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, full-page controller attached to scroll events; it does not occupy layout space but affects how the policy-toc behaves (sticky state and active item highlighting) and how the viewport scrolls to align sections beneath the TOC and header.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Page for viewing the website’s terms and conditions, with optional focus on room bookings, restaurant reservations, and gift card purchases based on URL context.

core_features: Load and display terms and conditions content using the getTermsAndConditionsContent interface, optionally scoped by the URL `context` parameter.
Show a clear indication of which context (e.g., general, rooms, restaurant, gift cards) the user is currently viewing, based on the `context` URL parameter.
Allow users to switch between different term contexts (or view all terms) by updating the `context` URL parameter and reloading terms via getTermsAndConditionsContent.
Generate a dynamic table of contents from the returned sections to allow quick navigation within the terms document.
Display each terms section using the API-provided `title` and `bodyHtml`, ensuring the full legal text is rendered without duplication or hardcoding.
Show the `lastUpdated` timestamp from getTermsAndConditionsContent so users know when the terms were last changed.
Handle loading and error states for the terms content, giving users feedback if content is being retrieved or if retrieval fails.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Direct access from footer without context:
1. User clicks the “Terms & Conditions” link in the site footer.
2. Browser opens `terms.html` with no `context` parameter in the URL.
3. On page load, the controller component reads the URL and detects that `context` is not present.
4. The controller calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with `context = undefined` (no argument or empty value).
5. The API returns `lastUpdated` and a list of generic terms `sections`.
6. The page displays the generic terms, a default context indication (e.g., “All terms”), the last updated date, and a generated table of contents based on the returned section titles.
7. The user can click items in the table of contents to scroll to specific sections within the document.

Workflow 2 – Access from rooms booking flow with rooms context:
1. User is on `rooms.html` reviewing room options or a booking summary.
2. User clicks a link such as “Booking terms & conditions” associated with room bookings.
3. The rooms page navigates to `terms.html?context=rooms`.
4. On page load, the controller reads `context=rooms` from the URL.
5. The controller calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with `context = 'rooms'`.
6. The API returns `lastUpdated` and a list of sections filtered or ordered for room-related terms.
7. The page displays a context indicator showing that room-related terms are being shown, along with the last updated date and section list.
8. The user scans or navigates via the table of contents; if needed, they can switch to another context (e.g., restaurant, gift cards, or all terms) using the context switcher, which updates the `context` URL parameter and triggers a new API call.

Workflow 3 – Access from restaurant page with restaurant context:
1. User is viewing the restaurant information or making a restaurant reservation on `restaurant.html`.
2. User clicks a link such as “Restaurant reservation terms” that leads to `terms.html?context=restaurant`.
3. The controller detects `context=restaurant` from the URL.
4. The controller invokes `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with `context = 'restaurant'`.
5. The API returns sections tailored to restaurant reservations.
6. The page shows the restaurant context indicator, the last updated date, and the sections list.
7. The user reviews relevant sections and can switch contexts (for example, to general terms) via the context switcher, which updates the URL `context` value and re-fetches content.

Workflow 4 – Access from gift card purchase/checkout with gift card context:
1. User is on `gift_cards.html` or `checkout.html` reviewing gift card purchase details.
2. User clicks a link such as “Gift card terms & conditions”, navigating to `terms.html?context=gift_cards`.
3. The controller reads `context=gift_cards` from the URL.
4. The controller calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with `context = 'gift_cards'`.
5. The API returns sections focused on gift card terms (validity, usage rules, etc.) according to the implementation.
6. The page shows the gift card context indicator and renders the returned sections and last updated date.
7. The user can follow the table of contents to jump to key sections and, if needed, clear the context (for example to see all terms) via a control that removes the `context` parameter and re-calls the API.

Workflow 5 – Switching context from within the terms page:
1. User is on `terms.html` with a current context (or no context) already applied and the corresponding terms displayed.
2. User interacts with the context switcher (e.g., selects another context option).
3. The switcher updates the browser URL query string `context` to the newly selected value using navigation (e.g., pushing a new URL such as `terms.html?context=restaurant`).
4. The controller reacts to the URL change and calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with the new `context` value.
5. The API returns the corresponding `lastUpdated` and `sections` for that context.
6. The page updates the context indicator, table of contents, and sections list to reflect the new context, resetting the scroll position to the top of the terms content.

Workflow 6 – Deep navigation within the terms document via table of contents:
1. User views loaded terms content (in any context) and sees a table of contents listing each section title.
2. User clicks a specific section entry in the table of contents.
3. The TOC component identifies the corresponding section element in the document.
4. The page scrolls to the associated section, optionally updating the URL fragment (hash) to reflect the section anchor without altering the `context` query parameter.
5. User can scroll further or click other TOC entries to move between sections while remaining in the same `context` state.
interactions: Page load: When `terms.html` loads, the controller reads the `context` parameter from the URL query string. It then triggers `getTermsAndConditionsContent(context)` and manages loading and error states until a response is received.

Context selection: When the user selects a specific context (for example, via a context button or dropdown), the context switcher updates the `context` query parameter in the URL and notifies the controller that the context has changed. The controller then calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with the new context and refreshes the displayed terms.

Clear context: When the user chooses to view generic or all terms (for example, by selecting an option like “All” in the context switcher), the component removes the `context` query parameter (or sets it to an empty value) in the URL. This triggers a fresh call to `getTermsAndConditionsContent` without a context so general terms can be displayed.

Table of contents click: When the user clicks a section entry in the table of contents, the TOC component locates the corresponding section element (by a generated anchor/ID derived from the API-provided `title`) and scrolls it into view. Optionally, the URL fragment (hash) may be updated, but the `context` query parameter remains unchanged.

Scroll within content: As the user scrolls through the terms sections, the TOC may highlight the currently visible section based on scroll position. This is a purely client-side interaction and does not change the URL `context` parameter or cause additional API calls.

Retry on error: If the call to `getTermsAndConditionsContent` fails, the error-state component can show a retry control. When the user clicks retry, the controller re-invokes `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with the current `context` value from the URL.

Back/forward navigation: When the user uses the browser’s back or forward buttons after context changes (which updated the URL), the controller re-reads the `context` parameter from the updated URL and calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` again to ensure the displayed terms match the historical URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the `context` URL query parameter (`terms.html?context=...`).

- On initial load, the controller parses the current page URL for the `context` query parameter. If `context` is present, its string value is passed directly to `getTermsAndConditionsContent(context)`. If it is absent or empty, the controller calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` without a context (or with a neutral value) to retrieve general terms.
- The result of `getTermsAndConditionsContent` (an object containing `lastUpdated` and an array of `sections`) defines the render state. No terms content is hardcoded; all section titles and bodies are taken from the API response.
- The currently active context is inferred solely from the `context` query parameter. UI elements such as the context switcher and context indicator read this value to determine which option is active.
- When the user changes context via the UI, the page updates the URL using navigation with a new `context` value (for example, changing the URL from `terms.html?context=rooms` to `terms.html?context=restaurant`). The controller listens for URL changes (or triggers its own handler after updates) and re-calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` with the new context value.
- When the user chooses to clear the context (e.g., view all terms), the page updates the URL to `terms.html` with no `context` parameter (or removes it from the query string). The controller then treats this as the general-terms state and calls `getTermsAndConditionsContent` without a context argument.
- Optional in-page navigation through section anchors may use URL fragments (e.g., `#section-2`), but these do not affect the query-string `context` parameter. Thus, the API call is only tied to the `context` query parameter, not to fragments.
- The page does not use localStorage or any other client-side persistent storage; navigation and state transitions are governed exclusively by the current URL and the response of `getTermsAndConditionsContent`.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered content container. Hero elements (logo, tagline, booking widget) are visually centered, while the header uses a full-width bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom hero structure as the mockup: a full-width dark green header bar overlaid on a full-bleed hero photo at the top, followed by a centrally aligned title cluster in the visual center of the hero. Below the hero, the Terms & Conditions content is arranged in a single main column, flowing vertically from an introductory summary into clearly separated sections (e.g., bookings, restaurant, gift cards). The navigation layout mirrors the mockup: logo card on the left, language in the center, and right-aligned navigation with a BOOK CTA. The hero replaces the large booking widget with a more understated legal page intro (title, optional context label) while preserving the vertical rhythm and centered composition.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom hero structure as the mockup: a full-width dark green header bar overlaid on a full-bleed hero photo at the top, followed by a centrally aligned title cluster in the visual center of the hero. Below the hero, the Terms & Conditions content is arranged in a single main column, flowing vertically from an introductory summary into clearly separated sections (e.g., bookings, restaurant, gift cards). The navigation layout mirrors the mockup: logo card on the left, language in the center, and right-aligned navigation with a BOOK CTA. The hero replaces the large booking widget with a more understated legal page intro (title, optional context label) while preserving the vertical rhythm and centered composition.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header still occupies roughly 12–15% of the viewport height, sitting over a full-bleed background image. The hero title block (page title, optional subtitle) is centered vertically in the upper-middle of the screen, using around 20–25% of the viewport height. The remaining viewport height in the hero is left as breathing space around the title cluster, letting the warm interior imagery frame the content. Below the hero, the Terms & Conditions body content fills the rest of the page, constrained to a 60–70% width center column aligned with the hero’s content column. Generous vertical spacing (24–40px) separates major text sections, with slightly tighter spacing (16–24px) between headings and their paragraphs to maintain readability without breaking the refined, spacious rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area remains relatively airy, with minimal functional elements: navigation at the top and a centered title block, ensuring the page feels calm and premium despite the text-heavy nature of legal content. In the body, content density increases, but the typography and spacing keep it from feeling heavy: paragraphs use comfortable line height, section headings have clear breathing room, and there is consistent spacing above each new section group. Overall, the density is moderate—richer than a minimal, ultra-spacious layout, but still controlled and elegant, in line with a boutique hotel’s brand.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the Z-pattern from the mockup. The eye first lands on the top-left logo badge in the header, then scans horizontally to the right-aligned navigation and BOOK CTA. From there, it drops diagonally to the centered hero title cluster (e.g., a gold-accented 'Terms & Conditions' heading, possibly flanked by subtle divider lines). After anchoring on the title, the eye naturally moves downward into the main text column, starting at the left edge of the content column. Within the body, section headings act as visual anchors, allowing the reader to scan vertically while the consistent center alignment of the overall column echoes the hero’s structure and keeps the experience cohesive.

layout.overall_description: The Terms & Conditions page opens with the same luxurious hero framework as the homepage: a full-width, edge-to-edge photograph of a warm, softly lit interior, overlaid by a dark green header bar fixed at the top. On the left of this bar, a small badge-like logo card slightly overlaps the hero image, reinforcing the brand’s boutique identity; in the center, a compact language selector sits with ample breathing room; on the right, a horizontal navigation menu culminates in a BOOK link styled as a subtle, high-priority CTA. Below this header, in the upper-middle of the viewport, the page title 'Terms & Conditions' is centered within the same 60–70% wide content column used on the homepage. The title is set in a refined serif, flanked on both sides by fine gold divider lines that stretch outward but stop well short of the viewport edges, echoing the tagline treatment in the mockup. A short, one- or two-line descriptive subheading sits directly beneath the title, also centered, providing context about what these terms cover (rooms, restaurant, gift cards), with 16–24px vertical spacing separating each typographic tier. The hero’s vertical space is balanced so that the header plus title block together consume around the top 35–40% of the screen, leaving the remaining hero area as softly blurred image space to keep the focus on the text. As the user scrolls, the background image gives way to a clean, off-white or very light warm background where the Terms & Conditions content lives in a single, centered column aligned perfectly with the hero title above. This column spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, creating generous margins on either side that feel like the white space of a premium printed contract. Section headings for different legal areas (e.g., 'Room Bookings', 'Restaurant Reservations', 'Gift Cards') are left-aligned within this column and spaced 32–40px above the preceding content, acting as visual anchors. Each heading is followed by tightly grouped paragraphs of body text with comfortable line height and 16–24px spacing between paragraph blocks. Optional elements like an in-page contents list or category labels nest at the top of the text area but maintain the same centered column, avoiding clutter. Throughout, the color accents—subtle gold lines separating major legal sections, dark green subheadings, and occasional small caps labels—mirror the original hero’s gold status bar and green booking frame without reintroducing the heavy booking UI. The overall effect is of a beautiful, legal 'document' laid over the warm, atmospheric hero frame of the brand: concentrated reading content anchored in a stable central column, framed by generous margins and a luxurious header, with the user’s eye guided smoothly from brand/navigation, to page title, to the structured, well-spaced legal text below.
